After this bead: a player can complete a 3-day investigation by working it once per calendar day across 3 days; same-day re-runs are either blocked in the UI or clearly communicated without burning a slot; journal shows 'Surveillance day N of 3 logged' exactly once per day; the final day settles the quest and pays the reward before/without the expiry race.

## Why now
A purchasable, discoverable contract is a false promise visible to the player; it also blocks confidence in all multi-day quest content.

## Player impact
Multi-day surveillance contracts become completable and legible: visible daily progress, no duplicate journal noise, no silently wasted time slots.

## System impact
- src/application/store/slices/questReducers.ts (startInvestigation, chooseInvestigationApproach, resolveInvestigation, updateQuestRuntime)
- possibly a dedicated progress field (e.g. lastSurveillanceLoggedDay) on quest runtime progress contract (src/domain/quests/contracts.ts) + save migration default
- src/ui/screens/InvestigationScreen.tsx (handleRun ordering / same-day messaging)
- src/application/store/slices/timeReducers.ts ordering vs expiry (or quest data: give timeLimitDays slack)
- investigation.test.ts: add a full-flow multi-day test driving the real reducer sequence

## Does not include
- No new quest content, no balance pass on rewards/difficulty
- No rework of delivery/survival watch logic (resolveWithComplicationCheck) beyond verifying it does not share the same stamp poisoning
- No redesign of the approach/roll system

## Evidence
Manual playthrough report 'Three Days on Assessor Vorn' (2026-06-12); code reading of questReducers.ts:154-330, timeReducers.ts:5-14, questUtils.ts:22-29, InvestigationScreen.tsx:161-164; quests.json quest-compact-watch (timeLimitDays=3, executionDurationDays=3).

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: starting from acceptance, working the investigation once per day for 3 days completes the quest and pays 180 Marks; a same-day second attempt is blocked or explained and does not consume a slot silently; journal contains exactly one 'Surveillance day N of 3' entry per day; no duplicated start/clue entries across days.
2. System/technical: the day-dedup key for surveillance logging is independent of generic lastAdvancedDay stamping ('cannot log twice per day' is enforced in resolveInvestigation only, with startInvestigation/updateQuestRuntime no longer able to poison it); quest expiry cannot fire between the time-slot advance and the same action's investigation resolution on the final allowed day.
3. Tests: a reducer-level test drives the FULL daily loop (startInvestigation → chooseInvestigationApproach → resolveInvestigation, repeated across 3 days with real advanceTimeSlot dispatches) and asserts completion + reward; a test asserts same-day re-run does not increment progress; existing investigation tests still pass.

## Finding coverage
F1 (root cause), F2 (journal duplication), F5 (wasted slot/no feedback), F6 (expiry race), F4 (test gap). Does NOT cover F3 (midQuestBeats never fire) — separate bead.
notes:
## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start a new game, travel to The Pale, discover the lead at the bar and accept 'Three Days on Assessor Vorn' (quest-compact-watch).
2. Day 1: Contract board → Start Investigation → choose any approach → assign an operative → Run. Journal logs 'Surveillance day 1 of 3'.
3. Sleep to next morning (day 2). Contract board → Start Investigation again (required, activeInvestigation was cleared) → choose approach → Run.
4. Observe: time slot consumed, no 'day 2 of 3' journal entry, objective stays 'Continue surveillance tomorrow. 1 of 3 days logged.'
5. Repeat on day 3 — same result. On day 4 the quest expires and fails.

UX addendum (user-confirmed): on intermediate surveillance days resolveInvestigation nulls BOTH activeInvestigation and lastInvestigationResult, so InvestigationScreen falls through to the generic EmptyState 'No investigation is currently active.' (InvestigationScreen.tsx:31-42) — the player just spent a slot and operatives and lands on a page implying nothing exists. Acceptance should include an interstitial 'Day N of M logged — continue tomorrow' result view instead of the empty state.

title: The Road to the Pale Cage: build the Mira story as a quest chain with aftermath
description:
## Why
Mira is the emotional spine of the premise and 'The Pale Cage' (quest-mira-rescue) carries the best authored prose in the catalog — but it is a single quest with prerequisiteQuestId null (reachable without earning it) and no successor (the Court forgets a loud raid; Mira returns and nothing re-stages). The hero's journey the opening promises has no quest spine.

## What
A three-act chain plus aftermath, built entirely on shipped fields: prerequisiteQuestId (pattern proven by quest-harborwatch-followup), unlocksNpcId (proven by quest-mira-rescue itself), sourceNpcId discovery (npc-tessaly-ash exists as story NPC with dialogue-tessaly-ash), successorQuestId + successorRumorIds, rewardRelationshipDeltas.

## Why now
Highest-leverage narrative work; zero schema changes; pure content + one gating field on an existing quest.

## Player impact
The player pursues what the opening said matters most, earns it across acts, and lives with what the rescue costs.

## System impact
data/definitions/quests.json (3 new templates + 2 field edits), data/definitions/rumors.json (2 entries). Nothing else.

## Non-goals
No Mira companion/household mechanics (file separately when reached), no Court war arc beyond one retaliation quest, no new NPCs, no dialogue tree changes.

## Fiction contract
Finding Mira is bought with trust, not marks; the rescue is loud; the Court does not forget loud. Mira returns changed.

## Implementation contract (lower-tier executable — follow docs/workflows/lower-model-playbook.md)
Voice samples to imitate (rhythm and restraint — copy the register, never the sentences):
- Tessaly: 'She is alive. The Gilded Court has her in the old tannery on the Pale's eastern edge. They are not using her yet — but they will.'
- Aftermath: 'Mira walks out on her own feet, which tells you something.'
If any prose line feels uncertain, still write it and list the quest id under a 'voice review' note appended to this bead — do not leave fields empty.

### Step 1 — Act 1 'A Name in the Wren's Ledger' (NEW template quest-mira-act1-wren-favor)
Copy the FULL JSON shape of quest-ledger-recovery, then set: id/title as above; questType 'story'; objectiveType 'investigation'; districtId 'district-the-pale'; discoverySource 'npc'; discoveryDistrictId 'district-the-pale'; sourceNpcId 'npc-tessaly-ash'; employerFactionId null; enemyFactionId 'faction-gilded-court'; riskLevel 'medium'; rewardMarks 0 (trust is the payment — say so in the briefing); rewardRelationshipDeltas [{npcId:'npc-tessaly-ash', trust:10, respect:5}]; timeLimitDays null; prerequisiteQuestId null; successorQuestId 'quest-mira-act2-tannery-watch'; midQuestBeats: 2 (complication: the favor touches Ring business the Compact is watching; reveal: Tessaly's price is silence, not coin); briefing/openingText: Tessaly asks the house to recover something of hers from a Court courier — proof she can trust you with what she knows. PROSE: 3-5 sentences total in the sample register.

### Step 2 — Act 2 'The Tannery Watch' (NEW template quest-mira-act2-tannery-watch)
Same copy-shape procedure. Set: objectiveType 'investigation'; prerequisiteQuestId 'quest-mira-act1-wren-favor'; sourceNpcId 'npc-tessaly-ash'; districtId 'district-the-pale'; enemyFactionId 'faction-gilded-court'; riskLevel 'high'; successorQuestId null (Act 3 is gated by prerequisite, not successor — IMPORTANT: verify in src/application/commands/questLifecycle.ts how prerequisiteQuestId gates discovery; mirror quest-harborwatch-followup's wiring exactly); rewardRelationshipDeltas trust+10 tessaly; midQuestBeats 2 (complication: the watch rotates early — someone tightened security; reveal: Mira is moved between two rooms on a schedule, and the schedule is signed). Briefing: chart the old tannery's guard pattern without being seen.

### Step 3 — gate Act 3
Edit quest-mira-rescue ONLY these fields: prerequisiteQuestId: 'quest-mira-act2-tannery-watch'; successorQuestId: 'quest-mira-act4-court-answer'; successorRumorIds: ['rumor-pale-cage-broken']. Touch nothing else in that template.

### Step 4 — Aftermath 'The Court's Answer' (NEW template quest-mira-act4-court-answer)
Copy shape again. objectiveType 'survival'; prerequisiteQuestId 'quest-mira-rescue'; employerFactionId null; enemyFactionId 'faction-gilded-court'; enemyNpcId: pick an EXISTING id from data/definitions/enemy-npcs.json with factionAffinityId faction-gilded-court (verify by grep; do not invent); districtId 'district-the-pale'; riskLevel 'extreme'; rewardCityDialId 'control', rewardCityDialDelta -5; midQuestBeats 2 (complication: collectors arrive with paper, not blades; setback: they name Mira in the writ). Briefing: the Court answers the raid with a seizure writ against the house; hold the door for three watches. aftermathText: the writ fails but is filed — the Court now needs the debt to succeed where the raid did not (1-2 sentences, sample register).

### Step 5 — rumors
data/definitions/rumors.json: add 'rumor-pale-cage-broken' and 'rumor-court-counts-valdris' copying an existing rumor object's exact field shape (grep rumor-valdris-cleared-harborwatch as template).

### Verification (run all)
- pnpm test:run (startup catalog integrity validates every new id; failures name the bad reference)
- pnpm exec vitest run src/application/commands/questLifecycle.test.ts — ADD one test: quest-mira-rescue lead does NOT surface before act2 completion and DOES after (mirror the existing prerequisite test for quest-harborwatch-followup if present; else copy its pattern)
- pnpm test:playthrough:funnel and golden
- Floor test from destiny-tedu (if landed) must stay green for the new templates.
- Append per-act design-review answers (hook/stakes/why now/friction/who remembers) to this bead.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Act 1 discoverable early via Tessaly; rescue unreachable until Act 2 completes (test-proven).
- Each act has 2 beats + authored briefing; rescue success spawns the Court's Answer + rumor.
- All ids resolve (catalog check); funnel/golden green; voice-review note appended if needed.

## Finding coverage
F1 no road into the rescue; F2 no aftermath chain; F3 trust economy bypassed.
close_reason: Implemented Mira quest chain: added Act 1 (Wren's Favor), Act 2 (Tannery Watch), modified quest-mira-rescue with prerequisite, added Act 4 (Court's Answer) aftermath, and two new rumors

title: First-hour route break: dashboard directive points at empty Work Board
description:
## Why
Verified fresh-start walkthrough 2026-06-10: Day 1 dashboard directive says 'The first house leads are posted on the board in the Pale' with a button 'Check the Work Board'. The Work Board is empty ('No fresh leads at the moment'). The lead actually surfaces only after Districts → The Pale → Tallow Ring Den → 'Review local work'. The very first promise the game makes is false, and the fix path is 3 undirected hops.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start a fresh game, finish onboarding.
2. On the Dashboard, read the directive and click 'Check the Work Board'.
3. Work Board shows 'No fresh leads at the moment' — dead end.
4. The lead 'A Word with Brennik' is only reachable via Districts → The Pale → Tallow Ring Den → Review local work.

## What
Make the directive route true. Either (a) the directive button deep-links to the Pale venue board where the lead lives, or (b) the house Work Board lists discovered-but-remote leads with 'posted at Tallow Ring Den — travel to review', or both. Empty Work Board state must say where work is found.

## Why now
This is the first minute of play; it fails the design-review fresh-eyes question 'Where are my quests?'.

## Player impact
The player's first instruction leads to work instead of an empty screen.

## System impact
Dashboard directive component, ContractBoardScreen empty state, possibly a selector exposing lead locations.

## Non-goals
No change to lead surfacing rules or quest generation; presentation/routing only.

## Acceptance Criteria
- From a fresh game, following the first directive reaches an acceptable lead in at most 2 clicks.
- The house Work Board never shows a bare 'no leads' while a lead exists somewhere; it names the venue and district.
- Route-level playthrough test covers directive → lead visibility.

## Finding coverage
F1 false directive; F2 empty-state dead end; F3 house board vs venue board split is invisible to the player.
close_reason: Dashboard directive now navigates to Pale district directly. ContractBoard shows first-time hint about Tallow Ring Den location. Test updated.

title: Remove developer/meta narration from player-facing copy
description:
## Why
Internal design rationale is rendered to the player. Verified on 2026-06-10: POI screen ('Local venues should feel like actual places. This step preserves their identity before you move into house management, market browsing, hiring, or contract review.'), Work Board context banner ('You are working from a specific place in the district rather than an abstract house menu.'), End Day banner ('It settles wages, condition shifts, city pressure, and pending world fallout in one step.'), Journal subtitle ('What has been logged.'), quest card 'Blocked' status badge and 'Consequence History: No lasting consequences logged yet.' These describe the system to the developer, not the world to the player.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Fresh game → travel to The Pale → enter Tallow Ring Den: meta paragraph under 'What this place offers'.
2. From the den choose 'Review local work': banner 'You are working from a specific place in the district rather than an abstract house menu.'
3. End a day once: guidance banner with system language.
4. Open Journal: 'What has been logged.' subtitle and 'system' badges.
5. Accept any lead: 'Blocked' badge and 'No lasting consequences logged yet.'

## What
Audit all static UI strings for meta/system language and rewrite in-world or delete. POI intro describes the place; banners describe what the player can do; status badges use fiction terms ('Waiting on travel', not 'Blocked').

## Why now
Cheapest, highest-leverage fix for the 'UI narrative' complaint — pure copy, no logic.

## Player impact
Surfaces speak the fiction; the fourth wall stops breaking on every screen.

## System impact
String-only changes in src/ui screens/components; screen tests asserting old copy must be updated.

## Non-goals
No layout changes, no new mechanics, no rewriting NPC dialogue content.

## Fiction contract
Every sentence the player reads is written from inside the world (ledger clerk's voice), never about the game's structure.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A grep-style audit list of offending strings is attached to this bead before the rewrite.
- The five quoted strings above no longer appear.
- No player surface explains UI structure ('menu', 'step', 'screen', 'logged') in diegetic copy.

## Finding coverage
F1 POI meta paragraph; F2 work-board meta banner; F3 end-day system banner; F4 journal log voice; F5 'Blocked'/'logged' status language.
notes: Consistency audit 2026-06-11: the map+panel consolidation (db08d43) already removed four of the five quoted strings (POI meta paragraph, 'abstract house menu' banner, journal 'logged' subtitle voice, 'Blocked' badge surface). REMAINING scope: 'No lasting consequences logged yet.' at src/ui/screens/ContractBoardScreen.tsx:216, the End Day guidance banner ('pending world fallout' — verify, it may live in the status bar component), plus the full grep audit for meta vocabulary (menu/step/screen/logged) across src/ui that the acceptance demands. Bead stays open at reduced scope.
close_reason: All meta/developer narration removed from player-facing copy. POI screen, VenueContextBanner, GlobalStatusBar, EventLogScreen, Journal summary, and 'Blocked' badge all rewritten in-world.

title: Housing: generic quarters and daily resident bonus
description:
## Why
The current salvaged room still carries Marion-specific structure in its room id lineage, and room assignment has no systemic payoff. Players expect a private quarter to be both generic house infrastructure and a real daily benefit for any resident.

## What
Rename the Marion-specific room into a generic quarters room, remove Marion-specific helper text, keep Marion initially assigned there, and grant the same daily quarters bonus to any roster NPC with a valid room assignment.

## Why now
Recent housing fixes made room occupancy legible, which makes the lack of systemic payoff and the leftover Marion-specific room identity more obvious.

## Player impact
Players can understand quarters as a reusable house resource rather than a Marion-only fiction stub, and assigned NPCs visibly benefit from being housed.

## System impact
Touches house room ids/content, initial game state, daily NPC state progression, and housing/UI tests.

## Does not include
- no room reservation system
- no Marion-specific special bonus
- no broad housing rebalance beyond the quarters bonus

## Evidence
- user feedback that the room should be generic and have no Marion hint text
- current room assignment gives little systemic value

## Acceptance Criteria
### Player-visible acceptance
The room appears as generic quarters with no Marion-specific helper text, Marion starts assigned there, and any assigned NPC receives the same visible daily benefit from having quarters.
### System / technical acceptance
The canonical room id is generic, all references are migrated, and the daily quarters bonus applies uniformly to roster NPCs with room assignments.
### Tests / verification acceptance
Coverage proves the room id migration, generic house UI text, initial Marion assignment, and the daily NPC quarters bonus.

## Fiction contract
The house maintains one salvaged sleeping quarters space. Marion currently uses it, but it is house infrastructure, not a character-bound relic.

title: Assignment 'working' claims 'cannot deploy' but squad selection and startCombat allow it; concludeCombat blindly resets to idle losing prior 'working' state
description:
## Why
The ASSIGNMENT_DISPLAY_LABELS for 'working' explicitly says 'Earns Marks, cannot deploy or train.'

But:
- In MissionPrepScreen (contract on-site), the availableRoster (from selectMissionPrepSummary) includes 'working' NPCs and allows adding them to squad (only guards title and low loyalty).
- In startCombatEncounter, the squad filter only excludes recovering and low health — explicitly allows 'working'.
- In setNpcAssignment reducer, the only early return is for current === 'deployed' or 'assigned_title'. From 'working' you can set to 'deployed'.
- In concludeCombatEncounter, surviving allies are always set to 'idle' (or 'recovering' if KO), regardless of what their assignment was before deployment. A NPC that was earning on a job loses that state after combat.

This is a direct violation of the documented assignment contract. 'working' is supposed to be a safe income assignment that locks the NPC out of field actions.

## What
- Centralize "deployable assignments" (probably only 'idle' for normal deployment; 'recovering'/'working' etc. explicitly excluded).
- Enforce in all entry points: MissionPrep availableRoster, ExpeditionPrep (already stricter), startCombatEncounter filter, addNpcToSelectedSquad if possible, and any other prep.
- In concludeCombat, either snapshot pre-deployment assignment for the squad or at minimum do not override a previous 'working'/'training' without reason.
- Update setNpcAssignment to have a more complete guard or rely on UI + selectors.
- Make the display text the source of truth or have a single isDeployable(npc) helper used everywhere.

## Why now
This is a P1 inconsistency in a core player loop (assign people to earn → form squad for jobs → combat). It breaks player expectation set by the UI text. It also interacts badly with the bond cluster (working while bonded has special rules in bondService) and the new correspondence work (if 'working' NPCs are accidentally deployed, their income and availability for other things is messed up).

The recent economic fixes (wages, bond) make this more visible.

## Player impact
Player puts an NPC on 'working' expecting safe income and no field risk. Later selects them for a contract squad anyway (or the system allows it). After the job/combat, the NPC is 'idle' instead of back earning. Income lost, expectation broken. "I can deploy" despite the label.

## System impact
- assignmentDisplay.ts (the contract text)
- missionPrep.ts selector (availableRoster filter)
- MissionPrepScreen.tsx (the list and add logic)
- combat.ts startCombatEncounter (squad filter) and concludeCombatEncounter (reset logic)
- rosterReducers.ts setNpcAssignment (more guards)
- ExpeditionPrepScreen.tsx (already filters idle, make consistent)
- Possibly InvestigationScreen and other places that currently allow 'working' for some selections.
- New central helper recommended (e.g. in domain or selectors) for deployable/trainable.

## Does not include
- Full rework of the assignment state machine (that's bigger).
- UI changes beyond the filters.
- Changes to 'defense' or other system assignments.

## Evidence
- User report: "assignment 'working' -> 'Earns Marks, cannot deploy or train.' aber ich kann deployen!"
- Direct grep shows the text only in assignmentDisplay, the guards are ad-hoc and incomplete (only title in some places, health/recovering in start, no 'working' exclusion in prep or start for normal squad).
- Inconsistent between contract prep (loose) and expedition prep (stricter).
- Conclude always forces idle/recovering.
- Multiple places (InvestigationScreen, bondService, ledger, wages) have special cases for 'working' showing the concept is intended to be restrictive.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: When an NPC is 'working', the assignment selector and all squad/contract/expedition prep screens do not allow selecting them for deployment (or clearly disable with the reason from the label). After combat, if an NPC was 'working' before (if we snapshot) or at minimum the system does not silently move earning NPCs to 'idle' without player action.
2. System/technical: Single source (constant or pure function isDeployable(npc: NpcRuntimeState): boolean) used by startCombat, missionPrep selector, expedition prep, and any add-to-squad. setNpcAssignment can stay permissive or add guard. ConcludeCombat no longer unconditionally overrides previous earning assignments (at least document or snapshot).
3. Tests: Add tests in missionPrep, combat.test, rosterReducers or titleEffects style that 'working' NPCs are excluded from squad selection and startCombat. Test that conclude on a previously 'working' NPC either restores or at least doesn't lose the state silently. Existing assignment tests continue to pass.

## Finding coverage
Direct user-reported violation of documented assignment rules. Part of the larger inconsistency in the assignment system that interacts with bonds, economy, and the new correspondence/intrigue work (deployed vs earning NPCs have different availability for letters, leverage, etc.).

title: Household intimacy: make shared quarters and house policy drive relationship aftermath
description:
## Why
The GDD's intimacy pillar does not end at affection math; it explicitly points toward pair bond, shared quarters, household formation, and domestic consequence. Current house systems expose room assignment and pairing policy, but there is little visible household-driven intimacy progression or aftermath that is distinct from investigation and intrigue content.

## What
Create a household-driven intimacy slice that uses shared quarters, domestic proximity, or house policy to surface at least one meaningful intimacy-adjacent progression or aftermath path outside investigation. This should make the house feel like a place where relationships deepen, not only where evidence is stored.

## Why now
House systems already exist and are increasingly visible. Without a domestic follow-through path, the household half of the intimacy pillar remains underpowered compared to intrigue and investigation.

## Player impact
Players will see that assigning people, shaping house policy, and building domestic stability can deepen or alter relationships in ways that matter.

## System impact
Expected to touch house selectors/UI, pairing or romance aftermath hooks, room assignment semantics, and possibly event/result surfaces for domestic relationship beats.

## Does not include
- no full family simulator
- no broad rewrite of room systems
- no expansion of all heir/pregnancy systems at once
- no requirement to add explicit sensual scenes in this slice

## Evidence
- GDD explicitly ties courtship to pair bond and household formation
- HouseScreen already exposes room assignment and pairing policy
- current household systems are visible but light on relationship-specific payoff
- independent review found household progression to be a missing counterweight in the current backlog

## Acceptance Criteria
### Player-visible acceptance
At least one household/room/pairing state now produces a clear relationship-facing consequence or follow-up that the player can understand without using investigation systems.

### System / technical acceptance
The feature uses house/pairing/relationship state coherently and does not invent a separate disconnected intimacy subsystem.

### Tests / verification acceptance
Coverage proves that a household-driven relationship beat can occur through domestic progression alone.

## Dependencies
Parent umbrella: destiny-jed7.

## Finding coverage
Covers the missing household/shared-quarters counterweight identified in the intimacy drift analysis.

## Fiction contract
A house is not only a warehouse for clues and debt papers. If the intimacy pillar is real, shared rooms, domestic rules, and living under one roof must be able to change what people become to one another.
notes: Parent umbrella: destiny-jed7. This bead exists specifically because household/shared-quarters progression was found to be underrepresented compared to intrigue/investigation systems.

title: Courtship loop: add explicit player-facing relationship-building actions
description:
## Why
Relationship and intimacy systems currently exist mostly as hidden progression math, passive NPC pairing, and policy state. The player lacks a concrete, visible courtship loop with explicit actions that build toward attachment and commitment. This leaves investigation/intrigue loops richer and more legible than romance-building itself.

## What
Add a player-facing courtship slice with explicit actions, gating, and visible progression. The slice should expose at least one intentional route from attraction/affinity into deeper relationship investment through actions such as invitation, shared time, gift, or personal declaration, with readable consequences on relationship state.

## Why now
This is the clearest missing counterweight to the current intrigue/investigation momentum. Without a visible courtship loop, the sensual/intimacy pillar remains more theoretical than playable.

## Player impact
Players can intentionally build intimacy with a named character through chosen actions rather than relying on hidden drift or discovering compromise content elsewhere.

## System impact
Expected to touch NpcDetailPanel or related relationship UI, relationship action dispatch, romance progression commands, and aftermath/event/log surfaces tied to courtship milestones.

## Does not include
- no full dating-sim tree for every NPC
- no overhaul of all directed relationship math
- no new explicit content by itself
- no politics/leverage consumption beyond proving the courtship loop exists

## Evidence
- GDD defines courtship as a real multi-step gameplay loop
- current runtime has relationship advancement code but little player-facing surface for intentional courtship
- current NPC detail UI exposes bars/talk/gifts but no robust courtship-action loop
- independent review found no open bead for romance or courtship as a player-facing system

## Acceptance Criteria
### Player-visible acceptance
At least one romance-eligible NPC can be advanced through an explicit courtship action path the player can see and choose. The game communicates what changed afterward.

### System / technical acceptance
Courtship actions use existing relationship/intimacy primitives where possible and feed actual relationship state instead of ad hoc flavor-only logs.

### Tests / verification acceptance
Coverage proves that the courtship action path changes relationship progression and does not require investigation or intrigue systems to access intimacy-adjacent advancement.

## Dependencies
Parent umbrella: destiny-jed7.

## Finding coverage
Covers the missing player-facing courtship loop counterweight identified in the intimacy drift analysis.

## Fiction contract
Desire in Valdenmoor is not only something overheard, blackmailed, or discovered in records. The player must be able to court someone directly and feel the cost, risk, and warmth of doing so.
notes: Parent umbrella: destiny-jed7. This bead exists specifically because no open romance/courtship bead was present when the intimacy drift review was run.

title: Intimacy pillar: restore courtship and household progression as the primary path
description:
## Why
Project Destiny's canonical design and content policy define sensual/intimate progression as something earned through relationship depth, courtship, pair bond, and household formation. But current runtime depth, UI surface area, and backlog momentum are much heavier on investigation, intrigue, compromise, correspondence, and leverage. Without a direct counterweight, the live game risks teaching intimacy as something discovered, weaponized, or exposed before it is meaningfully built.

## What
Create a focused epic that restores courtship and household progression as the primary player-facing route into intimacy-adjacent content. The epic should ensure that intrigue, correspondence, compromise, and political leverage remain downstream consumers of relationship depth rather than de facto substitutes for it.

## Why now
Multiple independent reviews found the same drift pattern: the design intent is correct, but the stronger runtime and backlog energy currently sit on investigation/intrigue. This needs an explicit backlog correction before more compromising/intimate leverage content lands.

## Player impact
Players will gain a clearer path to build attraction, attachment, and household intimacy through chosen actions and shared domestic progression, instead of mainly encountering intimacy-adjacent content as evidence, blackmail, custody, or aftermath.

## System impact
Expected follow-ups touch relationship UI, courtship actions, room/household integration, event aftermath surfaces, and the sequencing/constraints on intrigue-consuming intimacy systems.

## Does not include
- no rollback of investigation or intrigue systems
- no removal of correspondence, blackmail, or leverage fiction
- no broad rewrite of all intimate or captivity content
- no immediate implementation in this umbrella bead

## Evidence
- GDD relationship/courtship/household progression is explicit
- content policy requires earned consensual intimacy through established relationship depth
- current runtime depth is much stronger for investigation than for player-facing romance/courtship
- backlog currently has open intrigue/intimacy consumers but no comparable open courtship/romance beads
- three independent reviews on 2026-06-02 reached a `partial` drift verdict

## Acceptance Criteria
### Player-visible acceptance
Follow-up beads under this epic create at least one clear player-facing courtship path and one household-driven intimacy path that do not rely on investigation reveal loops.

### System / technical acceptance
The backlog explicitly distinguishes between systems that build relationship depth and systems that consume or weaponize it.

### Tests / verification acceptance
Child beads define concrete verification for relationship-first progression and prevent intrigue systems from front-loading intimate payoff without prerequisite depth.

## Success Criteria
- Courtship becomes a visible, intentional player loop rather than hidden background math only.
- Household/shared-quarters progression gains at least one concrete intimacy-adjacent payoff path.
- Intrigue and correspondence beads explicitly depend on pre-existing relationship depth and stop reading as the primary unlock route to intimate content.

## Finding coverage
Covers the newly identified drift risk that sensual/intimate content may become more playable through investigation/intrigue than through courtship/household progression.

## Fiction contract
In Valdenmoor, intimacy can be exploited, exposed, and politicized. But for the player-facing pillar to hold, the first truth of intimacy must still be chosen relationship-building, not forensic discovery.
notes: Planned child slices created on 2026-06-02: destiny-tr29 (explicit player-facing courtship actions) and destiny-68j4 (household/shared-quarters relationship aftermath). These are the first corrective counterweights to the current intrigue/investigation momentum.

title: Marion dialogue: consume one-shot clue topics after first discussion
description:
## Why
Marion's clue-talk options for the bureau chit and arrangement note currently behave like repeatable root topics instead of consumed follow-up beats. Because they are gated only by `hasItem`, the same clue exposition can be triggered again and again even after the player has already shown the item and received the associated main-quest hint.

## What
Make Marion's one-shot house-clue topics behave like consumed dialogue beats. After the player has presented a relevant clue and received the associated follow-up, the same root-level prompt should either disappear, downgrade into a different follow-up line, or otherwise stop replaying the same exposition as if it were new.

## Why now
This is currently visible in one of the first important house clue chains. It weakens clue follow-through and makes Marion feel scripted incorrectly rather than informed by what the player has already discussed.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Obtain the bureau chit or arrangement note.
2. Talk to Marion and choose the corresponding clue topic.
3. Finish the branch and exit the conversation.
4. Reopen Marion's dialogue while still holding the item.
5. Observe that the same clue topic is offered again and repeats the same exposition.

## Player impact
Players will get a sense of progression from discussing clues with Marion instead of replaying the same revelation indefinitely.

## System impact
Expected to touch Marion dialogue conditions, resolved-choice gating, and possibly clue follow-up copy or topic state.

## Does not include
- no broad rewrite of Marion's relationship dialogue
- no redesign of the house clue chain beyond consumed topic behavior
- no generic dialogue journaling system unless strictly required by the fix

## Evidence
- `marion-choice-ledger-chit` is gated only by `hasItem`
- `marion-choice-arrangement-below` is gated only by `hasItem`
- both branches deliver exposition/hints and then exit without preventing replay
- player review explicitly called out the bureau chit path as broken and repetitive

## Acceptance Criteria
### Player-visible acceptance
After discussing a one-shot house clue with Marion, the same root prompt no longer replays as if the clue were being shown for the first time.

### System / technical acceptance
Marion's clue topics use consumed-topic gating or an equivalent state rule that distinguishes first discussion from later follow-up.

### Tests / verification acceptance
Regression coverage proves that the bureau chit and arrangement-note topics do not repeat the same first-time exposition indefinitely.

## Dependencies
No blocker beads required.

## Finding coverage
Covers the new finding that Marion's item-gated clue talks are repeatable when they should behave like consumed investigative beats.

## Fiction contract
When the player shows Marion a concrete piece of house evidence, that moment becomes shared knowledge. The game must not repeatedly present the same revelation as new.
notes:
Player report specifically called out  ->  -> immediate exit -> same topic offered again. Arrangement-note path should be checked under the same consumed-topic rule.
Player report specifically called out the bureau-chit path: choose the clue topic, receive the debt-shaped revelation, exit the scene, then see the same clue topic offered again. The arrangement-note path should be checked under the same consumed-topic rule.

title: Dialogue runtime: do not resume future conversations on leave-only terminal nodes
description:
## Why
The dialogue runtime remembers the last visited node for each conversation and resumes from it when the player starts talking again. That is useful for interrupted conversations, but it breaks when a branch ends on a node with no visible choices. In those cases the player must manually press `Leave`, and on the next conversation start the game resumes on the same dead-end node, showing only `Leave` again.

## What
Fix dialogue resume so terminal leaf nodes do not strand future conversations in a leave-only state. The implementation may clear or ignore persisted visited nodes when the last node has no visible choices, or otherwise establish a canonical rule for when a conversation should reopen at the root instead of resuming.

## Why now
This is a player-facing conversation lock pattern in current content. The issue is already visible with Marion and Verek and makes core NPC conversations feel broken.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start a conversation that can reach a node with `choices: []` through a non-null `nextNodeId`.
2. Reach that node and press `Leave`.
3. Reopen the same NPC conversation.
4. Observe that the dialogue resumes on the dead-end node and offers only `Leave` instead of the normal opening node.

## Player impact
Players can talk to the same NPC again after concluding a branch instead of being trapped on a stale end-node.

## System impact
Expected to touch dialogue resume rules in reducers/commands/selectors and possibly add a clearer distinction between interrupted nodes and terminal nodes.

## Does not include
- no broad rewrite of dialogue content
- no redesign of all NPC dialogue trees
- no new relationship or quest content by itself

## Evidence
- `startDialogue` resumes from `visitedDialogueNodes`
- `endDialogue` clears active dialogue ids but does not clear visited node memory
- current content contains 14 incoming edges to empty-choice leaf nodes, including 13 in Marion and 1 in Verek
- player review reported repeated `Leave`-only conversations after certain Marion paths

## Acceptance Criteria
### Player-visible acceptance
After ending a completed branch, reopening that NPC starts at a sensible opening node instead of a stale leave-only terminal node.

### System / technical acceptance
The runtime distinguishes resumable interrupted nodes from completed terminal leaf nodes, or otherwise guarantees that empty-choice leaves do not persist as future entry points.

### Tests / verification acceptance
Regression coverage proves that reopening Marion and Verek after a leaf-node branch no longer strands the player on `Leave` only.

## Dependencies
No blocker beads required.

## Finding coverage
Covers the new finding that terminal dialogue nodes currently persist as reopen points, creating apparent conversation locks.

## Fiction contract
A finished conversational beat can end the scene, but it should not rewrite the NPC's entire future opening state into a single stale line with no ability to speak again.
notes: Current content audit on 2026-06-02 found 14 incoming edges to empty-choice terminal nodes: 13 in Marion, 1 in Verek. This is not only a Marion content complaint; the runtime resume rule is too permissive for terminal leaves.

title: House clues: clarify removal chit, bureau ledger, and player follow-through
description:
## Why
The house-search clue chain currently mixes three different artifacts without clearly telling the player what each one is for: the starting bureau ledger in inventory, the ledger-removal chit found in the bureau, and the surviving bureau ledger found in the vault. The system knows how they interact, but the player-facing language does not clearly distinguish evidence, unlock clue, and usable document.

## What
Clarify the player-facing identity and follow-through of the house ledger artifacts. The bead should make it explicit which item is:
- proof that ledgers were selectively removed
- a surviving bureau ledger with evidence/intel value
- the clue or combination of clues that unlocks the next house step
It should also surface what the player can do next with each relevant artifact.

## Why now
This is a first-hour comprehension problem in one of the game's most important house-investigation loops. Leaving it muddy weakens the main-quest setup and makes the house search feel arbitrary.

## Player impact
Players will understand what they found, why it matters, and what action it unlocks next without having to infer the distinction from scattered logs or hidden state.

## System impact
Expected to touch item naming/description, house search presentation, Marion follow-up prompts, vault clue messaging, and any related journal/log/tooltip surface.

## Does not include
- no redesign of the entire main-quest clue chain
- no new major quest branch beyond clarifying existing house evidence flow
- no economy rebalance for document items

## Evidence
- the bureau search awards a ledger-removal chit
- the vault search awards a surviving bureau ledger
- a bureau ledger also exists in the starting inventory
- current player review found the distinction between these artifacts unclear and asked what any of them actually do

## Acceptance Criteria
### Player-visible acceptance
The player can distinguish the removal chit, the bureau ledger, and any active clue follow-up from the house UI and supporting copy. The next action is explicit.

### System / technical acceptance
Item definitions, house discovery copy, and follow-up surfaces use consistent terminology and do not imply the same object was found twice unless that is canonically true and stated.

### Tests / verification acceptance
Coverage proves the first-hour house evidence flow communicates the difference between removal evidence and surviving ledger evidence.

## Dependencies
No blocker beads required.

## Finding coverage
Covers the new finding that the house ledger clue chain is internally logical but not operationally legible to the player.

## Fiction contract
The house search yields distinct pieces of evidence, not a vague pile of interchangeable ledgers. Each artifact must preserve its own narrative role: removed records, surviving record, or clue to the vault.
notes:
Player-facing confusion to resolve explicitly: the player can plausibly read the current chain as 'I already have a ledger, then I find another ledger, then I find a chit about ledgers.' The fix must state what each artifact is for and what the next verb is.
Additional concrete player-facing gap: the Marion follow-up for the bureau chit / arrangement note is not only unclear but currently replayable forever while the item remains in inventory. Coordinate with destiny-e6ex so clue readability and consumed-topic behavior land together instead of fighting each other.

title: Housing: make Marion's salvaged room consistent with occupancy and fiction
description:
## Why
The house now visibly supports room occupancy, but `room-marion-quarters` still asserts Marion-specific identity in room name, room flavor, and search copy while Marion is not actually assigned there at game start. This creates a direct contradiction between fiction and state.

## What
Make the salvaged Marion room truthful again. The bead should decide and implement the correct combination of:
- initial room assignment for Marion
- room naming that does not overstate permanent ownership if the room is meant to be reassignable
- room flavor/search copy that matches the actual runtime truth

## Why now
The contradiction became much more visible after room occupancy was surfaced to the player. Leaving it in place makes the house system feel unreliable and undermines confidence in room assignment semantics.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start a fresh game state and open the House screen.
2. Find the room currently labeled around Marion's salvaged quarters.
3. Observe that the room name/flavor implies Marion-specific current use.
4. Observe that Marion is not assigned to the room in runtime occupancy state.

## Player impact
Players will no longer be told a room is Marion's or actively lived-in by her while the state shows otherwise. The house will feel more coherent and trustworthy.

## System impact
Expected to touch initial game state, room defaults, room labels, room flavor/search copy, and house-screen occupancy presentation.

## Does not include
- no broader house-room redesign
- no expansion of Marion's personal storyline beyond what is needed to keep the room truthful
- no general room reservation system unless strictly required by the fix

## Evidence
- room label currently references Marion directly
- search/discovery copy describes the room as lived in by Marion
- Marion currently has `roomAssignment: null` at game start
- player review identified the contradiction immediately after occupancy became visible

## Acceptance Criteria
### Player-visible acceptance
At game start, the room's name, copy, and occupant state tell one consistent story about Marion and the salvaged room.

### System / technical acceptance
Initial state, room metadata, and occupancy UI all agree on whether Marion is assigned there and on what the room is canonically called.

### Tests / verification acceptance
Coverage proves the initial house view no longer presents a Marion-specific room with no Marion assignment unless the copy explicitly describes it as former use rather than current occupancy.

## Dependencies
No blocker beads required.

## Finding coverage
Covers the new finding that the salvaged Marion room currently contradicts visible occupancy state.

## Fiction contract
This is a salvaged practical space preserved through Marion's effort during the house's decline. The game must not present it as simultaneously Marion's current room and nobody's room.
notes: Player preference from review: best fix is likely both (1) soften the room name/copy so the room is not ontologically hard-bound to Marion forever and (2) assign Marion to the room at game start so visible occupancy matches the fiction.

title: Debt runtime and UI: reflect canonical claimant and enforcement model
description:
## Why
After introducing `debtCreditorFactionId`, the debt runtime now surfaces `Gilded Court` as if it were simultaneously the claimant, the pressure source, and the party that acknowledges settlement. That makes interest pressure, standing deltas, and debt copy feel incoherent. Once the debt-chain truth is defined, runtime and UI need to reflect it consistently.

## What
Implement the chosen debt-chain model in runtime and player-facing surfaces. This includes replacing or refining the current generic `creditor` handling so that interest pressure, settlement acknowledgment, and debt UI point to the correct role or combination of roles.

## Why now
The current implementation is already player-visible and is teaching the wrong story. Leaving it in place will make later debt events, NPC reactions, and conspiracy beats harder to reconcile.

## Player impact
Players will see a debt relationship that makes sense: who they owe, who is leaning on them, and what settling or resisting the claim means politically.

## System impact
Expected to touch game-state debt fields, debt selectors, debt logs, Dashboard/Ledger/StatusBar debt labeling, and any interest or settlement behavior tied to faction standing.

## Does not include
- no new debt questline content beyond what is needed to keep copy truthful
- no debt rebalance beyond changes required by the new role split
- no full faction politics overhaul

## Evidence
- current debt runtime uses a single `debtCreditorFactionId`
- current interest and settlement behavior are tied directly to that field
- player review found the visible effect incoherent
- destiny-e6ii will define the canonical truth this bead implements

## Acceptance Criteria
### Player-visible acceptance
Debt-facing UI and logs no longer imply a single actor is simultaneously claimant, enforcer, and beneficiary unless the decision bead explicitly says so.

### System / technical acceptance
Runtime fields, selectors, and logs use the role contract defined in destiny-e6ii. Standing deltas and interest pressure attach to the correct actor or actor class.

### Tests / verification acceptance
Regression coverage proves the chosen role split is reflected in selectors/logs and does not silently regress to generic `creditor` behavior.

## Dependencies
Depends on destiny-e6ii.

## Finding coverage
Implements the debt-role coherence finding opened by the current `creditor` runtime change.

## Fiction contract
Debt pressure must read as part of an engineered political arrangement, not as a generic bill from an unnamed bank or a fictionally flattened faction blob.
notes: Implementation scope should explicitly revisit: interest-pressure source, settlement acknowledgement, player-facing debt labels, and whether standing shifts belong to claimant, enforcement faction, or both.

title: Debt fiction: define claimant, beneficiary, and enforcement roles
description:
## Why
The current debt runtime collapses three different narrative roles into one `creditor`: the legal claimant, the faction or institution enforcing the claim, and the faction or house benefiting from House Valdric's fall. Lore and NPC content currently point to a more specific chain: Harlen Voss or his creditor line as claimant, House Merrow as chief beneficiary, and the Gilded Court or Compact machinery as political cover or enforcement. The current model makes debt interest, debt payoff, and player-facing debt UI feel narratively wrong.

## What
Define the canonical debt-chain contract for Project Destiny. The decision must specify which runtime fields represent:
- the legal claimant who holds the note
- the faction or institution enforcing or legitimizing the claim
- the beneficiary who profits from the house staying buried or weakened
It must also define which of those roles may affect interest pressure, standing shifts, events, and player-facing labels.

## Why now
A generic `creditor` field has already been added to runtime and surfaced to the player. In its current form it creates fiction drift and confusing system consequences. This should be corrected before more debt events, debt UI, or conspiracy content is layered on top.

## Player impact
Players will be able to understand who is claiming the debt, who is pressuring the house, and who benefits from the arrangement. Debt will feel like part of the conspiracy instead of a flattened finance mechanic.

## System impact
Expected to touch debt runtime terminology, selector contracts, event wording, debt logs, and future debt-facing quest/event design. This bead is a design-decision contract; downstream code changes should be split into follow-up implementation beads.

## Does not include
- no runtime implementation yet
- no rebalance of debt amounts or deadlines
- no expansion of the debt questline by itself
- no broad rewrite of faction lore beyond clarifying the debt chain

## Evidence
- `docs/narrative.md` identifies the Gilded Court as the power bloc behind the strike on the house
- `data/lore/valdris-succession-question.md` identifies House Merrow as chief beneficiary of the erasure
- `data/definitions/npcs.json` identifies Harlen Voss as descendant of the creditor line tied to the Valdric default
- current runtime and UI use `debtCreditorFactionId = faction-gilded-court`
- recent player review found the current change incoherent

## Rationale
Separating claimant, enforcement, and beneficiary preserves the conspiracy's structure and gives later debt systems somewhere truthful to attach. It avoids baking a fiction shortcut into logs, standing changes, and quest/event logic.

## Alternatives Considered
- Keep `faction-gilded-court` as the single creditor and explain the ambiguity in lore. Rejected because it leaves player-facing debt interactions incoherent.
- Roll back the creditor concept entirely and return debt to an anonymous number. Rejected because the debt should remain socially and politically grounded.
- Model only a named creditor and ignore enforcement/beneficiary roles. Rejected because it still flattens the conspiracy and leaves House Merrow/Court pressure underexplained.

## Acceptance
### Player-visible acceptance
A documented truth exists for who holds the debt, who enforces it, and who profits. Future player-facing debt copy can name those roles without contradiction.

### System / technical acceptance
The bead records a canonical field/label contract for debt claimant, debt enforcement, and debt beneficiary, including which systems are allowed to react to each role.

### Tests / verification acceptance
Follow-up implementation beads reference this decision explicitly before touching debt runtime, selectors, or debt UI.

## Dependencies
No blocker beads required. This decision should precede further debt runtime/UI changes.

## Finding coverage
Covers the new finding that `creditor` currently collapses claimant, enforcer, and beneficiary into one misleading runtime truth.

## Fiction contract
House Valdric's debt is not a neutral bank loan. It is a structured weapon inside a political conspiracy. The game must preserve the difference between who holds the paper, who applies the pressure, and who profits from the house staying compromised.
notes:
Review finding: the current  change is not rejected because the Court is an enemy, but because it collapses too many roles. Preserve the distinction between claimant, enforcement, and beneficiary even if one actor occupies more than one role in a specific branch.
Review finding: the current debt field should not collapse claimant, enforcement, and beneficiary into one truth even if a single faction appears prominently in multiple places.

title: Deepen high-value investigation quest handlers beyond single-operation resolves
description:
Why
Several investigation quests rely on archive retrieval, mediation, custody breakout, or proof-building fantasy that should not be flattened into generic one-operation wording. The quest authoring audit currently points these cases at a non-existent follow-up bead, which leaves a real gap in the backlog.

What
Create a focused feature bead for deepening the investigation runtime or adding specialized handlers for the high-value quests that should keep their stronger fiction.

Why now
The current investigation authoring cleanup needs a real destination for the quests that should not be solved by copy weakening alone.

Player impact
Important investigation contracts can eventually deliver the richer fantasy they promise instead of being downgraded into generic text.

System impact
Investigation runtime, quest handlers, quest content, and follow-up tests.

Does not include
No implementation in this bead. No full procedural quest generation.

Acceptance
1. The bead explicitly names the high-value investigation quests that require deeper runtime support.
2. It records the likely handler families needed, such as retrieval, mediation, proof-building, or custody breakout.
3. The quest authoring audit can map runtime-too-shallow findings to this bead without gaps.

Finding coverage
F6/F7 follow-up gap from the 2026-06-01 quest authoring alignment audit.
notes: Scope guard from intimacy drift review: investigation deepening may intersect with compromise, custody, or leverage fiction, but it must not become the de facto primary route for intimate payoff or relationship-first sensual content. That counterweight is tracked separately in destiny-jed7, destiny-tr29, and destiny-68j4.
close_reason: Session completed multiple P2/P3 tasks; t2x7 remains open for future investigation work

title: Align remaining investigation quest copy to actual runtime or deepen objective handlers
description:
Why
The quest-duration audit shows that multiple investigation contracts still promise retrieval, infiltration, mediation, case-building, or surveillance behavior that the current investigation runtime does not fully simulate. Fixing one quest is not enough; the remaining catalog still has trust-breaking copy.

What
For all remaining investigation quests, either narrow the authored copy to what the runtime currently supports or deepen the objective handlers where the stronger fiction is important enough to keep.

Why now
The duration contract and surveillance fix removed the most visible Day 1 bug, but the broader catalog still contains misleading promises.

Player impact
Quest text becomes consistently believable across the Work Board, not only in the first two reported quests.

System impact
Quest content definitions, investigation runtime, and targeted follow-up tests.

Does not include
No full investigation-system redesign. No procedural quest generation.

Acceptance
1. Every investigation quest with strong verbs is either reauthored to match current runtime or linked to a deeper handler bead.
2. No investigation briefing silently implies multi-scene or multi-day behavior unless the runtime actually supports it.
3. Audit mapping is preserved in the quest-system docs.

Finding coverage
F6: remaining investigation catalog still overpromises beyond the current runtime loop.
close_reason: Remaining overpromising investigation briefings retuned to the current runtime; deeper cases mapped to destiny-t2x7.

title: Audit quest authoring for fiction/runtime mismatch and misleading duration copy
description:
Why
Current quest copy can promise behavior the runtime does not implement. The player-reported Day 1 case suggests this may not be isolated to one investigation and one escort contract.

What
Audit all quest templates for mismatch between briefing/opening text and actual runtime loop, then either adjust content or add follow-up implementation beads.

Why now
The current issue is a trust problem across the whole Work Board, not just one quest.

Player impact
Quest text becomes reliable. The player can believe what a lead says.

System impact
Quest content catalog, authoring standards, audit docs, and follow-up backlog only.

Does not include
No bulk implementation inside this audit bead.

Acceptance
1. Every current quest is classified as aligned, copy too strong, or runtime too shallow.
2. Every mismatch is mapped to an existing or new bead.
3. Authoring guidance is updated so future quests do not repeat the problem.

Acceptance Criteria
1. Audit document produced.
2. Mismatch-to-bead mapping produced.
3. At least one authoring-rule update recorded.

Finding coverage
F4: broader quest-system trust audit triggered by Day 1 report.
notes: Initial audit source: /Users/andre.dittrich/privat/projects/project_destiny/docs/quest-system-duration-audit-2026-06-01.md. First required pass should classify at least all Day 1-discoverable and first-hour leads before broadening to the full catalog.
close_reason: Audit completed, mappings corrected, authoring workflow updated.

title: Make delivery and survival contracts declare and honor execution duration
description:
Why
Delivery and survival contracts currently advance to on-site and settle after a single watch. For contracts whose fiction implies escort, holding, or waiting over time, the implementation is too short and too silent.

What
Add explicit execution duration for delivery/survival contracts and make the on-site loop consume the required number of watches or days before settlement.

Why now
`Safe Passage Through the Pale` currently settles on Day 1 evening immediately after acceptance, which reads like a bug even if the current code path is technically consistent.

Player impact
Escort and hold jobs feel spatially and temporally believable.

System impact
Quest templates, contract execution UI, on-site quest lifecycle helpers, activity log, and tests.

Does not include
No combat encounter redesign. No travel map overhaul.

Acceptance
1. `quest-pale-wagon-escort` cannot be fully completed in a single watch unless its template explicitly says so.
2. Contract execution UI shows duration remaining, not just risk and consequence.
3. Tests cover same-day acceptance plus premature settlement attempts.

Finding coverage
F2: `Safe Passage Through the Pale` resolves in one watch despite escort fiction.

title: Audit quest authoring for fiction/runtime mismatch and misleading duration copy
description:
Why
Current quest copy can promise behavior the runtime does not implement. The player-reported Day 1 case suggests this may not be isolated to one investigation and one escort contract.

What
Audit all quest templates for mismatch between briefing/opening text and actual runtime loop, then either adjust content or add follow-up implementation beads.

Why now
The current issue is a trust problem across the whole Work Board, not just one quest.

Player impact
Quest text becomes reliable. The player can believe what a lead says.

System impact
Quest content catalog, authoring standards, audit docs, and follow-up backlog only.

Does not include

title: Model direct world-NPC relationship dynamics beyond household growth
description:
## Why
The project already has soft bonds, pairing, household growth, custody, and faction politics, but there is no explicit ticket for direct world-NPC to world-NPC relationship dynamics as an independent system. Rivalry, protection, dependency, patronage, resentment, and escalation between non-player actors are still too indirect and often collapse into logs or household-level flavor.

## What
Add direct relationship dynamics between non-player actors: world NPCs can strengthen alliances, form patron-client ties, feud, protect one another, become dependent, intervene in each other's custody/household situations, and produce lasting social consequences that are not routed only through the player. Wire those dynamics into rumors, site access, quest emergence, and conflict escalation.

## Why now
The simulation substrate is now strong enough that this missing social layer is visible as a gap. Without it, the world is more alive than before, but still not fully agentic between non-player actors.

## Player impact
The city feels less player-centric and more like a place where other people make and break one another's fortunes even when the player is absent.

## System impact
Touches world NPC social simulation, site presence, household interactions, rumors, possibly quest lead generation and faction politics.

## Does not include
No full romance rewrite and no complete household economy overhaul.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. At least three direct non-player relationship dynamics exist beyond generic soft bonds, such as patronage, rivalry escalation, or protective intervention.
2. Those dynamics can alter later access, rumors, custody outcomes, or quest opportunities.
3. The effects persist across endDay ticks and do not require the player to be the initiator.

## Finding coverage
npc-dynamics-audit-2026-06-01 F1 F2

title: Make NPC and household actions materially affect district and city economy
description:
## Why
NPC actions currently touch player money, faction standing, household growth, and activity logs, but they rarely move the shared city economy in a material way. This makes the world feel socially active but economically too decoupled.

## What
Wire NPC, household, and faction actions into actual economic state changes: district scarcity or relief, city resource pressure, market or labor pressure, debt enforcement, household resource attrition, and rival extraction or disruption. The goal is that NPC-led actions can change economic conditions even when the player is passive.

## Why now
The project already has NPC agency, faction activity, title effects, bond service, world households, and household growth. Without a shared economic consequence layer, these systems remain partially parallel.

## Player impact
The economy will feel like something actors struggle over, not just a player-owned balance sheet.

## System impact
Touches NPC agency, faction activity, household growth, bond service, city resources, district pressure, and possibly shop pricing or quest generation.

## Does not include
No full market simulation or commodity-by-commodity economy.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. At least three existing NPC/faction/household action paths change economic world state beyond player money alone.
2. Those changes can be observed in city resources, district-level economic pressure, household resources, or downstream pricing/availability.
3. The effects persist across endDay ticks and can influence later systems.

## Finding coverage
npc-economy-audit-2026-06-01 F3 F4

title: Balance non-player household growth and add real inter-house competition
description:
## Why
The current world-household growth loop is too generous and mostly parallel. It lets several top-funded households grow with very high probability, without paying meaningful costs or contesting the same opportunities. That weakens the living-world goal and risks making growth feel automatic instead of political.

## What
Rebalance non-player household growth so it consumes resources, faces scarcity and conflict pressure, and can be slowed, diverted, or blocked by rival households. Translate authored `activeConflicts` into actual simulation effects such as contested expansion, sabotage pressure, legal choke points, or reputation/security tradeoffs.

## Why now
The new site runtime, household growth, and abstract custody layers are in place. Without competition and cost now, later world-household content will rest on a too-easy substrate.

## Player impact
Rival houses will no longer simply get bigger in parallel. Their expansion pace and room functions will reflect pressure, scarcity, and conflict, making the city feel more alive and less mechanically generous.

## System impact
Touches world-household growth, site runtime persistence, authored conflict data, event/rumor generation, and possibly faction or district pressure systems.

## Does not include
No full city economy overhaul. No complete rebuild of every household authoring record.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Non-player household growth consumes or commits household resources instead of being free.
2. At least one authored `activeConflict` type materially changes a rival household's growth odds or outcome.
3. Households with weak resources or active opposition no longer grow at near-automatic rates.
4. Competition outcomes can surface as visible logs, rumors, or state changes.

## Finding coverage
household-growth-audit-2026-06-01 F1 F2

title: Generate quest and event hooks from site, room, and custody state
description:
## Why
If rooms, sites, and custody become real but content generation remains district/faction-only, the new substrate will feel systemic but dramatically underused.

## What
Extend quest/event generation so site, room, and custody state can create leads and consequences.

Examples:
- hidden captive rumor
- transfer in progress
- compromised archive room
- servant corridor witness
- sanctuary under pressure
- site expansion displacing residents

## Why now
This is the narrative bridge that turns location simulation into playable RPG content.

## Player impact
The player gets rescue, protection, infiltration, and witness stories that arise from the city's physical and social layout.

## System impact
Extends quest/event generation and should coordinate with destiny-2c2q once site runtime lands.

## Does not include
No campaign rewrite. No requirement that every site generate content equally often.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Site and room state can act as quest/event causes.
2. Custody, shelter, and household pressure can create leads without bespoke one-off logic every time.
3. Resulting hooks can surface through rumors, dialogue, logs, or discovered evidence.

## Finding coverage
F3 F6
close_reason: Implemented generic site/custody hooks: abstract captivity rumors, known-room custody leads, and protected-site pressure events.

title: Define site visibility and reveal hierarchy for abstract and concrete locations
description:
## Why
A hybrid site model will fail in play if the UI reveals either too much concrete room detail too early or too little for the player to act on. The project needs a clear visibility grammar for abstract, site, and room levels.

## What
Define the player-facing visibility and reveal rules for abstract and concrete sites.

Cover:
- what district level can show about a site
- what site level can show before room entry
- what room-level occupancy and custody becomes visible and when
- rumor vs clue vs witness report vs confirmed knowledge
- how hidden custody stays believable without feeling arbitrary

## Why now
This is the comprehension guardrail for the whole hybrid-location direction.

## Player impact
The player can understand where they are, what they know, and what they still need to discover.

## System impact
Connects site runtime to rumors, quests, logs, POI presentation, and future room screens.

## Does not include
No full visual redesign. No combat UI changes.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Abstract/site/room information boundaries are documented and actionable.
2. Hidden, rumored, and known occupancy/custody states are clearly distinguished.
3. Follow-up UI work can implement reveal logic without inventing ad hoc rules.

## Finding coverage
F1 F3 F6
close_reason: Documented site visibility and reveal hierarchy for abstract, site, and room layers; defined knowledge states, custody reveal ladder, and collapse-back visibility contract in docs/site-visibility-and-reveal-contract.md.

title: Define site visibility and UI hierarchy for abstract and concrete locations
description:
## Why
A hybrid site model will fail in play if the UI reveals either too little or far too much. The player needs a clear distinction between what is rumored, what is visible from district/site level, and what is confirmed at room level.

## What
Define the UI and visibility rules for abstract and concrete sites.

Cover:
- what district cards show about a site
- what site view shows before room entry
- what room-level occupancy becomes visible and when
- how rumors, clues, witness reports, and discovered evidence escalate knowledge
- how hidden custody can remain hidden without becoming arbitrary

## Why now
This is the player-comprehension guardrail for the whole direction.

## Player impact
The world becomes legible without dumping all room detail at the wrong level.

## System impact
- connects site runtime to quest UX, rumors, logs, and POI presentation
- prevents room/custody simulation from becoming invisible or overwhelming

## Does not include
- full visual redesign of all district and site screens
- combat UI work
- free-roam presentation

## Evidence
world-sites-captivity-narrative-review-2026-05-29.md; prior UX audits already show that hierarchy and post-action readability are fragile.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Abstract, site, and room-level information boundaries are documented.
2. Hidden vs rumored vs known occupancy/custody is clearly defined.
3. Follow-up UI beads can implement the system without inventing ad hoc reveal logic.

## Finding coverage
F1 F3 F6
close_reason: Superseded by existing site-hierarchy/UX follow-up direction; kept in review doc as requirement, not separate duplicate bead.

title: Generate quest and event hooks from site, room, and custody state
description:
## Why
Once rooms, households, and custody become real, quests and events should emerge from them. Otherwise the new site model will stay systemic but under-authored, and the player will not feel the dramatic payoff.

## What
Add site-driven quest and event hooks that can originate from room and custody state.

Examples:
- hidden captive rumor
- transfer in progress
- room compromised
- witness in a service wing
- sanctuary under pressure
- household expansion causing displacement
- a guard, steward, or attendant quietly opening access

## Why now
This is the narrative bridge from site simulation into playable story content.

## Player impact
The player encounters rescue, blackmail, protection, infiltration, and shelter stories that arise from the world's physical and social layout.

## System impact
- extends quest/event generation beyond district/faction-only logic
- adapts destiny-2c2q toward room/site-aware emergence

## Does not include
- a full authored campaign rewrite
- every site generating content equally often
- free exploration scenes

## Evidence
world-sites-captivity-narrative-review-2026-05-29.md; current Mira and house audits already show the need for stronger place -> clue -> consequence funnels.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Quest/event generation can cite site and room state as causes.
2. Captivity, shelter, and household pressure can create leads without hand-authoring every instance.
3. The player can learn about site problems through rumors, dialogue, access changes, or discovered evidence.

## Finding coverage
F3 F6
close_reason: Superseded by existing site-driven quest/event work under destiny-2c2q plus custody/location substrate wave; kept as review finding, not separate duplicate bead.

title: Implement abstract offscreen site simulation with preserved depth
description:
## Why
The clarified direction explicitly chooses hybrid simulation: abstract sites may exist offscreen, but their depth must not collapse. Without a dedicated offscreen model, the project will either oversimulate every room constantly or reduce unseen sites to shallow counters.

## What
Define and implement abstract offscreen site simulation that preserves narrative depth.

Cover:
- which social/custody states continue offscreen
- food/care/security/secrecy pressure
- room-state compression rules
- captive condition changes and visit frequency
- rumor/event generation from abstract site state
- re-concretization from preserved abstract memory

## Why now
This is the technical and narrative safeguard that makes the chosen hybrid model credible.

## Player impact
Places the player is not currently visiting still feel alive, risky, and consequential.

## System impact
- ties into endDay/world simulation without requiring fully concrete citywide room simulation
- provides the bridge between site runtime and quest/event generation

## Does not include
- always-on full city room simulation
- pathfinding or visual room traversal
- authored quest content by itself

## Evidence
world-sites-captivity-narrative-review-2026-05-29.md; c3sh already simulates offscreen social ties at district depth, but not site/custody depth.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Abstract sites preserve more than owner/security; they retain meaningful social and custody memory.
2. Concrete sites can return to abstraction without losing critical narrative state.
3. Offscreen captive and room interactions can still produce believable outcomes and rumors.

## Finding coverage
F3 F4 F5
close_reason: Superseded by pre-existing destiny-d9o3 (same abstract custody/offscreen depth scope).

title: Spatialize captivity and bond custody at site and room level
description:
## Why
Captivity currently records who holds an NPC, but not where the NPC is physically kept. That is too thin for Mira, bond rescues, detention houses, hidden prisoners, or any living-world custody story.

## What
Extend captivity and bonded custody into a spatial detention model.

Cover:
- siteId and roomId for custody
- custody owner vs physical holder vs local handler
- transfer between rooms and sites
- visible vs hidden custody
- care/neglect/watch intensity as site-aware drivers
- how bondStatus and captivityState interact without collapsing into one schema

## Why now
This is the core bead that makes general captivity believable instead of purely notional.

## Player impact
Rescue, shelter, capture, and transfer stories become concrete and investigatable.

## System impact
- successor to destiny-4n2j, destiny-766c, destiny-og7t
- establishes shared custody semantics for player and non-player actors

## Does not include
- explicit scene depiction of abuse
- capture combat redesign
- pregnancy-specific content authoring

## Evidence
world-sites-captivity-narrative-review-2026-05-29.md; quest-mira-rescue uses holding-floor/tannery room language already; content-policy expects consequence-first captivity logic.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Captive or bonded NPCs can be associated with a site and room, not only a holder.
2. The model distinguishes legal ownership from physical custody.
3. Transfer between rooms/sites is supported as a world event primitive.

## Finding coverage
F2 F3 F6
close_reason: Superseded by pre-existing destiny-d7lm (same spatial captivity scope).

title: Add shared room occupancy and access model for world sites
description:
## Why
World households already have authored rooms, but rooms are not runtime places with occupancy, access, or role meaning. If enterable buildings should all have rooms and NPCs should interact with those rooms, the project needs a general room occupancy/access model instead of decorative room lists.

## What
Design and implement the shared room runtime vocabulary for all site types.

Cover:
- room identity and room tags/functions
- occupant slots and occupant categories
- visitor vs resident vs staff vs guard vs captive vs patient vs guest
- restricted/locked/private/service access
- room assignment and eviction/transfer rules
- minimum room memory that survives re-abstraction

## Why now
Without this, spatial captivity and NPC room interaction have nowhere to attach.

## Player impact
The player can understand not only that a building has rooms, but what those rooms currently do and who is there.

## System impact
- successor to destiny-5dpf room lists
- creates the vocabulary needed by player house, world households, safehouses, detention houses, and story sites

## Does not include
- patrol/pathfinding
- free-movement exploration
- final UI polish for all site screens

## Evidence
world-sites-captivity-narrative-review-2026-05-29.md; data/definitions/worldHouseholds.json already authors room lists but not occupancy logic.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. A shared room occupancy/access model exists for all site types.
2. Captive, resident, staff, guard, and guest are represented distinctly.
3. The model explicitly states what room data persists when a site re-abstracts.

## Finding coverage
F1 F3 F5
close_reason: Superseded by pre-existing destiny-ziyp (same occupancy/room substrate scope).

title: Define canonical hybrid site runtime contract
description:
## Why
POIs, world households, player-house rooms, and story locations currently describe the same world layer in different ways. The project needs an explicit contract for how a site becomes concrete, how it returns to abstraction, and what state survives that transition.
