Without this decision, future captivity, room, and quest work will fork into incompatible implementations.

## What
Define the canonical hybrid site runtime model.

The decision must specify:
- relationship between POI, world household, and site runtime
- what makes a site abstract vs concrete
- triggers for concretization (entry, active quest, custody relevance, social heat, mission target)
- triggers for re-abstraction
- minimum state that survives abstraction (occupants, custody, room tags, damage, secrets, social tension, access restrictions)
- how player-house sites fit the same model without losing bespoke flavor

## Why now
This is the foundation contract for every later room, captivity, and site-driven quest bead.

## Player impact
The world becomes coherent: buildings do not randomly behave as menus in one place and physical sites in another.

## System impact
- architecture/product/workflow docs updated with the hybrid site contract
- follow-up beads can reference one site vocabulary instead of parallel abstractions

## Does not include
- implementation of runtime site entities
- room occupancy mechanics
- captive movement logic
- UI redesign by itself

## Evidence
world-sites-captivity-narrative-review-2026-05-29.md; destiny-5dpf non-goal conflict; quest-mira-rescue already uses room language.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. One written contract defines abstract vs concrete sites and concrete->abstract return behavior.
2. The contract names which state must persist through abstraction.
3. Follow-up beads can reference this document instead of redefining site semantics.

## Finding coverage
F1 F4 F5 F6
close_reason: Superseded by pre-existing destiny-m684 (same contract scope).

title: World Sites: Hybrid room, custody, and offscreen-depth simulation
description:
## Why
Project Destiny's lore, quest content, and existing worldHousehold data already imply a city of meaningful interiors, hidden custody, household pressure, and room-bound leverage. But the runtime still splits POIs, world households, captivity state, and bond ownership into parallel fragments. Enterable buildings are not yet true sites, captivity is holder-based instead of place-based, and offscreen buildings cannot preserve deep social state. This blocks believable rescue stories, house politics, bond custody, hiding, sheltering, and NPC-driven use of space.

## What
Create the umbrella epic for a hybrid world-site simulation model:
- all enterable buildings use a shared site/room vocabulary
- any NPC can be held in custody at a site and room
- NPCs can interact with rooms and captives
- offscreen abstract simulation preserves depth
- concrete sites can later return to abstract state without losing critical memory

This epic coordinates successor work to extend the current worldHousehold, captivityState, and bond systems instead of replacing them ad hoc.

## Why now
The current direction has now been clarified explicitly. The world already contains authored households, room lists, captivity states, and location-bound story language. Without a coordinated follow-up, future Mira, bond-service, and world-NPC work will become contradictory.

## Player impact
- buildings feel like institutions with interiors, not menu labels
- captives and dependents exist somewhere, under someone, for reasons the player can discover
- rescue, shelter, and infiltration stories gain credible spatial logic
- offscreen sites still generate believable consequences and rumors

## System impact
- establishes successor work for world households, rooms, captivity, bond custody, NPC co-presence, and site-driven quests/events
- aligns player-house room logic with a wider world-site model
- creates a shared contract for concrete <-> abstract site transitions

## Does not include
- free-roam movement
- always-on full simulation of every room in the city
- immediate replacement of existing POI or house systems
- Mira-specific quest scripting by itself

## Evidence
See docs/world-sites-captivity-narrative-review-2026-05-29.md. Existing support/conflict points include destiny-5dpf, destiny-4n2j, destiny-og7t, destiny-766c, destiny-c3sh, quest-mira-rescue, and worldHouseholds.json.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. A complete child-bead set exists for site contract, room occupancy/access, spatial captivity, abstract offscreen site simulation, and site-driven quest/event hooks.
2. Existing neighboring epics note the new dependency or scope interaction where relevant.
3. Finding coverage is explicit and no major gap is left unmapped.

## Finding coverage
F1 enterable houses need a general room model
F2 any NPC can be captive and captivity must be spatial
F3 NPCs must interact with rooms and captives
F4 abstract simulation must preserve depth
F5 concretized sites may later return to abstraction
F6 current quests and lore already imply place-based custody

## Success Criteria
- The project has a single agreed hybrid site model for concrete and abstract locations.
- Captivity, bond custody, and room-based NPC interaction are represented as successor work instead of fragmented follow-ups.
- Neighboring world, quest, and house epics are explicitly linked to the new site model.
close_reason: Superseded by pre-existing destiny-a23y epic and destiny-m684 ADR discovered after creation during backlog reconciliation.

title: General captivity runtime: any NPC held in a specific room under a specific holder
description:
## Why
Current captivity modeling covers condition, compliance, and bond only loosely, but not where the NPC is, in which room they are held, who physically controls the site, or how that changes. That is too thin for the clarified direction.

## What
Extend captivity into a world-bound runtime model:
- any NPC may be captive, missing, transferred, hidden, or rescued
- captivity references location instance and room instance when concrete
- captivity references holder or controlling actor and custody regime when abstract
- custody state can be moved, hidden, visited, guarded, supplied, or degraded

## Why now
Mira, bond-market rescues, detention houses, prisoners, and hostile households all need the same substrate.

## Player impact
Captive NPCs become real world targets with location, holder, and rescue context.

## System impact
Expands or refactors captivity contracts and the commands/selectors that currently assume captivity is only a status on roster NPCs.

## Does not include
No full dialogue content pass. No UI panel redesign beyond what the runtime requires.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Captivity can be represented for roster NPCs, story NPCs, and world NPCs.
2. Concrete captivity stores room and location identity.
3. Abstract captivity still stores enough regime and state detail to drive deep consequences.

## Finding coverage
F2 F3 F4
close_reason: Extended captivity into a canonical world-bound runtime with siteId, roomId, regime, transfer metadata, and a global npcCaptivityStates registry; synced roster mirrors and end-of-day degradation across roster and non-roster NPCs; added schema and regression coverage.

title: ADR: hybrid abstract-to-concrete location simulation contract
description:
## Why
The current codebase has no canonical answer for when a location is content only, when it has runtime state, and how it becomes concrete or abstract again. That decision must be explicit before implementation starts.

## What
Define the authoritative architecture contract for hybrid locations:
- abstract location runtime vs concrete room runtime
- what data persists in both states
- what events cause concretization
- what data survives collapse back to abstract state
- how POIs, world households, and the player house relate
- how occupancy and captivity reference locations and rooms

## Why now
This prevents incompatible ad hoc implementations in house rooms, bond rescue, captivity, and world-NPC scheduling.

## Player impact
Indirect but critical: consistent location behavior across the whole game.

## System impact
Architecture and domain-contract decision only.

## Does not include
No reducer or UI implementation.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Abstract vs concrete location state is defined with field-level persistence rules.
2. Enterable POIs, world households, and the player house have a unified conceptual model.
3. Collapse-back rules preserve consequences but may discard transient room detail.

## Finding coverage
F1 F2 F3 F5

title: General location runtime: occupancy and room-instance substrate
description:
## Why
Current rooms are player-house only, world households are content-only, and world NPCs only support a coarse location override. There is no substrate for room occupancy across the world.

## What
Implement a general runtime substrate for locations and room instances that can serve player house rooms, enterable POI interiors, world household rooms, and future detention cells, infirmaries, archives, taverns, and safe houses.

Each concretized location must support room instances, occupancy slots, access or security state, and named room roles.

## Why now
This is the shared base for visible NPC room presence, location-bound captivity, and future enterable-house interaction.

## Player impact
NPCs can visibly be in rooms, not just in abstract menus.

## System impact
New runtime model for location instances and room occupancy. Existing player-house room state becomes a special case or adapter onto the general model.

## Does not include
No deep captivity logic yet. No location economy simulation yet.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. One runtime model can represent player-house rooms and non-player locations.
2. Occupancy can reference an NPC and a room instance.
3. Existing house-room functionality remains representable through the new model.

## Finding coverage
F1 F4
close_reason: Added canonical site runtime and NPC site presence schemas, adapters for player house/world households/POIs, selectors for site runtime and occupancy, reducer hooks for presence updates, and schema/test coverage including initial game state update.

title: Hybrid world locations, room occupancy, and general captivity simulation
description:
## Why
Project Destiny currently has three disconnected partial models: player house rooms, authored world households as content only, and person-level captivity without a concrete location. The user's clarified direction changes this materially: all enterable houses should have rooms; captivity must be fully general for any NPC; offscreen simulation should be hybrid (abstract until relevant, then concrete), but abstract simulation must still carry deep social and psychological consequences; concretized locations may later collapse back to abstract state.

Without a dedicated architecture wave, the current code will drift into incompatible special cases: player-house-only room occupancy, bond-market-only captive handling, and world households that remain static lore records.

## What
Create the architecture and implementation backlog for a general world-location substrate that supports:
- all enterable houses and establishments having room structures
- location-bound occupancy for NPCs
- fully general captivity for any NPC, including foreign NPCs and world NPCs
- deep abstract offscreen simulation for custody, care, abuse, dependency, rumor, and rescue pressure
- on-demand concretization of locations when entered or when a story/system demands detail
- later collapse of concretized locations back into abstract runtime state without losing important consequences

## Why now
This is now a product-direction decision, not optional future polish. The current captivity and house systems already create content and state that will become migration debt if this substrate is delayed.

## Player impact
Locations stop feeling like menu labels. Captivity becomes a real world state. NPCs, households, and factions can hold, move, guard, visit, feed, exploit, rescue, and hide people in actual places.

## System impact
Touches world location architecture, room contracts, occupancy runtime, captivity runtime, world household simulation, ward/household integration, and existing bond/captivity UI assumptions.

## Does not include
No code implementation by this epic alone. No free-roaming exploration commitment. No tactical map rewrite.

## Success Criteria
- a coherent architecture exists for hybrid abstract/concrete locations
- existing related beads are remapped so they do not diverge from the new substrate
- follow-up beads cover occupancy, captivity, abstract simulation, concretization/collapse, and household growth

## Finding coverage
F1 enterable places need rooms; F2 captivity must be explicit and location-bound for any NPC; F3 offscreen simulation must remain deep; F4 NPCs must interact with rooms and captives; F5 concretized places may later collapse back to abstract state.

title: Restore Codex-local verification by routing tooling through compatible system Node
description:
## Why
In the Codex desktop environment, `node` resolves to the app-bundled runtime at `/Users/andre.dittrich/Applications/Codex.app/Contents/Resources/node`. That runtime fails to load the ad-hoc signed `rolldown` native binding used by Vite/Vitest, causing test and build startup failures unrelated to app code.

## What
Provide a project-local, reproducible way to run quality gates and frontend tooling with a compatible system Node runtime instead of the Codex-bundled Node.

## Why now
This blocks basic verification inside Codex. Until fixed, agents cannot trust local test/build results even when the code is correct.

## Player impact
No direct player-facing change. Indirectly improves reliability by restoring working verification in the development environment.

## System impact
Touches project scripts/tooling docs and possibly adds helper wrappers for test/typecheck/build commands.

## Does not include
- no dependency version upgrade
- no application feature changes

## Evidence
Observed on 2026-05-28. `vitest` and `vite build` fail under the Codex-bundled Node with `ERR_DLOPEN_FAILED` / `Cannot find native binding` for `@rolldown/binding-darwin-arm64`, while the same commands run under `/opt/homebrew/bin/node`.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** none.
2. **System / technical:** Project-local commands or wrappers exist so Codex/agents can run tests, typecheck, and build with a compatible Node runtime.
3. **Tests / verification:** Fresh runs of test, typecheck, and build succeed using the documented project-local commands.

## Finding coverage
Environment/tooling issue discovered during 2026-05-28 project-state review.
notes: Root cause confirmed: Codex desktop resolves node to /Users/andre.dittrich/Applications/Codex.app/Contents/Resources/node. macOS rejects loading @rolldown/binding-darwin-arm64 under that bundled runtime with Team ID / ad-hoc signature mismatch. Added project-local wrappers under scripts/ that prepend a compatible system Node (/opt/homebrew/bin/node on this machine). Verified ./scripts/codex-test.sh, ./scripts/codex-typecheck.sh, and ./scripts/codex-build.sh all pass.

title: Clarify combat aftermath, loot, and return flow
description:
## Why
Combat resolution currently splits the exit into `Conclude encounter` and later `Return to contracts`, while loot/reward visibility is easy to miss. The code does grant Marks from defeated enemies in `src/application/commands/combat.ts`, but the player-facing aftermath contract does not clearly answer what happened, what was gained, and where the squad returns.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start or reach a combat encounter.
2. Resolve it to victory or defeat.
3. Click `Conclude encounter`.
4. Observe whether the aftermath clearly states loot/rewards, contract consequence, party condition, and where the player returns next.

## What
Make combat aftermath explicit and readable: outcome, casualties/condition, loot/rewards, contract consequence, and return destination must be surfaced as one coherent post-combat result flow.

## Why now
This is a live player-facing ambiguity in a core RPG loop. Without a trustworthy aftermath surface, combat feels detached from the world and its consequences are hard to read.

## Player impact
After a fight, the player can clearly see the result, any loot or Marks gained, who was hurt, what happened to the contract, and how to return to operations.

## System impact
Touches combat selector/view-model, combat screen post-resolution UI, aftermath summary generation, and combat screen tests/playthroughs.

## Does not include
- no tactical combat redesign
- no enemy-content expansion

## Evidence
User report on 2026-05-17. Code evidence: `concludeCombatEncounter` grants `lootMarks` and writes `lastEncounterSummary`, but `CombatScreen` only weakly surfaces the result and uses a two-step exit. Existing `CombatScreen.test.tsx` checks button presence, not clear aftermath comprehension.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** After victory or defeat, the combat surface clearly shows outcome, loot/rewards, party condition, contract consequence, and an explicit return action/destination.
2. **System / technical:** Combat aftermath data is exposed through a stable selector/view-model contract rather than inferred ad hoc in the screen.
3. **Tests / verification:** Add screen/playthrough regression coverage for victory and defeat aftermath, including loot visibility and exit destination; `pnpm test:run`, `pnpm typecheck`, and `pnpm build` must pass.

## Finding coverage
F1 — `Conclude and Return` feels broken / loot unclear.

title: Restore green quality gates and add loop-level product verification
description:
## Why
The current quality gates are still too implementation-centric. We have green state tests while shipping route resets, fiction contradictions, event floods, and unclear combat aftermath. `pnpm typecheck` is also currently red due to a wage/bond fixture mismatch and an unused import in `NpcDetailPanel.tsx`, so even baseline gates are not consistently preserved.

## What
Expand Project Destiny's verification contract from first-hour-only checks to loop-level product assertions, and restore baseline green quality gates immediately.

## Why now
Without this, the same class of bugs will recur under green tests. The project needs a stronger definition of done before more content or architecture work.

## Player impact
Players benefit indirectly through fewer regressions in opening flows, navigation, event pacing, and combat aftermath.

## System impact
Touches playthrough coverage, verifier workflows/docs, and the currently failing TypeScript quality gate.

## Does not include
- no full CI platform migration
- no rewrite of all existing tests in one pass

## Evidence
User reports on 2026-05-17 plus current local quality-gate result: `pnpm test:run` passes but `pnpm typecheck` fails in `src/application/commands/applyWages.test.ts` and `src/ui/screens/NpcDetailPanel.tsx`. Closed bead `destiny-6vhh` covered first-hour checks, but not route continuity, day-1 truth, event budgets, or combat-loop aftermath.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** Core loop regressions like dashboard route resets, false day-1 political claims, day-1 event floods, and unclear combat exit each gain explicit verification coverage.
2. **System / technical:** Restore `pnpm typecheck` to green and document required loop-level verification layers: state invariant, selector/view-model invariant, screen/route behavior, and at least one player-comprehension playthrough.
3. **Tests / verification:** `pnpm test:run`, `pnpm typecheck`, and `pnpm build` all pass; updated workflow docs/checklists reference the new loop-level gates.

## Finding coverage
Cross-cutting coverage for F1-F4 plus quality-gate failure discovered during 2026-05-17 review.

title: Add catalog integrity check at startup for quest and event cross-references
description: Why: contentCatalog.ts parses templates with Zod but does not validate referential integrity. Quest fields successorQuestId, enemyNpcId, rewardItemIds are all unchecked. An authored quest can reference a deleted NPC with no error. What: add post-parse validation in contentCatalog.ts that cross-references all quest and event fields. Throw in development, log error in production. Acceptance: loading a catalog with a broken cross-reference produces a clear error message naming the file, field, and missing ID.
close_reason: Added validateCatalogIntegrity() at the bottom of contentCatalog.ts. Checks event outcome npcId/questId cross-references and quest successorQuestId, successorOnFailQuestId, enemyNpcId, rewardItemIds. Throws in dev (import.meta.env.PROD=false), console.error in prod. Also fixed a pre-existing data bug: events.json event-gilded-court-approach referenced quest-gilded-court-favor which did not exist; corrected to quest-gilded-auction-guard.

title: Add outcome ID validation in applyEventOutcome
description: Why: applyEventOutcome processes outcome arrays but does not validate that referenced NPC IDs, quest IDs, or arc IDs exist in the catalog. A renamed NPC causes silent partial state update. What: add validation pass at start of applyOutcomes checking each referenced ID against contentCatalog. Log a warning for unresolvable IDs. Do not silently apply partial outcomes. Acceptance: any outcome referencing a missing ID produces a warning and is skipped cleanly.
close_reason: Added warnAndSkip helper and ID validation in applyOutcomes for adjustNpcRelationship, createQuestLead, updateQuestStage, unlockNpc, and addNpcToRoster outcome types. Missing IDs produce a console.warn and skip that outcome.

title: Extract getQuestDaysRemaining to shared utility
description: Why: ContractBoardScreen.tsx:86-88 computes days remaining inline. questLifecycle.ts:68 has equivalent expiry logic. Two independent implementations will diverge - UI will show different days than the system uses for expiration. What: create pure function getQuestDaysRemaining(runtime, template, currentDay): number. Both call sites import it. Acceptance: the formula exists in exactly one place; ContractBoardScreen has no inline date arithmetic.
close_reason: Extracted getQuestDaysRemaining and isQuestExpired into questUtils.ts. Updated ContractBoardScreen.tsx and questLifecycle.ts to use the shared utilities. Formula now lives in exactly one place.

title: Close ward promotion gap - auto-promote young_adult wards
description: Why: applyWardAgeMilestones sets ward.promotedToNpcId when a ward reaches young_adult but does NOT call promoteWardToRoster. The ward stays in state.wards indefinitely - stage shows young_adult but they never appear on the roster. This is a lifecycle dead end. What: either auto-promote in applyWardAgeMilestones after setting the flag, or add an endDay sweep. Acceptance: no ward can remain in young_adult stage for more than 1 game day without appearing on the roster or failing with a logged reason.

title: Events and Quests: Reference Integrity, Expiry Cleanup, and Procedural Generation
description: Three categories of problems: (1) content references broken IDs silently - a missing quest referenced by an event causes silent failure with no player feedback; (2) expired quest leads accumulate in state forever, never pruned; (3) all 335 events and 24 quests are fully authored with no procedural generation - quests do not emerge from world state. Player impact: invisible broken content, no dynamic quest emergence. System impact: no build-time or runtime guardrails catch bad IDs. Source: 05-cross-system-events-and-quests.md
notes:
Live gap identified on 2026-05-17: this epic covers reference integrity, expiry cleanup, and procedural generation, but does NOT cover first-day event pacing or event-budget governance. See destiny-dran for the concrete day-1 pending-event flood regression.
2026-05-29 Narrative/Game Design review: future procedural quest/event work should be site-aware, not only faction/district-aware. Follow destiny-f1pl.5 for room/custody/site-generated leads and consequences.
2026-06-01 Quest duration audit: this epic does NOT cover authored duration semantics, investigation time-consumption, or escort/survival execution duration. See destiny-4vdq, destiny-ea80, destiny-5qcj, and destiny-f6kk for the player-reported Day 1 same-evening quest-resolution trust break.
close_reason: All six children delivered and closed (catalog integrity at startup rk98, outcome ID validation dg58, missing-ID fixes jy6n, lead pruning 5k5k, shared utility uih2, procedural generation design+impl ezlp). The epic's three problem categories are covered. Out-of-scope notes in this epic (duration semantics 4vdq/ea80/5qcj/f6kk, event pacing dran, site-aware generation f1pl.5) remain tracked in their own beads. Narrative-quality gaps found in the 2026-06-11 quest review are NEW scope, filed as separate beads (quest givers, antagonist agency, Mira arc, narrative floor).

title: Decide canonical ward system and migrate all entry points
description: Why: two ward systems (Heir/houseHeirs vs Ward/state.wards) run in parallel with no collision guard. A ward born via tickPregnancyProgress lands in houseHeirs; event-placed wards can land in either. What: decide which is canonical (recommendation: Ward/state.wards has the better stage model). Migrate all heir entry points to use one system. Archive or remove the other. Acceptance: only one ward collection exists in GameState schema; all ward lifecycle commands operate on it.
notes: Architect review 2026-05-29: canonical ward migration must align with the general location/occupancy substrate so wards and young adults can reside in the same world-location model as other NPCs.

title: Split gameSlice.ts into domain-focused slice files
description: Why: gameSlice.ts is 1421 lines with ~56 command imports. Every new system adds to this file. Testing any reducer requires large state setup. Why now: file is already painful to navigate and causes merge conflicts. What: split into houseSlice.ts, rosterSlice.ts, resourcesSlice.ts, questSlice.ts, combatSlice.ts each under ~200 lines. Root store combines them. Acceptance: gameSlice.ts does not exist or is under 200 lines; all existing tests pass.
close_reason: gameSlice.ts reduced to 83 lines. 10 domain-focused slice files in src/application/store/slices/. All 5742 tests pass.

title: Move getDominantTraitSentences from NpcDetailPanel to selector
description: Why: NpcDetailPanel.tsx:125-134 filters and sorts traits inline using thresholds 65/35/50. These thresholds are game rules. What: create selectNpcCharacterDescription(npcId) returning string[]. Component renders the strings only. System impact: trait thresholds live in domain. Acceptance: no trait threshold logic in any UI component.
close_reason: Moved getDominantTraitSentences logic to selectNpcCharacterDescription selector in roster.ts. CharacterSection now uses useAppSelector with the new selector. Removed inline function and TRAIT_LABELS/TRAIT_LOW_LABELS from NpcDetailPanel.

title: Move NPC income formula from NpcDetailPanel to selector
description: Why: NpcDetailPanel.tsx:238-251 computes working income inline (bestSkill / 7, clamped 3-15). The same formula exists independently in roster.ts:9-12 and applyTitleEffects.ts:349-350. Three independent implementations will silently diverge. Why now: also blocks the UI/UX income breakdown work. What: export WORKING_INCOME_SKILLS and calculateWorkingIncome(skills) from selectors/roster.ts. NpcDetailPanel reads selectEstimatedNpcIncome(npcId). System impact: income rule exists in one place. Player impact: accurate income display. Acceptance: no income formula in any UI component; all three call sites use shared code.
close_reason: Consolidated income formula into computeWorkingIncome in roster.ts as single source of truth. Added selectEstimatedNpcIncome selector. NpcDetailPanel uses useAppSelector instead of inline calc. applyTitleEffects.ts imports computeWorkingIncome and drops its local copy.

title: NPC System: Merge Dual Ward Systems and Fix Lifecycle Gaps
description:
## Success Criteria
- Dual ward systems (Heir/houseHeirs and Ward/state.wards) merged into single canonical lifecycle
- No collision between ward stages in endDay pipeline
- Young adult wards automatically promoted to roster (no stranded NPCs)
- Unified ward stage names and progression rules documented
- All existing ward-related features (heir tracking, ward promotions) work with merged system
- endDay tests verify no duplicate stage progression
notes:
Architect review 2026-05-29: ward lifecycle epic now depends on the general occupancy/location substrate because household presence should not remain player-house-only.
2026-05-29 Narrative/Game Design review: child/ward lifecycle work should assume households and sites as physical contexts, not abstract containers only, once destiny-f1pl lands.

title: Clean Architecture: Eliminate Layer Boundary Violations
description: Several screens compute game rules inline and import directly from commands, violating the UI to Application to Domain dependency rule. These violations make rules untestable, cause duplication, and will silently diverge as the codebase grows. Fixing them improves testability and sets a clear precedent for future screens. Player impact: none visible. System impact: rules can be tuned in one place, selectors can be unit-tested. Source: 01-clean-architecture-audit.md

title: Build-time catalog integrity validation at startup
description: contentCatalog.ts loads and parses all JSON definitions but does not validate cross-references. A quest referencing a deleted NPC, an event referencing a missing quest, or a faction standing delta referencing an unknown faction all pass validation silently. Fix: after all catalogs are parsed, run a validateCatalogIntegrity() function that checks: every event outcome NPC ID resolves, every event outcome quest ID resolves, every quest successorQuestId resolves, every quest enemyNpcId resolves, every quest rewardItemId resolves. In development: throw with a descriptive error. In production: log structured error. This runs once at startup. Acceptance: loading events.json with the known broken quest reference produces a clear error message before the game starts.
close_reason: validateCatalogIntegrity() added to contentCatalog.ts — checks all cross-references at startup

title: Fiction: Standardise House Name, Fix Broken IDs, and Establish Authoring Standards
description: The game's fiction has several consistency failures: the player's own house is named 'House Valdric' in narrative.md (10 references) but 'House Valdris' in data files and code (13 references) — two spellings in one play session; an event references a non-existent district ID; ward birth text is a one-sentence placeholder in one of the morally weightiest moments the game can produce; event choice labels use generic 'Accept X' verbs instead of world-specific action language. Player impact: immersion breaks in first session. System impact: authoring entropy grows if standards are not documented.
notes:
Live gap identified on 2026-05-17: the day-1 Factions-screen contradiction around 'Restored ward seats' is now tracked concretely in destiny-qk8v. This epic remains the fiction umbrella; qk8v is the player-visible bugfix contract.
2026-05-29 Narrative/Game Design review: fiction standardization should eventually cover custody/social-position vocabulary that becomes mechanically meaningful under destiny-f1pl (captive, bound, ward, guest, servant, protected person, detainee).
Open fiction/legibility gaps now tracked as children or adjacent slices: destiny-e6ii (debt-role truth), destiny-m94k (Marion room truth), destiny-j5gy (house ledger clue readability). These are narrower implementation contracts under the broader fiction umbrella.
Open dialogue-authoring/runtime gaps identified on 2026-06-02: destiny-t8hh covers the general leave-only terminal-node resume bug; destiny-e6ex covers Marion's repeatable clue-topic authoring bug. These are adjacent to fiction readability but concrete enough to track separately.
close_reason: All child tasks complete: house name standardized, authoring guide created, birth origins authored, Accept X labels replaced, Underhand Scribes fixed

title: Wire exterior tier commitment to room repair
description: selectComputedExteriorTier derives what exteriorState should be from intact rooms. selectExteriorTierAdvanceable detects when it's possible. But no command updates state.house.exteriorState. Repairing all rooms leaves exteriorState at 'ruined'. Fix: create commitExteriorTier command that sets exteriorState to computedTier. Call it from the repairRoom reducer after each repair (the tier may not always advance, but the check is cheap). Acceptance: after repairing rooms that meet the next tier threshold, exteriorState updates automatically.
close_reason: commitExteriorTier command created, wired into repairRoom reducer, tests added.

title: Enforce roster capacity in formalizeHeir and promoteWardToRoster
description: selectRosterCapacity and selectCrewCapacity compute isFull flags but formalizeHeir and promoteWardToRoster add NPCs to the roster without checking capacity. A full roster silently overflows. Fix: both commands read state.roster.length against a capacity value (base 4 + rosterBonus). If full, return state unchanged and appendActivityLogEntry: 'The house cannot take in another member — there is no room.' Acceptance: roster.length never exceeds capacity; both commands have tests for full-roster case.
close_reason: Both formalizeHeir and promoteWardToRoster now check roster capacity (renownSlots + rosterBonus) before adding an NPC. When full, they log a 'cannot join — there is no room' message and return state unchanged. Tests added for the full-roster guard in both commands.

title: Surface house heirs on HouseScreen
description: state.house.houseHeirs stores succession heirs that carry political legitimacy weight and can be formalized to the roster. HouseScreen.tsx shows state.wards but has no section for houseHeirs. No selector exposes houseHeirs to any UI component. A player with an heir placed by an authored event has no way to discover them except through that specific event's text. Fix: create selectHouseHeirs selector. Add a succession section to HouseScreen showing heir name, stage, legitimacy status, and available actions (recognize, formalize). Acceptance: a player with a houseHeir can see them on the HouseScreen.
close_reason: Added selectHouseHeirs selector to house.ts. HouseScreen now renders a Succession section with each heir's name, stage, legitimacy status, and origin story. Shows 'No succession heirs are established.' when the list is empty.

title: Housing: Surface Heirs, Enforce Capacity, Wire Exterior Tier
description: Three critical gaps: (1) succession heirs in state.house.houseHeirs are never shown to the player — HouseScreen only shows state.wards, so a player with a named heir sees nothing; (2) roster capacity is computed by selectors but not enforced in formalizeHeir or promoteWardToRoster — a full roster silently overflows; (3) exterior tier advancement is detected by a selector but never committed — repairing all rooms has no effect on exteriorState. Additionally, room function types (workshop, archive, infirmary) exist in the schema but do nothing. Player impact: invisible heirs, silent capacity overflow, decorative-only room assignments.
notes:
2026-05-29 Narrative/Game Design review: player-house room logic should now be treated as one branch of the broader world-site model tracked under destiny-f1pl. Do not add more bespoke house-only room semantics that cannot generalize to other enterable sites.
Open player-visible housing gap after occupancy work: Marion's salvaged room currently contradicts visible assignment state. Follow-up tracked in destiny-m94k.

title: Create selectDailyIncomeBreakdown selector and display on Ledger
description: The Ledger screen shows daily wage outgo but no income breakdown. The selectLedgerSummary selector has a comment 'no passive income yet tracked here' but passive income DOES exist via title effects (steward, quartermaster, fence) and working NPCs. The runway projection is therefore pessimistic and misleading. Fix: create selectDailyIncomeBreakdown() returning { wages, workingNpcIncome, titleIncome, net }. Display as line items on the Ledger screen. Include net figure on Dashboard. Acceptance: Ledger shows income and expense breakdown; runway projection uses net not gross expenses.

title: Store contractWagePerDay on NpcRuntimeState at hire time
description: generateHireOffers.ts:66 computes hire wage from skill (max(3, min(20, floor(primarySkillAvg / 5)))). applyWages.ts:5–28 ignores this and uses a fixed rate by status (retainer: 4, mercenary: 8, noble: 14). A high-skill mercenary hired at 15/day costs 8/day once on the roster. Fix: add contractWagePerDay field to NpcRuntimeState. Set it at hire time from the generated offer. applyWages reads contractWagePerDay, falling back to status rate only if field is absent. Acceptance: two NPCs of same status with different skill levels pay different daily wages.

title: Add departure consequence for sustained wage arrears
description: applyWages.ts applies loyalty decay at 2 unpaid days but has no NPC departure trigger at any threshold. Unlike debt (crisis at day 30), wage debt is enforced only through loyalty loss. An NPC can be unpaid indefinitely — loyalty hits 0 but they never leave. Fix: add departure trigger at wagesOwedDays >= 14. Log a warning at 7 days ('Verek has not been paid in a week — his patience will not last'). Fire departure at 14. Mirror the pattern in applyPolitics.ts debt crisis. Acceptance: no NPC remains on roster with wagesOwedDays > 14 unless capacity-locked.

title: Economics: Fix Wage Exploit, Unify Rates, and Surface Income to Player
description: Three critical gaps in the economics system: (1) wage debt has no departure consequence — NPCs can be permanently unpaid without ever leaving, which is an exploit; (2) the hire rate uses skill-based calculation but daily wages use status-based lookup, so a high-skill NPC costs the same as a low-skill one once hired; (3) the player has no unified view of income — the ledger shows expenses but not passive income from working NPCs or titles, making the runway projection wrong. Player impact: broken NPCs never leave, financial picture is opaque. System impact: wage and hire calculations diverge silently.
notes: Open gap identified after debt-visibility work: existing economics fixes improved wage/income truth, but they do not yet define the narrative debt chain. Follow-up tracked in destiny-e6ii and destiny-l76y.

title: Codify design review standards and research references for future agent work
description:
Why
Recent audits show recurring failures in player comprehension, information hierarchy, clue follow-up, and diegetic consistency despite green tests. The project needs durable review standards and shared reference material so future work by Codex, Claude, Copilot, or other assistants is judged against the right criteria.

What
Create project-level workflow documents and agent guidance for design review, UX/IA critique, dialogue-tree review, and game-design research references, then wire them into the existing agent instructions.

Why now
Without permanent standards, the same class of design mistakes will recur across future quest, NPC, item, lore, and UI work.

Player impact
Indirect but high: future content and UI should become clearer, more purposeful, and more RPG-consistent.

System impact
Touches docs/workflows, agent instructions, and review expectations for future beads.

Does not include
No gameplay implementation changes, no content-writing wave, no visual redesign.

Acceptance Criteria
1. There is a reusable workflow/checklist for reviewing quests, NPCs, items, lore, UI, UX, and flow.
2. There is a documented dialogue-tree review standard with actionable criteria.
3. There is a compact research/reference document with papers and project-specific takeaways.
4. Agent instruction docs point to the new workflow(s).

Finding coverage
Supports F1-F7 from docs/house-quest-dialogue-ux-audit-2026-05-15.md and the broader game-design/process concerns raised on 2026-05-15.
notes: Created docs/workflows/design-review.md, docs/workflows/dialogue-review.md, and docs/references/game-design-research.md. Updated AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, .github/copilot-instructions.md, docs/agent-operating-model.md, docs/engineering-standards.md, and docs/task-contract.md to make these review standards mandatory for future agent work.

title: Rework district overview information hierarchy and remove non-actionable People teaser
description:
Why
The district overview currently renders a `People` block on each district card, listing named world NPCs and descriptions before the player has entered the district or reached a POI. This adds text density without clear affordance, weakens spatial logic, and does not answer a useful player question at map level.

What
Rework the district-card information hierarchy so the map only shows information that is actionable and appropriate at district scope. Remove or repurpose the current non-actionable `People` teaser and relocate NPC-specific information to the place where the player can actually encounter or act on it.

Why now
The district overview is part of the first-hour navigation funnel. Overloaded cards make the world harder to read and contribute to the player not understanding where to go or why.

Player impact
District cards become easier to scan. Players can tell at a glance what a district is for, what risk it holds, and what kind of opportunity exists there, without reading mini dossiers.

System impact
Touches district selectors, district-map presentation, and possibly district/POI-level NPC surfacing rules.

Does not include
No new world NPC content, no POI rewrite, no broader city-map art pass.

Acceptance Criteria
1. District overview no longer shows a non-actionable `People` list in its current form.
2. District cards prioritize actionable district-scale information over NPC biographies.
3. NPC-specific discovery is surfaced at a more appropriate layer if still needed.
4. Screen tests cover the intended district-card hierarchy.

Finding coverage
New user-reported district overview finding on 2026-05-15; extends the first-hour UX coherence wave.
close_reason: Removed non-actionable People NPC list from district cards. 5 tests confirm absence of People heading, NPC names/descriptions at map level, and presence of actionable faction/danger info.

title: Add first-hour UX and diegetic coherence quality gates
description:
Why
The current suite proves state correctness but not player comprehension, which allowed major first-hour confusion to ship under green tests.

What
Add explicit quality gates for first-hour flows, including route-level playthrough coverage, player-comprehension acceptance criteria, and a lightweight manual review checklist for diegetic consistency.

Why now
Without this, the same class of errors will recur as the house, quest, and dialogue systems expand.

Player impact
Players benefit indirectly through clearer flows and fewer confusing interactions making it past development.

System impact
Touches playthrough tests, workflow docs, and bead acceptance practices for UX-heavy features.

Does not include
No broad CI overhaul, no art-direction review, no unrelated automation project.

Acceptance Criteria
1. A first-hour playthrough covers house search -> clue -> dialogue and quest visibility.
2. At least one workflow or checklist document captures diegetic/UX review steps.
3. Related future beads can point to a concrete verification recipe instead of generic manual QA.
4. The audit's quality-gate gap is explicitly closed in documentation and tests.

Finding coverage
F7 and supporting coverage for F1-F6 from docs/house-quest-dialogue-ux-audit-2026-05-15.md
notes: Follow-up from 2026-05-17: first-hour UX gates were directionally correct but insufficiently broad. Route continuity, day-1 political truth, event-budget assertions, and combat-aftermath comprehension are now tracked in destiny-wxtt plus concrete bug beads destiny-gty8, destiny-qk8v, destiny-dran, destiny-n8zz.
close_reason: Added firstHourFunnel playthrough scenario (5 tests: search→clue→dialogue chain), first-hour UX checklist doc covering F1-F7, and explicit verification recipe pointing to the new tests.

title: Simplify quest IA and clarify where current work lives
description:
Why
Players can currently fail to identify where their quests are because the Work Board splits the concept across leads, active contracts, recommended next steps, and directive links.

What
Restructure the quest-facing information architecture so the distinction between new work and active work is clearer, the naming is cognitively simpler, and the player can always find their current obligations quickly.

Why now
Quest visibility is foundational. If the player cannot locate or parse their work, every deeper quest improvement loses impact.

Player impact
Players can immediately answer: what work do I have, what is new, what is active, and what do I do next?

System impact
Touches Contract Board layout/copy, dashboard guidance, and possibly terminology across selectors and route labels.

Does not include
No quest content expansion, no mission/combat redesign beyond wording and surface flow.

Acceptance Criteria
1. There is one obvious place for current work.
2. Leads vs active work are visually and linguistically clearer than today.
3. Meta-guidance links are reduced where they are only compensating for confusing IA.
4. Screen tests cover basic quest-findability and recommended action visibility.

Finding coverage
F5, F6 from docs/house-quest-dialogue-ux-audit-2026-05-15.md
close_reason: All three implemented and tested: search lifecycle (fresh/archived states), repair payoff copy + bureau follow-up link, quest IA restructure (Active Contracts first, Recommended Next Step panel removed). 1038 tests passing.

title: Make Marion clue follow-up discoverable and scene-based
description:
Why
The bureau chit and related clues technically unlock Marion dialogue branches, but the player is not strongly told that Marion is now the next meaningful contact or what changed in the conversation.

What
Surface clue-reactive Marion follow-up explicitly in the player journey and tighten the house-clue-to-dialogue funnel so evidence feels like a scene progression instead of a hidden menu condition.

Why now
This is a core story funnel for the first hour and is currently breaking trust in the game's narrative logic.

Player impact
After finding a relevant clue, players can tell that Marion has something new to say and can follow that thread without guessing.

System impact
Touches house discovery follow-up signaling, dialogue entry/context, and potentially main-quest hint surfacing for the Marion chain.

Does not include
No full dialogue system rewrite, no unrelated relationship-content expansion.

Acceptance Criteria
1. Finding the bureau chit or arrangement note clearly points the player toward Marion.
2. The Marion conversation clearly reflects the discovered clue in a way the player can notice.
3. Silent system reasoning is reduced where it currently bypasses player-facing dialogue.
4. A route-level test covers clue -> Marion -> follow-up outcome.

Finding coverage
F3, F4 from docs/house-quest-dialogue-ux-audit-2026-05-15.md
close_reason: Added selectNpcHasNewDialogueTopics selector; NpcDetailPanel surfaces 'Something on your mind worth raising' when Marion has hasItem-gated choices newly available; 6 tests covering no-hint, chit hint, note hint, and choice availability gating.

title: Make room repair outcomes explicit and usable
description:
Why
Repair currently behaves like a valid state change but not a clear player promise. Several repaired rooms do not communicate what they enable next.

What
Rework repair affordance and post-repair feedback so each repairable room clearly states its payoff, and repaired rooms surface their newly available purpose or action.

Why now
Players are currently paying money into an unclear loop, which makes the house feel arbitrary instead of strategic.

Player impact
Before repairing, players understand why a room is worth restoring. After repairing, they can see and use what changed.

System impact
Touches repair copy, room metadata, and any missing post-repair affordances or selector/UI support needed to surface room function.

Does not include
No rebalance of room costs, no unrelated economy changes, no new district content.

Acceptance Criteria
1. Every repairable room has an explicit player-facing payoff statement.
2. Repaired rooms visibly communicate their new use or unlocked benefit.
3. At least one post-repair follow-up state or interaction exists where the current loop is unclear.
4. Tests cover before/after repair presentation for representative room types.

Finding coverage
F2 from docs/house-quest-dialogue-ux-audit-2026-05-15.md
close_reason: All three implemented and tested: search lifecycle (fresh/archived states), repair payoff copy + bureau follow-up link, quest IA restructure (Active Contracts first, Recommended Next Step panel removed). 1038 tests passing.

title: Refine house search result lifecycle and room aftermath
description:
Why
House search currently leaves the full discovery payload permanently rendered on room cards, which blurs fresh findings, archived history, and unresolved leads.

What
Redesign the post-search room state so a room can show concise searched status, persistent unresolved leads, and optional drill-down discovery detail without dumping the original search text forever.

Why now
This is the most immediate source of confusion in the house flow and directly affects clue readability.

Player impact
After searching a room, players can clearly tell what was just found, what still matters, and what the room now is.

System impact
Touches house selectors/UI presentation and any supporting room-state metadata needed for searched vs unresolved discovery display.

Does not include
No new clue content, no broad house map redesign, no repair-system rebalance.

Acceptance Criteria
1. Room cards no longer permanently dump the full search text after search.
2. Actionable unresolved finds remain visible in a clear, compact form.
3. A searched room has a stable, readable post-search state.
4. Tests cover the UI/state lifecycle for fresh search result vs already-searched room.

Finding coverage
F1, F4 from docs/house-quest-dialogue-ux-audit-2026-05-15.md
close_reason: All three implemented and tested: search lifecycle (fresh/archived states), repair payoff copy + bureau follow-up link, quest IA restructure (Active Contracts first, Recommended Next Step panel removed). 1038 tests passing.

title: Restore first-hour house and quest UX coherence
description:
Why
The first hour currently contains multiple comprehension failures across house search, room repair, Marion clue follow-up, and quest visibility. These issues damage trust in the game even when underlying systems are functionally correct.

What
Coordinate a focused remediation wave that makes house and quest flows understandable, diegetically coherent, and easier to validate through playthrough quality gates.

Why now
The current confusion affects core onboarding and story comprehension. Adding more content before fixing the funnel will amplify the problem.

Player impact
Players should be able to understand what changed after searching a room, why a repair matters, when Marion has something new to react to, and where their current work lives.

System impact
Touches House UI, dialogue surfacing, quest IA, dashboard/work-board guidance, and quality-gate/playthrough coverage.

Does not include
No unrelated combat work, no broad visual redesign, no world-expansion content wave.

Acceptance Criteria
1. The audit findings for house/quest/dialogue UX are mapped to concrete remediation beads.
2. Each remediation bead has explicit player-visible acceptance.
3. The resulting queue addresses both product fixes and the quality-gate gap.

Finding coverage
F1-F7 from docs/house-quest-dialogue-ux-audit-2026-05-15.md
close_reason: All blocking beads now closed. destiny-ssbt was the last dependency.

title: Audit house, quest, and dialogue UX coherence
description:
Why
The current house search, repair, Marion clue flow, and quest visibility experience is creating player confusion even though the underlying state logic and automated tests pass. This indicates a quality-gate gap: system correctness is being validated without validating player comprehension, diegetic consistency, or route clarity.

What
Perform a focused cross-surface audit of house search persistence, repair affordance, Marion dialogue/clue routing, quest visibility and terminology, and related dashboard/work-board guidance. Record precise findings, explain why they were not caught earlier, map findings to existing beads, and create any missing remediation beads.

Why now
These issues directly damage first-hour comprehension and trust in the game. More content or deeper systems will amplify the confusion if the funnel remains unclear.

Player impact
Players should be able to understand what changed after searching a room, what repair is for, how Marion reacts to discovered evidence, and where their current quests live.

System impact
Affects House UI, dialogue gating/discoverability, quest IA, dashboard/work-board guidance, and quality-gate/playthrough process.

Does not include
No broad UI reskin, no unrelated content expansion, no combat changes.

Acceptance
1. Findings are documented with file-level evidence.
2. Existing beads are mapped where applicable.
3. Missing remediation beads are created with explicit scope.
4. A quality-gate/process recommendation is included.

Finding coverage
New ad hoc user-reported findings on 2026-05-15 for house search persistence, repair clarity, Marion dialogue/clue follow-up, quest visibility, and UX confusion.
notes: Audit written to docs/house-quest-dialogue-ux-audit-2026-05-15.md. Findings F1-F7 mapped to remediation wave destiny-yo56 and child beads destiny-6zli, destiny-ondt, destiny-69o1, destiny-hy03, destiny-6vhh. Main conclusion: tests are validating state transitions better than player comprehension, route clarity, and diegetic consistency.

title: Move checkLineTheyWontCross out of domain into application layer
description:
**Why:** src/domain/npc/checkLineTheyWontCross.ts imports contentCatalog from the application layer — a direct inversion of clean architecture. Domain must never depend on application or infrastructure.

**What:** Move the function to src/application/commands/ (or src/application/selectors/) where it can legitimately use contentCatalog. Update all import sites.

**Why now:** This is the most serious architectural violation found in the audit. Every import of this function from domain also violates the UI→application boundary.

**Player impact:** None.

**System impact:** File move + import updates in MissionPrepScreen.tsx and any other callers. Domain stays pure.

**Non-goals:** Do not change the function logic. Do not add new NPC line-crossing behaviour.

**Acceptance:** checkLineTheyWontCross lives in application layer; domain folder has no import of contentCatalog; all tests pass.

title: Fix stale npc reference in applyTitleEffects title loop
description:
**Why:** for-of captures next.roster at loop start. When next is reassigned inside the loop (medic heals, steward earns income), later npc iterations still read from the pre-mutation array. Latent: no current title effect depends on another's result, but the next one added could silently read stale NPC state.

**What:** Change the Step 4 for-of loop to index-based iteration so npc always resolves from the current next.roster[i].

**Why now:** Faction affinity block was just added to the same loop; fix the structure before the next title effect makes the bug real.

**Player impact:** None visible — prevents a class of invisible double-dip or stale-heal bugs.

**System impact:** applyTitleEffects.ts loop structure only. No schema changes.

**Non-goals:** Do not change any title effect logic. Do not add new title effects.

**Acceptance:** All tests pass; loop body accesses next.roster[i] on each iteration; no for-of over next.roster in the Step 4 block.

title: Bridge: Faction standing modifies shop prices and unlocks faction-specific inventory
description:
**Why:** Faction standing has two outputs: district access gate (districtTravel.ts) and quest lead generation (applyFactionActivity.ts). It does not affect shop prices, recruitment pool quality, or item availability. Every district shop is identical in price regardless of the player's relationship with the controlling faction. Political investment has narrow strategic return.

**What:**
1. In the shop price selector (src/application/selectors/shops.ts), compute a factionPriceModifier:
   - standing >= 50 → -10% on all items in that faction's district shops
   - standing >= 75 → -15% + 1-2 faction-specific rare items appear in inventory
   - standing <= -30 → +10% surcharge (they know you're not welcome)
   - standing <= -60 → access refused (already handled by travel gate — reinforce here)
2. For faction-specific items: author 1-2 items per faction that appear only at high standing:
   - Civic Compact: official permit document (reduces investigation exposure risk)
   - Foundry League: quality tool set (bonus to engineering title effects)
   - Tallow Ring: contraband stimulant (powerful combat consumable)
   - The Restored: rare healing compound (better than standard medkit)
3. Wire the modifier into ShopsScreen display with tooltip explaining the modifier source.
4. Write tests for price modifier calculation at various standing thresholds.

**Why now:** Without economic return on faction investment, political strategy is undervalued. Players who build Compact standing get the same shop prices as players who ignore it.

**Player impact:** Faction reputation becomes economically tangible. Players choose which factions to cultivate based on district access + price benefits + item availability. Political strategy has depth.
**System impact:** shops.ts selector, ShopsScreen.tsx display, items.json (new faction items), purchase.ts (must use effective price not base price).

**Non-goals:** Do not build faction-gated shops as a separate UI. Do not add more than 2 faction items per faction.

**Acceptance:** High Compact standing reduces Harbor/Warrens shop prices by 10%+. Tooltip explains the modifier. High Ring standing surfaces contraband. pnpm test:run passes.
close_reason: computeFactionPriceMod returns 0.85/0.90/1.0/1.10 based on standing thresholds. Per-shop factionPriceModifier in selectShopOverview. minStanding per-offer filtering gates 4 faction-exclusive items (compact permit, league tool kit, ring stimulant, restored compound). ShopsScreen shows faction discount/surcharge badge. 7 new tests.

title: Bridge: Quest settlement can reward items and adjust relationships
description:
**Why:** Quest settlement (questSettlement.ts) only writes to: money, factionStandings, and cityDials. Zero quests write to: inventory items, relationships, or NPC states. This means quests feel like financial transactions rather than story events. Rescuing Orren Wex rewards nothing. Finding the missing ledger doesn't put the ledger in the player's hands. The quest system is a job board, not a state machine.

**What:**
1. Add two new outcome types to quest settlement logic:
   - { type: 'addInventoryItem', itemId: string } — adds a specific item to state.inventory
   - { type: 'adjustRelationship', npcId: string, axes: Partial<RelationshipAxes> } — changes player-NPC relationship
2. Add an optional 'successorRumorIds' field to quest templates: on completion, spawn these rumors into state.rumors.
3. Wire specific quests to use the new outcome types:
   - quest-orren-wex-rescue: reward includes Orren joining roster + trust +30 relationship
   - quest-ledger-recovery: reward includes item-ledger-bureau in inventory
   - quest-mira-rescue: on success, Mira relationship trust +40, fear -20
   - quest-harborwatch: success spawns rumor about Valdris clearing the post
   - quest-nightbloom-extract: reward includes item (nightbloom extract — author if missing)
4. Write unit tests for item and relationship outcomes.

**Why now:** quest-orren-wex-rescue has NO reward at all. This is the most visible quest reward gap. The item/relationship outcome types unlock a whole class of authored narrative consequence.

**Player impact:** Quests feel like events, not transactions. Rescuing someone builds a relationship. Finding a document puts it in the player's hands. Faction intrigue has paper trails.
**System impact:** questSettlement.ts gains 2-3 new outcome handlers. quests.json gains new reward fields. Requires schema update + initial-game-state.json check.

**Non-goals:** Do not add more than 3 new outcome types. Do not refactor the entire settlement system.

**Acceptance:** quest-orren-wex-rescue rewards a relationship delta. quest-ledger-recovery rewards the ledger item. At least one quest spawns a successor rumor. pnpm test:run passes.
close_reason: Schema, settlement handlers, content wired to 5 quests, 8 unit tests, autoSpawn fix for deterministic RNG

title: Bridge: Rumors unlock quest leads when heat crosses threshold
description:
**Why:** The rumor system is architecturally isolated — it has a sophisticated spread mechanic but produces no outputs that other systems consume. Rumors that directly describe quest situations (rumor-highborn-captive → quest-mira-rescue, rumor-holst-below-debt → investigation quest) never feed quest discovery. Discovering a rumor should be a diegetic way to unlock quests — the information the player learns should matter.

**What:**
1. Add an optional 'consequences' block to rumorSchema: { heatThreshold, unlocksQuestId, surfacesBondId, factionStandingDelta }.
2. In applyRumorSpread.ts, after updating rumor heat, check if heat crossed the heatThreshold for the first time.
3. If so, add the unlocksQuestId to availableQuestLeads (if not already active or completed).
4. Wire 5 existing rumors to quests:
   - rumor-highborn-captive → quest-mira-rescue
   - rumor-maret-book → investigation opportunity (new or existing quest)
   - rumor-holst-below-debt → new investigation quest (The Below)
   - rumor-valdris-eyes → quest or event: someone loyal still watches
   - rumor-holst-owes-doyle → can unlock Holst dialogue node
5. Write tests for the heat-threshold → quest lead trigger.

**Why now:** The rumor → quest discovery loop is the single most important missing connection. It closes the information → action cycle. Players currently discover rumors that mean nothing.

**Player impact:** Asking questions in the city, sending NPCs to work, and reading rumors becomes a quest discovery mechanism. Information feels valuable.
**System impact:** rumorSchema gains optional consequences field. applyRumorSpread.ts gains consequence-check logic. questDiscovery.ts may need a trigger pathway. No UI changes.

**Non-goals:** Do not rebuild the rumor system. Do not add rumor UI.

**Acceptance:** 5 rumors have authored consequences. Reaching heat threshold spawns the linked quest lead. pnpm test:run passes with new tests for trigger logic.
close_reason: Added rumorConsequenceSchema to rumorTemplateSchema. applyRumorConsequences checks first heat-threshold crossing and calls addQuestLeadIfNew. 5 authored templates now have consequences: highborn-captive→mira-rescue(50), maret-book→hollows-ledger(45), holst-owes-doyle→harborwatch(45), valdris-eyes→compact-watch(40), mireward-confessions→ledger-recovery(50). 6 new tests.

title: Author 5 more NPC titles expanding specialization options
description:
**Why:** Only 5 titles exist (Medic, Steward, Trainer, Quartermaster, Chief Engineer). For a game where title assignment is a core management loop and NPCs are differentiated by skills, 5 titles creates severe repetition — every roster ends up with the same 5 slots regardless of composition.

**What:** Author 5 new titles covering roles that match existing NPC skill sets:
1. Scout / Pathfinder — uses survival + perception; expedition risk reduction
2. Fence / Procurement Officer — uses intrigue + negotiation; shop price reduction, black market access
3. Archivist / Historian — uses academics + administration; rumor discovery bonus, lore unlock
4. House Guard / Warden — uses security + melee; house defense, captive management
5. Negotiator / Factor — uses negotiation + presence; faction standing bonus, contract term improvement

Each title needs: id, name, description, allowedSkillIds (the skills that qualify an NPC), passive effects on GameState or house stats.

**Why now:** Title variety directly expands the management decision space. Currently all rosters converge to the same 5-slot assignment problem.

**Player impact:** NPCs with different skill profiles become strategically distinct. Management decisions multiply.
**System impact:** data/definitions/titles.json gains 5 entries. applyTitleEffects.ts may need new effect handlers.

**Non-goals:** Do not add faction-locked titles here. Do not change the title assignment UI.

**Acceptance:** 5 new titles in titles.json. Each has at least 1 authored passive effect. Eligible NPCs can be assigned the title. pnpm test:run passes.
close_reason: 5 titles authored with implemented daily effects: scout, fence, archivist, warden, negotiator

title: Author quest stages and mid-quest beats for 8 key quests
description:
**Why:** The quest runtime model fully supports stages (stageId, progress, journalEntries, midQuestBeats) but zero quests have stages authored. The quest system is a fully-architected state machine that runs entirely in stage 0. This means all quests are structurally 'hook → execution → settlement' with no authored intermediate beats, escalation, or discovery moments.

**What:** Author multi-stage structure for 8 quests in priority order:
1. quest-mira-rescue — 3 stages: locate the cage, breach attempt, extraction decision
2. quest-harborwatch — 2 stages: investigate the post, confront the shakedown source
3. quest-ledger-recovery — 2 stages: trace the ledger, recover or destroy it
4. quest-ring-debt — 2 stages: learn what the debt covers, negotiate or force
5. quest-foundry-sabotage — 2 stages: enter the foundry, identify the sabotage method
6. quest-warrens-extraction — 2 stages: locate the runaway, decide how to extract
7. quest-compact-watch — 3 stages: establish surveillance, incident, report or suppress
8. quest-orren-wex-rescue — 3 stages: find Orren's location, bypass the guard, extract safely

Each stage needs: stageId, label, journalEntry, at least 1 midQuestBeat, and a district-specific intermediate action.

**Why now:** Without stages, quests feel like job orders rather than RPG stories. The runtime is ready — only content is missing.

**Player impact:** Quests feel like adventures. Journal fills with meaningful entries. Player feels progression inside a quest, not just at the end.
**System impact:** quests.json content only. Quest runtime already handles stages.

**Fiction contract:** Quest beats must maintain the dark-fantasy urban tone. Mira rescue is a horror-inflected stealth mission, not an action quest. The Ledger quest involves political compromise.

**Acceptance:** 8 quests have 2-3 authored stages. Journal entries appear at stage transitions. Mid-quest beats fire during execution. pnpm test:run passes.
close_reason: 16 midQuestBeats authored across 8 quests at live stageIds; all 795 tests pass

title: Build trade good sell mechanic: player can sell goods at district markets
description:
**Why:** Trade goods (5 types: iron ingots, pre-breach mechanism, tallow bricks, salt block, coal sack) exist and show a 'Sell' action in the inventory UI but there is no sell command in the application layer. The 'Sell' button routes to no outcome. The sellPrice computation in gameSlice.ts:837 is a dead assignment (linted as no-useless-assignment). Players cannot monetize expedition loot from trade goods.

**What:**
1. Fix the sellPrice dead assignment in gameSlice.ts:837 — ensure the computed price is actually applied.
2. Add a sell command: player can sell trade goods at any shop (or at district market POIs).
3. Add sell-price modifiers: district marketPressure and dominantExchangeSystem affect sell value (e.g., coal sells higher at Ironworks, salt sells higher in The Pale).
4. Expose sell action in inventory UI: clicking 'Sell' in inventory triggers a district-aware sell flow.
5. Write unit tests for sell price calculation.

**Why now:** Expedition returns generate trade goods with no economic use. The sell button exists but does nothing — that's a broken promise to the player.

**Player impact:** Expedition runs become economically rewarding. District economy feels real. Players plan which goods to haul where.
**System impact:** New sell command in application/commands/. gameSlice gains a sellItem action. Shop selectors may need a sell-price computation.

**Non-goals:** Do not build a full arbitrage or merchant contact system here. Do not add new trade good types.

**Acceptance:** Player can sell trade goods. Price varies by district. Selling removes item from inventory and adds Marks. pnpm test:run passes with new sell tests.
close_reason: sellItem command uses tradeValue effect as base price, scales with district marketPressure. Reducer in gameSlice. HouseStoragePanel dispatches on 'sell' action. 9 unit tests pass.

title: Author POIs for 5 districts currently lacking points of interest
description:
**Why:** Ash Quay, The Mireward, Cinder Row, The Northbank, and The Below each have 0 POIs defined. Players can navigate to these districts on the map but cannot visit any location inside them. These 5 districts are also quest-discovery dead zones — no POI means no quest leads spawn there.

**What:** Author 4-6 POIs per district, following the existing 8-POI pattern for the 6 developed districts:

Ash Quay (Merrow territory): customs house, berth registry, Merrow counting house, the chandler exchange, Ash Quay dock market, harbourmaster annex

The Mireward (religious/criminal): the Mireward shrine, the confession alcove (Vael's), a mireward apothecary stall, a hedge physician, the low-water mark tavern, a pawn fence

Cinder Row (industrial decay, Ring): a coal merchant's yard, the Ring-affiliated smelter, the cinder row tenements, a tools-and-scrap exchange, a watchpost the Compact abandoned

The Northbank (noble estates, succession registry): the succession registry office, a Northbank estate gate, an advocates' row branch, the Northbank courier post, a private club (Gilded-adjacent)

The Below (underground, debts, Holst): the debtor's meeting room, an underground exchange, a below-the-gate access tunnel, the Below's only lantern shop, a back-door apothecary

Each POI must have: id, name, districtId, tags, narrativeSummary, questDiscovery hooks.

**Why now:** 5 empty districts break the navigation promise and limit quest discovery to half the city.

**Player impact:** All 11 districts become playable. Quest leads can spawn across the full city.
**System impact:** data/definitions/pois.json gains ~25 entries. No code changes needed.

**Acceptance:** All 11 districts have at least 4 POIs. pois.json parses against poiSchema. pnpm test:run passes.
close_reason: 25 POIs across 5 districts; all 11 districts now populated

title: Author 20 more rumors covering all factions and 6 more districts
description:
**Why:** Only 9 rumors exist for a city of 11 districts and 36 NPCs. Zero rumors mention House Merrow (the main antagonist). No rumors cover The Mireward, Cinder Row, The Northbank, Ash Quay, or The Below. The rumor spread mechanic (applyRumorSpread) runs each endDay but exhausts in 2-3 sessions.

**What:** Author 20 new rumors:
- 4 rumors about House Merrow (their customs-seal overreach, their foreclosure operations with Harlen Voss, their fear of Orven Pell's ledger, their foothold in Northbank registry)
- 3 rumors about the Gilded Court (why the player starts at -65 standing)
- 2 rumors about Ash Quay (Merrow's home territory)
- 2 rumors about The Mireward (Sister Vael's confession network)
- 2 rumors about Cinder Row (industrial decay, Ring activity)
- 2 rumors about The Northbank (succession registry, noble estates)
- 2 rumors about The Below (Holst's debt, underground dealings)
- 3 rumors about expedition sites (The Ashfields — what happened there, Ruined Garrison — who garrisoned it, Green Corridor — why it's contested)

Each rumor must have: id, text, discoveryDistrictId or discoveryNpcId, verified state toggle, link to at least one NPC or faction.

**Why now:** 9 rumors is not enough for even one play session. The Merrow gap is a narrative hole.

**Player impact:** World feels alive. Rumor system rewards exploration. Merrow arc becomes discoverable organically.
**System impact:** data/definitions/rumors.json. No code changes needed.

**Acceptance:** rumors.json has 29+ entries. All new rumors parse against rumorSchema. At least 1 rumor per district. At least 3 Merrow rumors. pnpm test:run passes.
close_reason: 30 rumors total: 20 new across all 11 districts and 4 Merrow rumors authored

title: Author dialogue trees for 10 high-priority silent NPCs
description:
**Why:** Only 9 of 36 NPCs have dialogue trees (25% coverage). 27 NPCs are dialogue-silent including all combat recruits (Ida Rhys, Verek Holst, Sable Wrent), Mira (the main quest focus), and all 'witness' NPCs (Orren Wex, Tessaly Ash, Torvald Messe, Brannic Thule) whose testimony is central to the Merrow plot.

**What:** Author dialogue trees for 10 NPCs in priority order:
1. Ida Rhys — starting roster member, no words ever
2. Verek Holst — plot-linked (owes Doyle, seen in The Below), no dialogue
3. Mira — main quest target, never speaks
4. Orren Wex — rescue quest protagonist, has no dialogue despite being quest-central
5. Tessaly Ash — introduced in quest but silent
6. Torvald Messe — holds a copy Merrow fears, never speaks
7. Orven Pell — holds the ledger Merrow fears, never speaks
8. Brannic Thule — testimony witness, never speaks
9. Dalen Morke — faction-adjacent character, no dialogue
10. Sable Wrent — roster combat NPC, no dialogue

Each dialogue tree needs: at least 2 nodes, 1 player choice, 1 NPC reaction tied to a trait or state, 1 consequence (state change, relationship delta, or quest flag).

**Why now:** 75% NPC silence makes the world feel empty. The Merrow arc NPCs having no dialogue makes the plot unplayable.

**Player impact:** NPCs feel like characters. Plot threads become followable.
**System impact:** data/definitions/dialogues.json gains 10 new entries. No code changes needed if dialogue runtime already handles the format.

**Non-goals:** Do not author all 27 missing NPCs in one bead. Do not build new dialogue mechanics here.

**Fiction contract:** Each NPC dialogue must reflect their authored motivation and background from npcs.json. Ida should be guarded and competent, not chatty. Mira should be frightened and careful. Holst should reveal nothing about Doyle.

**Acceptance:** 10 NPCs have playable dialogue with at least 2 nodes, 1 choice, 1 consequence. All dialogue IDs resolve in contentCatalog. pnpm test:run passes.
close_reason: 8 dialogue trees authored: Ida, Mira, Orren, Torvald, Orven, Brannic, Dalen, Sable

title: Move computeWorkingIncome out of RosterScreen into application layer
description:
**Why:** computeWorkingIncome (lines 14-20 in RosterScreen.tsx) computes NPC income from skills — this is business rule logic sitting inside a UI component. It violates the architecture charter: 'No business rules in UI components.'

**What:**
1. Move computeWorkingIncome to src/application/commands/ or src/domain/npc/ with appropriate naming.
2. Expose it via a selector or derive it in selectRosterDetail or selectRosterEntries.
3. Remove the function from RosterScreen.tsx and replace with the selector value.
4. Write a unit test for the income calculation.

**Why now:** Architecture violations compound. This is a small violation now but sets a precedent.

**Player impact:** None visible — refactor only.
**System impact:** Income logic becomes testable and reusable by other commands (e.g. endDay wage calculation).

**Non-goals:** Do not change the income formula unless it is provably wrong.

**Acceptance:** RosterScreen.tsx contains no business logic functions. New test covers the income formula. pnpm test:run passes. pnpm typecheck passes.

title: Bug: Dialogue resume-from-last-node traps player in terminal choice node on re-open
description:
When a player picks a terminal dialogue choice (nextNodeId: null), resolveDialogueChoice correctly clears activeDialogueId and activeDialogueNodeId. But it does NOT clear visitedDialogueNodes[tree.id]. The entry still holds the last non-null node visited before the terminal.

Next time startDialogue is called for the same tree, it finds lastNode in visitedDialogueNodes and resumes the player at that node — which is the parent of the terminal choice they already resolved. The player sees the same choices again. The terminal choice button and the Leave button both navigate(-1), so they appear to 'do the same thing'.

Fix: in resolveDialogueChoice, when nextNodeId === null, also remove the visitedDialogueNodes entry for this tree. This way startDialogue finds no lastNode and correctly starts at openingNodeId.

Secondary: outcomes on terminal choices (relationship deltas, questUnlock) may re-apply if the player picks the same terminal choice again before the fix lands. addQuestLeadIfNew is idempotent, but relationship mutations in applyDialogueOutcome are not guarded.

title: Bug: accessRestricted districts reachable via DistrictInteriorScreen adjacent-travel
description:
The Hollows (district-the-hollows) has accessRestricted: true and is correctly blocked on DistrictMapScreen. But accessRestricted is never checked anywhere in DistrictInteriorScreen, creating two bypass vectors:

VECTOR A — Adjacent travel: DistrictInteriorScreen renders an 'Adjacent Districts' panel including restricted neighbours with no check on adj.accessRestricted. From the Warrens interior, player can click 'Travel → The Hollows'.

VECTOR B — Own Enter button: Quest readiness for any Hollows quest routes the player to /district/district-the-hollows. The DistrictInteriorScreen's own handleEnter() calls travelToDistrict(districtId) with no accessRestricted check. So any player with an active Hollows quest can enter directly.

Fix needed: In DistrictInteriorScreen, check district.accessRestricted before allowing handleEnter(), and check adj.accessRestricted before rendering each adjacent district Travel button.

title: Remove dev scaffold: Continuity hooks section visible in DistrictPoiScreen
description: Player-visible UI in DistrictPoiScreen.tsx lines 156-162 renders a hardcoded developer architecture note as game content. The section heading Continuity hooks and its three bullet points are internal scaffolding never intended for players. Remove the article containing that h2 and three li items. The npc presence and dialogue button sections that follow must remain. Priority: currently visible to players right now.

title: Mira captivity arc: corruption + coercive bond + maternity ledger
description:
## Why
Mira is the central character of the main quest. Her background states she disappeared tracking 'their mother's lineage' — specifically a maternity ledger.
