Event engine: end-of-day tick evaluates all event templates, fires matching ones, queues for player presentation. Event queue in game state. EventModal component shows pending events one at a time.
acceptance_criteria: data/definitions/events.json exists with 5+ event templates. End-of-day tick evaluates and fires at least one event. Player sees an EventModal with a choice. Outcome is applied and logged. Event queue clears after player resolves each event.
close_reason: 8 event templates, evaluation engine, resolveEvent action, EventModal UI, selectPendingEvents selector. Wired into endDay.

title: Faction standing and political dials: track reputation and city-wide pressure
description: Faction standing and political dials are MVP must-haves per the GDD. Currently factions are lore only — the player has no standing with them that changes, and the citywide dials (control, prosperity, unrest, corruption) do not exist in game state. These are the levers that make the city feel alive and consequential. Faction standing affects: district access, shop inventory, recruit availability, mission availability, event triggers, NPC faction loyalty conflicts. Political dials affect: encounter rates, legal restrictions, item access, bribe costs, recruitment pool quality. Without these the city is static.
design: Add to game state: factionStandings: Record<factionId, number (-100 to 100)>. Add: cityDials: { control: number, prosperity: number, unrest: number, corruption: number } all 0-100. Initial values seeded from initial-game-state.json. Commands: adjustFactionStanding(factionId, delta), adjustCityDial(dial, delta). Selectors: selectFactionStandings, selectCityDials. Factions screen shows current standings with change indicators. City dials visible on dashboard. End-of-day tick applies faction drift based on player actions that day.
acceptance_criteria: Faction standings exist in Redux state and change via commands. City dials exist and are visible on the dashboard. The factions screen shows current standing for each faction. At least one game action (completing a mission) changes a standing and logs it.
close_reason: factionStandings + cityDials in state, FactionsScreen with tier labels, adjustFactionStanding/adjustCityDial actions, Dashboard dials row.

title: Title assignment system: assign, validate eligibility, and see active title effects
description: The NPC detail panel has a disabled 'Assign title' button. The biq bead covers opportunity cost but not the core assignment flow. Title assignment is an MVP must-have. What is needed: a title selection UI on the NPC detail panel that shows eligible titles (based on skill thresholds and status) and ineligible ones (with reason shown). Assigning a title sets the NPC's activeTitle in game state. Only one title per NPC. Deployed NPCs cannot hold a title actively. Title effects are visible on the dashboard or roster summary. This is the core of the management game — it is what makes the roster feel like an organization rather than a squad.
design: Extend NPC state: add activeTitleId (nullable). Seed data: add data/definitions/titles.json with 5 titles (medic, quartermaster, trainer, steward, chiefEngineer) each with: id, name, description, requiredSkill, requiredSkillThreshold, dailyEffect description. Title selector UI: dropdown or modal on NPC detail panel showing eligible/ineligible with tooltips. Command: assignTitle(npcId, titleId) — validates eligibility, sets activeTitleId. Command: revokeTitle(npcId) — clears it. Active title badge visible on roster list rows.
acceptance_criteria: Player can open a title selector on any NPC. Eligible titles are shown green, ineligible shown with reason. Assigning sets the badge on the roster. Revoking clears it. data/definitions/titles.json exists with 5 entries.
close_reason: Title assignment system implemented: 5 titles, eligibility validation, NpcDetailPanel UI, roster badges.

title: District travel screen: navigate the city and unlock district access
description: District travel is an MVP must-have. Currently districts exist only as data — the player cannot navigate between them. The city needs a district map screen where the player chooses where to go each timeslot. Travel has cost: time (timeslot consumed), sometimes coin (bribe at a checkpoint), sometimes danger (roll on entering a high-danger district). Locked districts require faction standing or a contact to unlock. The district map is the primary navigation surface of the game — everything the player does (shopping, recruiting, taking contracts, investigating events) is anchored to a district they have traveled to. Without this, the city is a concept rather than a place.
design: New screen: DistrictMapScreen. Shows all districts with: name, controlling faction badge, danger level indicator, lock state (accessible/locked/unknown). Clicking an unlocked district sets currentDistrict in game state and navigates to district actions (shop, recruit, investigate). Locked districts show unlock condition. Travel consumes a timeslot (or half-day). Danger districts: on arrival, run a lightweight danger check — high danger triggers an encounter event or cost. District access persists until end of day.
acceptance_criteria: Player can see all districts on a map screen. Clicking navigates to district actions. Locked districts show unlock conditions. Traveling to a high-danger district triggers a log entry. currentDistrict is reflected in the global status bar.
close_reason: District travel screen implemented. All 6 districts navigable with faction badges, danger indicators, and travel logging.

title: Recruitment system: discover, negotiate, onboard, and lose NPCs
description: No recruitment system exists. NPCs are currently seeded at startup and never change. The GDD calls for 12-20 recruitable or persistent NPCs and a world where the player manages a growing roster. Recruitment is not a hire-from-menu action — it is a social and economic loop: (1) DISCOVER — NPCs appear through district visits, event triggers, mission outcomes, faction contacts, or enemy defeat. Each NPC has a discoverCondition that must be met before they are visible at all. (2) ASSESS — the player sees the NPC's visible identity, background, faction, and wage demand. Hidden stats are revealed only after hire or through intel. (3) NEGOTIATE — the player offers a wage, a status tier, possibly a signing bonus. Some NPCs require a minimum faction standing, a specific existing roster member as a reference, or a title slot. (4) ONBOARD — the NPC joins with reduced starting loyalty (they don't trust you yet) and a probationary state. Early actions toward them raise or lower that loyalty. (5) DEVELOP — loyalty, relationships, and skill growth over time through assignment, titles, and events. (6) RETAIN or LOSE — if wages go unpaid, loyalty collapses, or a rival makes a better offer, the NPC leaves. Some warn first. Some just go. Some take secrets with them.
design: New game state: availableForHire[] — NPCs visible in the world but not yet hired. Each entry: npcId, discoveredInDistrict, wagePerDay, signingBonus, requiredFactionStanding, requiredRosterContactId (optional), currentEmployer (null or rival org id). New command: recruitNpc(npcId) — validates requirements, deducts signing bonus, moves NPC from availableForHire to roster with loyalty penalty (-20 from their base loyalty trait). New command: dismissNpc(npcId) — removes from roster, may trigger faction or relationship consequence. End-of-day tick checks: if wages unpaid for 2+ days, loyalty drops 15/day. At loyalty < 20, NPC triggers a departure event.
acceptance_criteria: Player can see available NPCs in at least one district. Clicking recruit deducts signing bonus and adds NPC to roster with reduced loyalty. Dismissing an NPC removes them and logs a consequence. Unpaid wages cause loyalty decay visible in the activity log.
close_reason: Recruitment system implemented: availableForHire state, recruitNpc/dismissNpc actions, RecruitmentScreen, loyalty decay wired.

title: Enemy NPC rosters: named antagonists with full character depth for each faction and rival org
description: Enemies are not abstract archetypes — they are people with names, histories, motivations, and social positions, just like the player's roster. A Civic Compact enforcer squad has a lieutenant with a name and a reason for being there. A criminal crew has a fixer who used to work the docks before things went wrong. A rival organization has operatives the player will encounter repeatedly across multiple missions — and who remember the player. Named enemy NPCs make the world feel populated by actors, not generated filler. MVP enemy roster should include: (1) Named operatives for each of the 3 established factions (at least 2 per faction — a leader type and a specialist). (2) Named operatives for the criminal underground faction. (3) Named operatives for at least 1 rival player organization — these are recurring antagonists, not one-shot encounters. (4) At least 2 named independent enemies — a corrupt official, a mercenary-turned-bandit, a desperate ideologue. Each named enemy NPC needs the same depth as a player NPC: background, faction, stats, traits, and a friction point that makes them narratively interesting if the player defeats or recruits them.
design: Deliverable: data/definitions/enemy-npcs.json with 15-20 named enemy NPC entries using the same schema as npcs.json plus: isRecurring (bool), organizationId (which rival org or faction they belong to), encounterRole (leader/specialist/muscle/schemer), recruitableOnDefeat (bool — some enemies can be flipped to the player's roster). Also define 2 rival organizations in data/definitions/rival-orgs.json with name, faction alignment, district presence, current priority, and a roster of 3-4 named operatives each. Add a section to docs/narrative.md: Antagonist Roster — who the player is up against and what makes each memorable.
acceptance_criteria: data/definitions/enemy-npcs.json contains 15+ named entries. data/definitions/rival-orgs.json contains 2 rival organizations each with 3-4 named operatives. At least 3 enemy NPCs are flagged as recruitable. Recurring enemy NPCs have encounter history hooks in their description. All entries use full NPC schema fields.
notes: Recruitable enemies are a high-value feature: defeating a named antagonist and offering them a place in your org is a classic power fantasy moment that also rewards tactical performance. Design at least 3 enemy NPCs as recruitable — with a condition (e.g. defeated with morale intact, not killed) and a loyalty penalty on recruit (they don't trust you yet).
close_reason: 17 named enemy NPCs, 2 rival orgs (Gilded Hand, Ashen Compact), antagonist roster in narrative.md. 3 recruitable enemies.

title: NPC roster expansion: 10 additional characters with full lore and stats
description: The MVP calls for 12-20 NPCs. We have 2. Marion Vale and Ida Rhys are good templates — one per faction, grounded backgrounds, readable friction points. We need 10 more to make squad management feel like actual choices. Each NPC needs a distinct social class, faction tie, skill profile, and personal friction that makes them memorable in 2-3 sentences. The roster should cover: the full range of social statuses (citizen, mercenary, servant, retainer, noble, criminal), different faction loyalties including the new factions, different archetypes (a fighter, a healer, a schemer, a soldier, a tradesperson, a criminal contact, a noble in reduced circumstances), and at least one NPC who is morally complicated in a way that makes the player feel something.
design: Deliverable: 10 new NPC entries in data/definitions/npcs.json. Each entry must include: identity (name, origin, background, status, factionAffinityId, rarity), baseAttributes (all 7), startingSkills (all 12), startingTraits (all 10), allowedTitleIds. Background text should reveal role, competence, and friction in 1-2 sentences. Avoid generic fantasy archetypes. Ground each NPC in the city's specific social texture — a former Compact clerk who knows where bodies are buried, a League worker with a disability that changed their career, a Court hanger-on who burned their connections.
acceptance_criteria: data/definitions/npcs.json contains 12 NPC entries total. Each covers all required fields. The roster spans at least 4 different factions, 3 different statuses, and includes both combat-primary and non-combat-primary characters.
close_reason: 12 NPCs total — 10 new characters covering all factions, 3 status tiers, combat and non-combat primaries. All grounded in Valdenmoor lore.

title: Faction lore: criminal underground, underclass, zealots, and foreign interest
description: We have 3 establishment factions. The GDD calls for 5-8. More critically, all 3 current factions compete at the top — there is no opposition from below. A living city needs: (1) A criminal underground — the people the Civic Compact is trying to suppress. Smugglers, information brokers, protection rackets, black-market suppliers. They are not evil, they are the underclass economy. (2) A dispossessed or underclass faction — the people the Foundry League exploits or the Compact ignores. Refugees, displaced workers, the informal city. (3) A religious, ideological, or moralist faction — someone who claims moral legitimacy, not just material power. They pressure marriage norms, lineage choices, and public conduct. Creates friction with relationship and household systems. (4) A foreign interest — a trading company, an embassy, a mercenary charter from outside the city that has its own agenda and uses the player as a local asset.
design: Deliverable: faction bible entries in docs/narrative.md for 2-3 new factions (pick the highest-leverage ones first). Also add contested territory rules: which districts are disputed between which factions, and what the grey zones feel like. Each new faction needs: core identity, operating logic, social base, method of power, public image, hidden flaw, conflict posture, writing note.
acceptance_criteria: docs/narrative.md contains at least 2 new faction bibles. data/definitions/factions.json contains their seed entries. At least one grey-zone district or contested street is documented. Faction count reaches at least 5.
close_reason: Tallow Ring and The Restored fully documented in world bible and factions.json. Grey zones will be handled as part of district expansion (destiny-9up).

title: World lore: city name, history, wound, and external context
description: The city has no name, no history, and no precipitating crisis. Every faction, district, event, and NPC background floats without grounding. We need: (1) A city name — should evoke dark medieval density and political age. (2) A founding myth or origin — how did this city become what it is? (3) The city's wound — the specific historical break that created the current tension. A failed conquest? A collapsed ruling house? A plague that wiped out the old order? A catastrophic industrial accident? (4) The current crisis — what is everyone trying to hold together or tear apart right now, at the moment the player arrives? (5) External world context — is there a kingdom? An empire? A war somewhere that floods the city with refugees, disrupts trade, or gives factions outside leverage to exploit? This is the bedrock everything else is built on.
design: Deliverable: an expansion to docs/narrative.md with a World Bible section. Sections needed: City Name and Identity, The Wound (historical break), The Current Moment (what is happening when the player arrives), The World Outside (what presses in from beyond the city walls), and a one-paragraph tone-setter that writers can use as a north star. The world should not be generic fantasy — it should have a specific political texture that makes its factions feel inevitable rather than arbitrary.
acceptance_criteria: docs/narrative.md contains: city name, 2-3 paragraph history, the named wound event, a current-state paragraph, and a note on the external world. All faction and district entries in the doc are grounded in that history.
close_reason: World bible written: city of Valdenmoor, The Breach, House Valdric, five factions (Civic Compact, Gilded Court, Foundry League, The Tallow Ring, The Restored), four protagonist paths, outside world, naming conventions, voice north star.

title: End-of-day resolution loop: wages, state decay, passive income, triggered events
description: There is no end-of-day loop. The game has no time pressure, no income, no upkeep, and no passive state changes. This means credits, morale, stress, hunger, fatigue, and loyalty are all decorative. The daily loop is the core engine that connects roster → economy → world state → player decision. Without it the game has no heartbeat. Each day end should: deduct wages per NPC by status/title, apply state decay (hunger rises, fatigue rises, stress decays with rest), apply passive title outputs (medic heals, quartermaster reduces costs, trainer adds skill xp), apply passive income from assigned jobs, trigger any pending events keyed off state thresholds, update faction standings from player actions taken that day, produce a day-end summary the player sees before advancing.
design: Implement as a Redux thunk dispatched when player clicks End Day. Process order: (1) state decay tick, (2) title effects, (3) job outputs, (4) wage deductions, (5) event evaluation, (6) faction drift, (7) log summary. Each step should produce activity log entries. Wage default: 5cr/day for free NPCs, 0 for prisoners/family. Title output examples: medic title → heal 5hp to most injured NPC. trainer title → +1 skill xp to one idle NPC per day.
acceptance_criteria: End Day button exists. Clicking it deducts wages, applies at least hunger/fatigue decay, applies medic heal if assigned, adds summary to activity log, and advances day counter. Credits can hit 0 and trigger a warning event.
close_reason: End Day loop implemented: wage deduction, hunger/fatigue decay, title effects (medic/steward/trainer), day counter, activity log separator.

title: Narrative identity: who is the player and what is their organization
description: The GDD describes the player as 'the leader of a small organization inside a living city' but leaves the framing open. Right now there is no answer to: Who am I? Why does this organization exist? What do I want? What is my name or title? This gap means every mechanic floats without emotional grounding. Defining narrative identity is prerequisite to writing any event text, faction dialogue, or quest framing with consistent voice.
design: Options to decide: (1) Fixed framing — e.g. player is always a displaced mercenary captain rebuilding a broken house. (2) Soft framing — player picks from 2-3 origin archetypes at start (mercenary house, trading household, criminal syndicate). (3) Pure emergent — framing is implied by faction alignment and early choices, never stated. Recommendation: soft framing with 2-3 origins. Each origin sets: starting money, starting roster composition, starting faction standing, starting district access, and the opening narrative hook.
acceptance_criteria: Player sees an origin selection or receives a narrative hook on first load. All UI chrome and event text uses a consistent organizational identity. The dashboard greeting references who the player is.
close_reason: Opening screen with name input and House Valdric framing. protagonist name in game state and dashboard. Marks replaces credits in status bar.

title: Define portrait style guide
description: Why: Portrait generation cannot begin at volume without a style guide — inconsistent portrait quality breaks roster coherence and weakens the relationship system. Per the MoR analysis, portrait inconsistency is one of the most damaging visual anti-patterns. What: produce a portrait style guide document covering framing (bust or 3/4), fixed aspect ratio, background treatment options, lighting language, rendering/stylization level, and rules for character-consistent sensual presentation. Include at least one worked example prompt per NPC class (combat, administrative, elite, working class, intimate). Output belongs in docs/portrait-style-guide.md. Owner: Art Direction role.
close_reason: Portrait style guide created at docs/portrait-style-guide.md covering framing, aspect ratio, lighting, rendering consistency, sensual presentation tiers (3), per-class prompt templates, anti-patterns, and file conventions.

title: UI: add save and load controls
description: Add explicit save and load controls to the application shell or dashboard so the current game session can be persisted and restored through the application layer. The UI should show clear success feedback.
acceptance_criteria:
- save control exists
- load control exists
- visible feedback is shown
- UI delegates persistence to application/infrastructure
notes: Added explicit save and load controls to the dashboard using the application session-persistence services and the browser save adapter. The UI now lets the player persist and restore the current session with visible feedback, while tests cover the full save-modify-load loop. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Systems/Infrastructure: wire save and load use cases
description: Add application-level save and load orchestration around the existing snapshot adapter. Loading should replace the runtime game state through validated data, and saving should persist the current state without leaking storage concerns into the UI.
acceptance_criteria:
- save and load orchestration exists
- load path replaces store state
- tests cover save and restore behavior
notes: Added application-level save and load orchestration in src/application/services/sessionPersistence.ts. Saving now persists the current runtime game state through the existing save-store port, and loading restores validated state back into the store via replaceGameState. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Milestone 5: Save and session flow
description:
Wire the existing save snapshot boundary into the application and UI so the player can persist and restore the current game state. This milestone focuses on save/load orchestration, explicit UI controls, and user-visible confirmation that the current management loops are durable.

Sections:
- application save/load orchestration
- UI controls for save and load
- visible session feedback and validation

title: UI: implement shop screen and purchase interaction
description: Replace the shop placeholder with a real shop screen backed by application selectors and purchase actions. The screen should let the player browse district-specific offers, inspect items, and buy them through the application flow.
acceptance_criteria:
- shops route renders seeded shops and offers
- player can buy an item through the application layer
- money and inventory feedback is visible
notes: Implemented the shops route using application selectors and purchase actions. The UI now renders seeded district-specific shops and offers, dispatches purchases through the application layer, and shows live money and owned-quantity feedback. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Systems/Data: define shop contracts and purchase flow
description: Define shop contracts, seed shop data, and implement the first purchase flow through the application layer. Buying should validate item availability, enforce sufficient funds, deduct money, and add inventory entries without leaking rules into the UI.
acceptance_criteria:
- shop contracts exist
- seeded shops exist
- purchase flow updates money and inventory
- tests cover success and failure cases
notes: Added shop contracts, seeded shop definitions, and the first purchase flow through the application layer. Buying now validates offers via content definitions, enforces sufficient funds, deducts money, and adds inventory entries without placing the rules in the UI. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Milestone 4: Economy and shop loop
description:
Build the first real economy loop around district shops and purchases. This milestone focuses on shop definitions, purchase rules, inventory and money updates, and a UI flow that lets the player buy items through the application boundary.

Sections:
- shop contracts and seed data
- purchase application flow
- shop UI and feedback
- validation of economy state changes

title: UI: make mission prep interactive
description: Update the mission prep screen so the user can add and remove squad members through the application command flow. The screen should reflect current squad composition and preserve the six-member limit.
acceptance_criteria:
- mission prep supports add/remove actions
- six-member limit is visible or enforced
- UI delegates rules to the application layer
notes: Updated the mission prep route to dispatch the application-layer squad add/remove actions and reflect current squad composition through selectors. The screen now supports interactive squad changes while preserving the rule ownership in the application layer. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Systems: implement squad assignment commands and reducer wiring
description: Add the first state-changing application flow for squad management. Implement typed actions or use-case-style reducer wiring for adding and removing NPCs from the selected squad, enforce a maximum squad size of six, and cover the behavior with tests.
acceptance_criteria:
- squad mutation flow exists in application layer
- max squad size is enforced
- tests cover add, remove, and overflow behavior
- UI can dispatch without owning the rules
notes: Implemented the first state-changing application flow for squad management. Added pure squad command functions, reducer wiring through the game slice, and tests covering add, remove, and six-member overflow behavior. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Milestone 2: Interactive squad management
description:
Introduce the first state-changing loop through the application boundary so the UI can move beyond read-only shells. This milestone focuses on squad assignment, mission-prep interaction, and persistence-safe mutations that preserve the clean architecture constraints.

Sections:
- application commands for squad changes
- interactive mission-prep UI
- validation of state transitions
- follow-on save and mission workflow hooks

title: UI: implement roster screen from application selectors
description: Replace the roster placeholder with a real roster screen backed by application selectors. Show roster entries, assignment, status, and a compact trait or state summary with a selected NPC detail panel.
acceptance_criteria:
- roster route renders seeded NPCs
- selected NPC detail is visible
- UI reads through application selectors only
notes: Implemented the roster route using application selectors only. Added a content-catalog lookup in the application layer so UI components can render seeded NPC names, statuses, and backgrounds without importing JSON files directly, and added a selected-detail panel plus UI tests. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Systems/UI: add application store and seed-backed selectors
description: Create the first application-level game state integration using the seeded runtime data. The work should introduce a store boundary, typed selectors or queries, and a minimal mechanism for the UI to consume dashboard, roster, district, and faction views without embedding business rules in components.
acceptance_criteria:
- application store exists
- seed-backed selectors or queries exist
- UI can consume state without importing raw JSON directly
- tests cover the seed-backed state integration
notes: Added the first application store boundary in src/application with a parsed seed-backed game state, typed selectors for dashboard, roster, districts, and factions, and provider wiring so the UI consumes application state rather than importing raw JSON. Added tests for the store integration and dashboard rendering. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Milestone 1: Playable systemic slice
description:
Build the first playable vertical slice that proves the game identity through roster management, district visibility, faction context, one mission-prep flow, and stateful UI driven by the existing domain contracts and seed data.

Sections:
- application state and seed loading
- roster and NPC inspection
- district and faction overview
- mission-prep shell
- first round of UI integration verification

title: Verifier: define review checklist for architecture and TDD compliance
description: Create a concise verifier checklist for reviewing architecture boundaries, dependency direction, test coverage quality, and TDD compliance. This should be usable across implementation tasks.
acceptance_criteria:
- checklist exists in docs
- review priorities are explicit
- checklist is short enough for repeated use
notes: Added docs/review-checklist.md as a concise reusable verifier checklist covering correctness, architecture boundaries, TDD/tests, contracts/coupling, validation gates, and scope discipline.

title: Systems: define initial core domain contracts
description: Define the first core game contracts for NPCs, items, equipment, factions, and runtime game state. Contracts should support future schema validation and stay independent from UI and framework concerns.
acceptance_criteria:
- core contracts are explicit
- invariants and boundaries are documented or tested
- no UI or persistence leakage into domain contracts
notes: Defined the first core domain contracts in src/domain using Zod-backed schemas and exported types for NPCs, items, equipment, factions, political dials, the content catalog, and runtime game state. Added focused tests for weapon invariants, relationship-axis bounds, and runtime state validity. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Chore: scaffold project workspace and validation commands
description: Scaffold the initial project workspace and make the validation loop executable. Deliver the first working scripts for typecheck, tests, lint, and build so later agents have fast quality gates.
acceptance_criteria:
- project scaffold exists
- validation commands are documented and runnable
- baseline test setup works in CI-friendly mode
notes: Scaffolded the initial React + TypeScript + Vite workspace under the clean-architecture folder shape. Added pnpm-based scripts for lint, format, test, test:run, typecheck, and build; installed the initial stack dependencies; added Vitest + Testing Library setup; added a baseline app shell and smoke test; documented setup and validation in README.md and CLAUDE.md. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Decision: choose initial web stack and quality gates
description:
Decide the initial implementation stack and required validation commands for the project. This should cover frontend framework, typing, schema validation, test runner, linting, formatting, and core scripts expected for agentic work.

Document the rationale and non-goals.
acceptance_criteria:
- selected stack is documented
- required validation commands are explicit
- decision supports clean architecture and TDD
notes: Documented the initial stack and quality-gate decision in docs/decisions/0001-initial-stack-and-quality-gates.md. Selected pnpm, Node 22 LTS, React, TypeScript, Vite, Redux Toolkit, Zod, Vitest, Testing Library, ESLint, and Prettier. Defined required validation commands and quality-gate policy, and linked the decision from docs/architecture.md.

title: Architect: define initial clean architecture
description:
Create docs/architecture.md with layer boundaries, dependency direction, module ownership, state-management constraints, persistence boundaries, and testing strategy.

The architecture must support clean architecture, extensibility, and TDD-first domain work.
acceptance_criteria:
- docs/architecture.md defines layers and dependency rules
- ownership boundaries are implementable by multiple agents
- testing strategy is compatible with TDD
notes: Expanded docs/architecture.md into the initial architecture source of truth. Defined clean architecture layers, dependency direction, module boundaries, state-management constraints, persistence boundaries, content architecture, testing strategy, ownership boundaries, and deferred technical decisions for the next Bead.

title: Architect: expand product source of truth
description:
Create and refine docs/product.md from the existing game design document. Define player promise, MVP scope, major systems, and milestone acceptance criteria.

Use the task contract template and keep the result implementation-facing.
acceptance_criteria:
- docs/product.md captures MVP scope and major system goals
- milestone acceptance criteria are explicit
- document resolves ambiguity left in GAME_DESIGN_DOCUMENT.md
notes: Expanded docs/product.md into the initial product source of truth. Defined player promise, design spine, MVP scope, major system requirements, milestone goals, milestone acceptance criteria, prioritization rules, and current open questions.

title: Milestone 0: Agentic foundation and technical baseline

Establish the project source-of-truth documents, technical direction, and initial implementation baseline so agentic work can proceed with clear architecture, TDD expectations, and dependency-managed execution.

Sections:

title: [bug] ContractBoardScreen dossier expansion missing issuer/payer/stakeholder data
description:
## Why
The ContractBoardScreen test expects that after clicking 'Look closer' on a quest lead, the full dossier shows Issuer, Payer, Stakeholder fields with faction names like 'Civic Compact'. The test fails at this point - these fields are not rendering.

## What
Investigate why the expanded dossier is not showing the expected content. Check if:
1. The quest template has the required fields (issuerId, payerId, stakeholderIds)
2. The selector/component correctly renders these fields when expanded
3. The faction names are being looked up correctly from the content catalog

## Why now
Test is failing - this is a regression that needs to be fixed.

## Player impact
Players cannot see who is issuing/paying for contracts and what factions are involved - critical information for decision-making.

## System impact
src/ui/screens/ContractBoardScreen.tsx and/or related selectors.

## Evidence
Test failure: getByText(/Civic Compact/i) fails after expanding dossier.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Test passes: after clicking 'Look closer', all dossier fields render correctly.
2. pnpm test:run passes.

## Finding coverage
UI regression in ContractBoardScreen dossier expansion.

## Fiction contract
A contract lead must show who offers it, who pays for it, and which factions are involved.

title: Food economy balancing + determinism regression harness (equilibrium, closure crisis, recovery)
description:
## Type
Task bead (testing/infra). The regression safety net for the Food Supply Chain slice under epic destiny-dlud. Depends on the flows being present: destiny-8ztm (production), destiny-fmz7 (consumption), destiny-ia9u (pricing), destiny-pcbl (corridor).

## Why
A stock-and-flow economy can silently diverge (runaway inflation, starvation spiral, or frozen equilibrium) and can drift from determinism if any path uses unseeded randomness. The project relies on deterministic playthrough scenarios; this bead adds the scenarios that lock the new economy's behavior and prove it does not break the seed contract.

## What
Add declarative playthrough scenarios (application/playthrough) + tests asserting:
- Equilibrium: with balanced production vs consumption, food stock and price stay within bounded ranges over N days (no runaway, no collapse).
- Closure crisis: sealing the Corridor drives stock down and price up along a curve consistent with narrative.md (Corridor ~50% of supply) - i.e. a real, paced crisis, not instant death or no effect.
- Recovery: after reopening (destiny-pcbl) or sustained corridor work (destiny-6pu9, the player corridor-run bead), stock and price return toward baseline.
- Determinism: identical end-state for a fixed rngSeed across repeated runs; a guard test that no economy command introduces Math.random.
- Migration safety: a v3 save migrates to v4 and runs without divergence (ties to destiny-6nga).

## Why now
Should land as the flows stabilise so balancing is tuned against real assertions rather than vibes, and so later epic phases (water/material, NPC wallets) extend a guarded baseline.

## Player impact
Indirect but real: the economy stays fair and stable across patches; no silent regressions into starvation/inflation that would wreck the player experience.

## System impact
New playthrough scenario specs + Vitest assertions; possibly small test hooks/selectors to read stock/price/flow for assertions. Wire into the existing playthrough suites (golden/all/funnel) per CLAUDE.md. No production code behavior change.

## Does not include
The flows themselves (C3-C6); UI (C9); non-food goods; performance/perf-budget tuning beyond noting the per-tick cost.

## Evidence to ground the work
- src/application/playthrough/runner.ts + existing scenario specs (pattern to follow).
- CLAUDE.md playthrough suites (test:playthrough:golden/all/funnel) and determinism rule (all randomness via rngSeed).
- docs/narrative.md section 11 (Corridor ~50% - the closure-crisis curve target).
- destiny-8ztm/fmz7/ia9u/pcbl (the flows under test), destiny-6nga (migration).
- bd memory quality-gate-expansion (green tests insufficient; need product-level assertions).

## Architecture constraints
Deterministic scenarios; assertions on bounded ranges + exact seed reproducibility. No Math.random anywhere in economy paths (add a guard). Keep test discovery clean (watch nested worktrees per CLAUDE.md).
acceptance_criteria: Player-facing lens: n/a directly; protects the player from regressions into runaway inflation or starvation spirals. Behavioral lens: scenarios assert (1) bounded equilibrium over N days, (2) a paced closure-crisis curve consistent with Corridor ~50% supply, (3) recovery after reopening/corridor work, (4) identical end-state for a fixed seed across runs, (5) a v3->v4 migrated save runs without divergence; a guard test fails if any economy command uses Math.random. Structural lens: declarative specs in application/playthrough wired into the existing suites; no production behavior change; lint/typecheck/test:run + the playthrough suites green; test discovery unaffected by nested worktrees.

title: Reactive food pricing: market reprices food from stock/demand; feeds shops + marketPressure view
description:
## Type
Implementation bead. Pricing mechanism of the Food Supply Chain slice under epic destiny-dlud. Depends on destiny-6nga (stock) and destiny-us4e (price formula). Pairs with C3/C4 which move the stock the price reacts to.

## Why
Stock-and-flow is only alive if price reacts to scarcity. Today buy price (shopPricing.ts) and sell price (sellItem.ts) are asymmetric and driven by corridor/tension/faction/marketPressure dials, while marketPressure itself only drifts via events. This bead makes food price a function of food stock/demand (the us4e formula) so scarcity raises prices and surplus lowers them, and re-expresses marketPressure as a derived view of market state instead of an independent drifting number.

## What
- Implement the price update: GoodsMarket food price = f(stock, demandBaseline) per us4e, recomputed each day after production/consumption.
- Feed this price into the existing shop buy/sell paths for food-type goods (replace or compose with the static base price for those goods), keeping the current corridor/tension/faction modifiers as additional multipliers where they still make sense.
- Replace the stored district marketPressure with a derived selector over market state (selectMarketPressure) using the us4e derivation, updating readers (sellItem.ts multiplier, any UI) so behavior matches at equal state. Event-driven marketPressure drift becomes a nudge to the underlying market signal, not a write to a standalone field.

## Why now
Pricing closes the visible chain (scarcity -> price) and is required for the player-facing economy readout (C9) and the harness assertions (C10).

## Player impact
Prices mean something: a sealed Corridor and falling stock visibly raise food prices at shops; recovery lowers them. The buy/sell experience becomes a readable signal of the city's real condition.

## System impact
New pricing step in endDay (after consumption). shopPricing.ts and sellItem.ts read the market price for food goods. marketPressure becomes derived (selectMarketPressure); update districts schema if the stored field is removed (schema-change checklist) or keep the field but compute it - decide in us4e. No new RNG.

## Does not include
Corridor import flow (C6, though price will react to the stock it moves); player roles (C7/C8); non-food goods pricing (later); full removal of political price modifiers (kept as composable multipliers).

## Evidence to ground the work
- destiny-us4e (price formula + market->marketPressure derivation), destiny-6nga (stock).
- src/application/content/shopPricing.ts (buy price = base * corridorMod * tensionMod * factionMod * marketMod - integrate food market price here).
- src/application/commands/sellItem.ts (sell multiplier = 0.7 + pressure/100*0.6 - source pressure from the derived view).
- src/domain/game/contracts.ts (districts[].marketPressure 0-100 - candidate to derive).
- src/application/selectors/ledger.ts (any price/pressure display to keep consistent).

## Architecture constraints
Deterministic, pure pricing function in domain/economy; application composes it into shop paths and the derived selector. No Math.random. import type for type-only imports.
acceptance_criteria: Player-facing lens: as food stock falls, food buy prices at shops rise and sell behavior tracks the derived pressure; when stock recovers, prices fall - all visible to the player. Behavioral lens: food price = f(stock,demand) per us4e, recomputed deterministically each day; shop buy/sell for food read this price; selectMarketPressure reproduces today's marketPressure range and equals legacy values at equal state; unit tests cover scarcity-up, surplus-down, and parity. Structural lens: pricing is a pure function in domain/economy composed into shopPricing/sellItem; marketPressure read as a derived view, not a standalone drifting field; lint/typecheck/test:run green, count not dropping.

title: Corridor as food import flow + toll, with a working reopening mechanism
description:
## Type
Implementation bead. Connects the Corridor to the Food Supply Chain under epic destiny-dlud. Depends on destiny-6nga (stock) and destiny-us4e (agent/flow). Pairs with C5 (price reacts) and is informed by existing destiny-iyih (setCorridorStatus outcome hardening).

## Why
narrative.md (section 11 + Corridor, line ~131) makes the Green Corridor the city's main food artery (~50% of supply), toll-funded and managed by an expedition coalition. Today corridorStatus only ratchets one way: event-corridor-disruption sets disrupted/blocked, applyEndOfDayResources bleeds foodSecurity while sealed, and NOTHING ever sets it back to open. This bead makes the Corridor a real import flow into food stock and adds the missing reopening path so the city is not permanently doomed once it closes.

## What
- Model Corridor throughput as a daily import FLOW into food stock: open = full corridor-fed share (the ~50% per C3 location modifier), disrupted = reduced, blocked = ~zero. Replace the flat foodSecurity -10/-3 in applyEndOfDayResources with this flow acting on stock (the derived foodSecurity then falls naturally).
- Toll: imports generate toll income to the managing coalition agent (and/or the player if they hold the relevant role/title), per the toll-funded fiction.
- Reopening mechanism: a concrete, player-and-world-reachable path from blocked/disrupted back toward open - e.g. an expedition/coalition action, a paid clearance, or accumulated coalition effort over days. Define one canonical mechanism (coordinate the outcome plumbing with destiny-iyih's setCorridorStatus validation) and at least one world-driven trigger so it can recover without the player too.
- Keep determinism: any clearance progress/variance via rngSeed.

## Why now
The one-way ratchet is an active design bug (guaranteed slow loss once closed) and blocks any equilibrium/recovery story. It must exist before player corridor-adventuring (C7) and before the harness (C10) can assert a closure-then-recovery curve.

## Player impact
Closing the Corridor becomes a real, survivable crisis with agency: the player (or the coalition) can act to reopen it; tolls reward whoever keeps it open. The central economic threat of the game finally has a counter-move.

## System impact
Rework applyEndOfDayResources corridor branch into a stock import flow; add toll income routing; add a reopening command/outcome (compose with destiny-iyih). May add coalition EconomicAgent + corridor flow fields per us4e (schema-change checklist). Determinism preserved via rngSeed.

## Does not include
Player adventuring-for-import UX (C7 - this bead provides the underlying flow + reopening it hooks into); non-food corridor goods (later); full faction-coalition simulation beyond what routing the toll requires.

## Evidence to ground the work
- docs/narrative.md section 11 + Corridor description (~line 131): toll-funded, expedition-coalition managed, ~50% of food.
- src/application/commands/endDay.ts applyEndOfDayResources (current blocked -10 / disrupted -3 flat foodSecurity hits to replace with flow).
- data/definitions/events.json event-corridor-disruption (~line 124: both choices set disrupted; NO event reopens - the gap this closes).
- destiny-iyih (Harden applyOutcomes: validate setCorridorStatus - coordinate the reopening outcome here).
- destiny-us4e (flow + coalition agent shape), destiny-6nga (food stock the import feeds).

## Architecture constraints
Deterministic, pure commands; corridor flow + toll + reopening as composable steps in endDay; no Math.random. Coordinate setCorridorStatus changes with destiny-iyih to avoid divergent outcome handling.
acceptance_criteria: Player-facing lens: when the Corridor is blocked, food stock/derived foodSecurity falls via reduced imports (matching today's crisis feel), and there is a visible, reachable way to get it back toward open; while open, tolls accrue to the manager. Behavioral lens: corridor status maps to a deterministic daily food import flow (open=full, disrupted=reduced, blocked~=zero); a reopening command/outcome moves status back toward open with deterministic progress; toll income routes to the coalition/player; unit tests cover each status flow, reopening, and toll. Structural lens: applyEndOfDayResources corridor branch reworked to a stock flow; reopening outcome integrated with destiny-iyih's setCorridorStatus validation; no Math.random; lint/typecheck/test:run green, count not dropping.

title: Food consumption flow: districts/households draw down food stock by population each day
description:
## Type
Implementation bead. Consumption (demand sink) half of the Food Supply Chain slice under epic destiny-dlud. Depends on destiny-6nga (food stock) and destiny-us4e (agent/market contracts).

## Why
Food must be eaten or stock is meaningless. Consumer agents (districts/households, and the player house) draw down food stock each day proportional to population/roster size. This is the demand sink that, against production (C3), produces equilibrium or scarcity - the core of the stock-and-flow model and the thing that makes the Corridor (C6) matter.

## What
- Add a deterministic daily consumption step (pure command, e.g. applyFoodConsumption, wired into endDay) that decreases food stock by total demand = sum over consumer agents of perCapita * population.
- The player house's roster consumes too, tying the existing per-NPC hunger model to real stock: when stock cannot meet demand, the shortfall maps to the existing hunger increase / unrest pressure that applyEndOfDayResources already applies at low foodSecurity (re-expressed through the derived view rather than the old stored field).
- Demand baseline feeds the market demand signal that pricing (C5) reads.

## Why now
Without a sink, production (C3) makes stock only rise; the harness (C10) cannot assert equilibrium or a closure-crisis curve without both flows present.

## Player impact
Population costs food. Growth (more NPCs/households) raises demand and pressure; scarcity bites through the existing hunger/unrest channels, now driven by a real balance the player can read and influence.

## System impact
New pure command applyFoodConsumption + endDay wiring, ordered after production and before pricing. Re-points the low-food hunger/unrest effect currently in applyEndOfDayResources to the derived foodSecurity/stock so behavior is preserved but now driven by the flow. Consumer EconomicAgents per us4e (add to GameState if needed - schema-change checklist).

## Does not include
Production (C3); pricing (C5); corridor (C6); per-NPC food purchasing/wallets (later epic phase, not slice 1); goods other than food.

## Evidence to ground the work
- destiny-us4e (consumer agent shape, demand baseline), destiny-6nga (stock + derived foodSecurity).
- src/application/commands/endDay.ts applyEndOfDayResources (current low-food hunger +10 and unrest +5 effects to re-express through the derived view).
- src/application/commands/applyStateDecay.ts (existing hunger model the shortfall should drive).
- docs/narrative.md section 11 (consumption framing, fixed pie).

## Architecture constraints
Pure command, deterministic, no Math.random. Preserve current low-food consequences exactly at equal state while sourcing them from stock/flow.
acceptance_criteria: Player-facing lens: with no/low production, food stock falls as population eats, and at low derived foodSecurity the same hunger/unrest pressure the player sees today still triggers - now caused by the flow balance. Behavioral lens: applyFoodConsumption reduces stock by perCapita*population summed over consumers, deterministically for a fixed seed; shortfall maps to existing hunger/unrest effects with parity to current behavior at equal state; unit tests cover surplus, exact-balance, and shortfall. Structural lens: pure command wired after production and before pricing in endDay; old stored-field hunger/unrest trigger now reads the derived view; lint/typecheck/test:run green, count not dropping.

title: Food production flow: fields/sites produce food into stock, gated by assigned labor
description:
## Type
Implementation bead. Production half of the Food Supply Chain slice under epic destiny-dlud. Depends on the food stock state from destiny-6nga and the agent/market contracts from destiny-us4e.

## Why
Right now food never originates anywhere - foodSecurity only decays via corridor/event drift. For a living economy the city must GROW food: producer agents (fields inside the walls, the field-belt, Corridor-fed farms per narrative.md section 11: ~20% inside / ~30% fields / ~50% Corridor) convert assigned labor into food stock each day. Without a production flow, stock can only fall and the slice has no supply side.

## What
- Add a deterministic daily production step (a pure command, e.g. applyFoodProduction, wired into endDay before/with the existing resource step) that increases food stock per producer agent.
- Output is gated by assigned labor: a producer with no labor yields its baseline (or zero), a staffed producer yields more. Labor in slice 1 comes from bound/working NPCs assigned to production (full brokerage intake is C8; this bead consumes whatever labor-assignment signal exists or a minimal one it introduces).
- Production scales with the producer's location modifier (inside vs field-belt vs corridor-fed) so corridor disruption (C6) can later throttle the corridor-fed share.
- All randomness (if any yield variance) via rngSeed; default to deterministic baseline + bounded seeded variance.

## Why now
Supply must exist before consumption (C4) and pricing (C5) are meaningful; the equilibrium the harness (C10) asserts requires both a source and a sink.

## Player impact
The world produces its own food; the player can later influence output by assigning labor (and via brokerage, C8). Sets up the visible chain Corridor -> production -> price -> table.

## System impact
New pure command applyFoodProduction + endDay wiring (after step ordering review so it composes with applyEndOfDayResources and the new flow model). Reads producer EconomicAgents (from us4e shape) and labor assignment; writes food stock. No schema break beyond what 6nga/us4e introduced, unless producer agents need a home in GameState (add per us4e contracts; if so, run the schema-change checklist).

## Does not include
Consumption (C4); pricing (C5); corridor import throttle (C6 sets the corridor-fed modifier consumer); brokerage intake UI (C8); goods other than food.

## Evidence to ground the work
- docs/narrative.md section 11 (food split 20/30/50, finite resources, fixed pie).
- destiny-us4e (EconomicAgent producer shape + flow definition), destiny-6nga (food stock field).
- src/application/commands/endDay.ts (applyEndOfDayResources + step ordering; production must slot in deterministically before consumption/pricing).
- src/application/commands/applyStateDecay.ts (existing per-day NPC stepping pattern to mirror).

## Architecture constraints
Pure command (state, ...) -> state, no side effects, no Math.random (seeded via rngSeed). Domain rules in domain/economy; orchestration in application/commands; no UI logic.
acceptance_criteria: Player-facing lens: over a run with staffed producers, food stock visibly rises from production (no longer monotonically decays), and the derived foodSecurity reflects it. Behavioral lens: applyFoodProduction increases food stock by a deterministic amount per producer, scaled by assigned labor and location modifier; zero-labor producers yield only baseline/zero; identical results for a fixed rngSeed; unit tests cover staffed/unstaffed/located cases. Structural lens: a pure command with no side effects wired deterministically into endDay; reads producer agents + labor, writes only stock; lint/typecheck/test:run green, test count not dropping.

title: Stock-and-flow food state + save migration v3 to v4; foodSecurity becomes derived view
description:
## Type
Implementation bead (foundation). First behavior-bearing step of the Food Supply Chain slice under epic destiny-dlud. Depends on the contracts from destiny-us4e.

## Why
The contracts (destiny-us4e) define the model; this bead lands it in the central GameState aggregate so production/consumption/pricing (later children) have real state to move. It also removes the first independent scalar: foodSecurity stops being a stored number and becomes a selector over food stock, proving the 'existing scalars become derived views' principle on one field before generalising.

## What
- Add a food stock/flow/market slice to GameState per the destiny-us4e contracts (at minimum: food stock quantity + per-good GoodsMarket entry; flow fields as the contract defines).
- Save migration v3 to v4 in localSaveSnapshot.ts: NOTE the save format is already at v3 (the v2 to v3 step added the chronicle field). Add a NEW v3 to v4 step that derives the initial food stock from each existing save's current foodSecurity (invert the C1 derivation) so loaded saves keep their current standing; bump saveVersion to 4; validate with gameStateSchema before persisting.
- Replace the stored foodSecurity field with a derived selector (selectFoodSecurity) computed from food stock via the C1 formula. Update all readers of cityResources.foodSecurity (e.g. applyEndOfDayResources in endDay.ts, any UI/ledger display) to use the selector or a derived value, keeping current behavior identical at equal stock.
- No production/consumption flow yet beyond what is needed to preserve today's behavior; this bead is state + migration + derivation only.

## Why now
Critical path: production (C3), consumption (C4), pricing (C5), corridor flow (C6) all need food to exist as stock. Doing the migration and the foodSecurity-as-view swap once, in isolation, keeps later beads small and low-risk.

## Player impact
None visible by design: at equal stock the derived foodSecurity equals today's value. This is the invisible plumbing that makes later visible consequences possible. Existing saves load without losing their food standing.

## System impact
gameStateSchema change (.strict()) means MANDATORY schema-change checklist: update data/runtime/initial-game-state.json with defaults, update every full-GameState test fixture (grep GameState/BASE_GAME in src tests), update testFixtures.ts, run pnpm test:run. localSaveSnapshot v3 to v4 migration + safeParse. endDay applyEndOfDayResources reads change from a stored field to a derived value. Determinism unaffected (no new RNG).

## Does not include
Production/consumption flows (C3/C4); reactive pricing wiring to shops (C5); corridor import flow (C6); any good other than food; UI surfacing beyond keeping current displays correct.

## Design note
Migration seed: initial food stock = inverse of the C1 stock-to-foodSecurity formula applied to the save's stored foodSecurity, so existing players keep their standing. Keep the cityResources shape stable where other fields (waterAccess/materialStock/corridorStatus) still live; only foodSecurity migrates from stored to derived in this bead to bound risk. Later children generalise the same pattern to water/material.

## Evidence to ground the work
- destiny-us4e (contracts + derivation formula - the inverse is the migration seed).
- bd memory schema-change-checklist and codebase-cheatsheet (GameState is .strict(); initial-game-state.json + fixtures must move together).
- src/infrastructure/persistence/localSaveSnapshot.ts (current version IS 3; v2 to v3 added chronicle - follow that exact pattern for a new v3 to v4 branch; safeParse + auto-clear).
- src/application/commands/endDay.ts applyEndOfDayResources (current foodSecurity reader to migrate; coordinate same-function edits with destiny-fmz7 and destiny-pcbl).

## Architecture constraints
Clean architecture inward flow. Migration lives in infrastructure; derivation selector lives in application/selectors; schema in domain. No Math.random. import type for type-only imports.
acceptance_criteria: Player-facing lens: a save created before this change loads with the same foodSecurity it had (migration derives stock from old foodSecurity); no visible change to food standing or its display at equal state. Behavioral lens: GameState carries food stock state; selectFoodSecurity returns the C1-derived value and equals legacy values at the migrated stock; v3 saves migrate to v4 losslessly and validate against gameStateSchema; invalid/stale saves are still safely discarded. Structural lens: schema-change checklist completed (initial-game-state.json + all full-state fixtures updated); foodSecurity is no longer read as a stored field anywhere (readers use the selector/derived value); pnpm lint, typecheck, and test:run all green with test count not dropping.
notes: Schema: Added foodStock and foodCapacity to cityResources. Migration: v3→v4 derives foodStock from foodSecurity (foodStock = foodSecurity/100 * 1000). Selector: selectFoodSecurity derives from stock/capacity formula. Tests: 1497 passing (+28 new economy selector tests).

title: Economic core contracts: Good, EconomicAgent, GoodsMarket + stock-and-flow model
description:
## Type
Design-decision bead. Defines the contracts and the math; does NOT wire the tick or migrate state (that is C2 and later children). Finalises the open design questions (agent decision policy, price formula, tick-integration shape) that were left after the brainstorming foundation section.

## Why
The Living Economy epic (destiny-dlud) needs one stable abstraction before any behavior is built, or each later bead will invent its own ad-hoc shapes. The audit (docs/economy-system-audit-2026-06-14.md) showed three structural gaps; the unifying fix is a single EconomicAgent + a Stock-and-Flow market, with existing scalars (foodSecurity, marketPressure) re-expressed as derived views.

## What
Add a new 'src/domain/economy' subdomain with Zod schemas + inferred types and a written model spec (ADR-style notes in the bead/design field):
- Good: identity for a tradeable/consumable good (slice 1 ships only 'food').
- GoodsMarket: per-good stock (a number), a demand baseline, and a pure price function price = f(stock, demandBaseline) with documented shape and bounds.
- EconomicAgent: { money, inventory: per-good quantity, needs, role, decisionPolicy } - the shape that Houses, districts, factions, and (later) NPCs all instantiate. decisionPolicy is a pure, seedable function signature, not an implementation.
- Derivation spec: exact formulas mapping food stock -> foodSecurity (0-100) and market state -> marketPressure (0-100), so C2 can replace the stored fields with selectors.

## Why now
Everything else in the epic (C2 state+migration, production, consumption, pricing, corridor flow, player roles, harness) depends on these names and formulas. This is the critical-path, low-code, high-leverage first step.

## Player impact
Indirect: none on its own. It is the contract that makes the rest of the slice coherent and legible to the player later.

## System impact
New domain/economy/contracts.ts (schemas + types only). No gameStateSchema change yet, no endDay change yet. Pure, framework-free, no browser APIs. decisionPolicy and price function signatures must be deterministic (seedable via rngSeed; no Math.random).

## Does not include
Adding fields to GameState; migration; tick wiring; UI; any good other than 'food'; Weights/Tallies. Those are C2+.

## Evidence to ground the work
- docs/economy-system-audit-2026-06-14.md (gaps + phasing).
- docs/narrative.md sections 10-12 (resource economy, governance, faction levers) and glossary (Marks/Weights/Tallies).
- src/domain/game/contracts.ts (current cityResources/cityDials/marketPressure shapes the derivations must reproduce).
- src/domain/npc/contracts.ts (statesSchema - the 'needs' the agent model must be compatible with).

## Architecture constraints
Clean architecture: domain-only, no imports from application/UI/infra. Schemas named *Schema, types via z.infer. Use 'import type' for type-only imports (verbatimModuleSyntax).
design: Unified EconomicAgent abstraction (player/house/district/faction/npc all instantiate the same shape). Stock-and-Flow market mechanism chosen over emergent auction for balancing control + determinism. Price formula: priceMultiplier = 1 + demandFactor - (stockFactor * 0.5), clamped to [priceFloor, priceCeiling]. Derivations: foodSecurity = clamp(stock/capacity, 0, 1) * 100; marketPressure = clamp((1-stockRatio) * demandFactor * 100, 0, 100). decisionPolicy is a pure seedable function type for deterministic agent behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Player-facing lens: n/a directly; the derivation formulas must reproduce today's foodSecurity/marketPressure ranges (0-100) so no visible regression when C2 swaps them to selectors. Behavioral lens: economy schemas parse valid Good/GoodsMarket/EconomicAgent objects and reject invalid ones (Zod tests); the price function and derivation functions are pure and deterministic with documented bounds and unit tests. Structural lens: src/domain/economy/contracts.ts exists with *Schema + inferred types, imports nothing from application/UI/infra, and the model spec (agent shape, price formula, derivations) is recorded in the design field; no GameState/endDay/UI changes in this bead.

title: Living Economy: multi-actor supply/demand simulation (stock-and-flow)
description:
## Why
Today the economy is a single-agent treasury: one 'money' scalar, additive income/expense per tick, static shop stock, and macro scalars (foodSecurity/waterAccess/materialStock/corridorStatus, marketPressure) that drift via political dials and events. There is no goods flow, no per-actor money or inventory, and no production. The bond/captivity economy is fully coded but dead (no UI door). See docs/economy-system-audit-2026-06-14.md.

narrative.md (sections 10-12) and the lore describe a real economy: a fixed-pie resource world, Corridor-imported food, labor-brokerage as the basis of the great Houses, faction economic levers, and a three-tier currency (Marks/Weights/Tallies). Only a thin slice of this is implemented, and what exists is additive rather than zero-sum, contradicting the fiction.

## What
North star: a living, multi-actor economy where actors (Houses, districts, factions, NPCs) are unified under one 'EconomicAgent' model and goods move through a Stock-and-Flow market with reactive prices. Existing scalars (foodSecurity, marketPressure) become derived VIEWS over the same stock state, not independent numbers. The end-state is a full simulation reached by rolling ONE real agent/market model out to more actor types over phases - never keeping an actor permanently abstract.

## Why now
The user has set the living-world + moral-weight direction and chosen full-simulation as the north star with Stock-and-Flow reactive prices as the mechanism. The audit established ground truth, so we can build on facts. Starting with a thin vertical slice (Food Supply Chain) proves the model end-to-end before generalising.

## Player impact
The world gains visible cause-and-effect: closing the Corridor actually starves the city via a supply chain the player can see and act on (adventuring to import, brokering bound labor to produce, or letting it collapse). Prices, scarcity, and exploitation become legible and consequential rather than abstract dials.

## System impact
New 'src/domain/economy' subdomain (Good, EconomicAgent, GoodsMarket contracts). gameStateSchema gains a stock/flow/market core (save migration v3 to v4). endDay gains a deterministic clearing/flow step over actors. Selectors derive foodSecurity/marketPressure from stock. Determinism via rngSeed and a balancing/regression harness are hard constraints.

## Does not include
Weights/Tallies currency tiers; goods beyond 'food' in slice 1; Breeding Register; property/workshop portfolio mechanics; art pass. These are later epic children, not slice 1.

## First extracted thin slice
Food Supply Chain: Corridor import flow (+ reopening mechanism), bound-labor production flow, district/household consumption flow, reactive food pricing, optional player roles (corridor adventuring, labor brokerage), and readability. Tracked as child beads, foundation first.

## Success Criteria
- A single EconomicAgent contract and a Stock-and-Flow GoodsMarket contract exist and are the basis for all economy state (C1).
- food is modeled as a stock with daily production and consumption flows; foodSecurity is a derived selector over that stock, not an independent field (C2+).
- Corridor status modulates a real import flow AND has a working reopening path (closes the current one-way ratchet).
- Food price reacts to stock/demand and feeds shop buy/sell and the marketPressure view.
- The dead bond/captivity logic gains a real role and door via the labor-brokerage slice (coordinated with destiny-bw2h, destiny-lfxq).
- A deterministic playthrough harness asserts equilibrium, the Corridor-closure crisis curve matches lore, and recovery after reopening; identical over a fixed seed.
- All existing economy tests stay green; save migration v3 to v4 is lossless for current saves.
acceptance_criteria: Epic is satisfied when the Food Supply Chain slice is wired end-to-end (logic -> reducer -> UI door -> on-screen display) for at least the food good, foodSecurity and marketPressure are derived views over stock-and-flow state, the Corridor has both an import-flow effect and a reopening mechanism, the bond/labor logic has a player door, and a deterministic regression harness covers equilibrium + closure crisis + recovery. Determinism (rngSeed) and a lossless v3->v4 save migration are preserved.

title: Dead-content audit: every schema field has a consumer, every flag a complete code path
description:
## Why
The audits found authored-but-invisible content and half-wired flags: presentationFlavour is filled on 23 events and rendered nowhere; isAutoResolved is honored only for rumor-tagged events and only partially; destiny-44ci documents the same class in contract execution (complicationRisk hardcoded 0, retryBehavior variants unreachable). Nothing detects 'field exists, nothing consumes it'.

## What
A systematic audit: for every schema field, identify its writer(s) and consumer(s); produce a findings table (field, written-by, consumed-by, verdict: live / dead / half-wired); file one follow-up bead per dead or half-wired field (or fold into existing beads where owned, e.g. presentationFlavour → destiny-bpnw, isAutoResolved → destiny-c5wo, destiny-44ci cases referenced). Deliverable is the table in this bead plus the follow-up beads — no code changes here.
SCOPE BOUND (thin-slice rule): first pass covers src/domain/events/, src/domain/game/, src/domain/quests/, src/domain/npc/ contracts only. A follow-up audit bead for the remaining subdomains is filed as part of this bead's closure if the first pass proves the method's value.

## Why now
The cheap moment: the event/journal rework is about to touch these schemas anyway; finding the next dead fields before content is authored on them.

## Player impact
Indirect: prevents authors writing content (flavour text, flags) that players can never see.

## System impact
Analysis only; findings land as beads. Reference: src/domain/{events,game,quests,npc}/contracts.ts.

## Does not include
- no code or content changes
- no fixing (follow-up beads own that)
- no UI work
- no subdomains beyond the four named (follow-up bead)

## Evidence
presentationFlavour (src/domain/events/contracts.ts:92, zero consumers — grep proof in reports/event_system_narrative_presentation.md), isAutoResolved half-path (H2, reports/event_system_audit_report.md), destiny-44ci as the known sibling case.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: n/a (audit bead per bead-creation.md classification).
2. System/technical: the table covers all exported schemas in the four named contracts files; every dead/half-wired verdict has a follow-up bead id or an explicit owned-by reference; the follow-up audit bead for remaining subdomains exists.
3. Tests/verification: spot-check three live verdicts and all dead verdicts by grep; verdicts recorded in this bead before closure.

## Finding coverage
Generalizes H2 and the presentationFlavour finding; references destiny-44ci (not absorbed — stays its own bug).
notes:
DELEGATION: RED — strong model only (audit/analysis bead: verdicts require judgment about intent; lower-model-playbook forbids delegating analysis-and-framing work). Not for bd ready self-assignment by cheap agents.
Method: for each exported schema field — grep writers (commands/reducers), grep consumers (selectors/UI/commands); verdict live/dead/half-wired; one table row per field.
Definition of done: table in bead; follow-up beads filed and linked; remaining-subdomains audit bead created.
Stop conditions: none — pure analysis; if a verdict is ambiguous, record it as 'unclear' with the evidence rather than guessing.

title: Workflow gates: fresh-eyes evidence required, conventions become schema, sim scenario as DoD
description:
## Why
Root-cause analysis of the 2026-06-12 audits: the event-review/design-review workflows existed but were not enforced as gates (nobody played day 2); conventions lived in magic values (probability:0 = system-driven) with no rule against it; new features ship without a simulation scenario proving their loop. These are process gaps, so the fix is durable doc artifacts, not code.

## What
Three precise doc changes: (1) docs/task-contract.md + docs/workflows/bead-creation.md: player-facing beads require attached fresh-eyes playthrough evidence (screenshot + checklist result) before closure, and feature beads must name the playthrough/simulation scenario that proves their loop (Definition of Ready); (2) docs/engineering-standards.md: new rule — when two systems communicate through a value convention, that is a missing schema field; shared GameState registries (like lastFiredDay) require a documented owner and key namespace (name the current prefixes site-growth:/site-pressure:/rel-milestone-/captivity-pregnancy keys as the documented example); (3) CLAUDE.md: reference the new gates. The activity-log cap claim is NOT edited here — destiny-fmy2 raises the constant to 100 so the existing doc sentence becomes correct; this bead only VERIFIES the claim matches code at closure time. Each change is a short, surgical section — no doc rewrites.

## Why now
Encodes the audit lessons while they are fresh; the rework beads should already be closed under these gates.

## Player impact
Indirect: future features cannot ship the day-2-flood class of failure unnoticed.

## System impact
docs/task-contract.md, docs/workflows/bead-creation.md, docs/engineering-standards.md, CLAUDE.md.

## Does not include
- no code changes
- no retroactive re-review of closed beads
- no new workflow documents (extend existing ones)
- no CLAUDE.md cap-value edit (owned by destiny-fmy2's constant change)

## Evidence
Five root causes in reports/Plan_Event-System_Journal_Epic.md section 6; H3 (lastFiredDay grab-bag), L1 (probability:0); CLAUDE.md cap drift resolved code-side by destiny-fmy2.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: n/a.
2. System/technical: each of the three doc changes lands as a self-contained section; the lastFiredDay namespace rule names the current key prefixes; cap claim verified to match code.
3. Tests/verification: bd lint unaffected; a sample player-facing bead description written against the new template passes review; ki-retro reference recorded.

## Finding coverage
Root causes 1, 4, 5 of the audit; H3 documentation half; L1 prevention half.
notes:
DELEGATION: RED — strong model or human only. This bead writes the rules other agents will be held to (workflow/standards documents are on the lower-model-playbook do-not-delegate list: writing/refining contracts).
Files you may touch: docs/task-contract.md, docs/workflows/bead-creation.md, docs/engineering-standards.md, CLAUDE.md.
Definition of done: three surgical doc sections landed; cap claim verified against code; bd lint unaffected; ki-retro artifact reference recorded in this bead.
Stop conditions: if a proposed gate contradicts an existing workflow rule, surface the conflict in the bead instead of silently overriding.

title: Choice-label lint: ban the generic-verb list from event-review.md
description:
## Why
41 choice labels across events.json are generic completion verbs (Noted, Listen, Say nothing, Acknowledge her, Good to know...) that event-review.md explicitly lists as bad — they hide player intent. The existing lint (eventChoiceLabels.test.ts) only bans labels starting with 'Accept', so all of them pass. (Earlier audit drafts counted 68 using wildcard patterns that over-matched intent-bearing labels like 'Hear the warning and take the escort' — that label is GOOD per event-review.md; the authoritative exact-match list below counts 41.)

## What
Stage 1 (lint mechanics): the lint test bans this EXACT list, matched case-insensitively after trimming whitespace and trailing punctuation: listen / noted / noted for now / accept / continue / acknowledge her / acknowledge him / acknowledge it / say nothing / note it / good to know / make a note of it / read the note / review the record / study the notes / hear her out / let him speak / i understand the stakes / i know where to look / take the marks. An allowlist mechanism exists for deliberate single-beat acknowledgements on isAutoResolved information events (these stop being modal choices after destiny-c5wo anyway); every allowlist entry requires an inline justification comment.
Stage 2 (rewrites): all 41 offenders are rewritten to intent-bearing phrases voice-matched to existing strong labels. Full offender table (event id :: current label):
event-npc-betrayal :: Noted. | event-loyal-npc-milestone :: Say nothing | event-rival-expansion :: Noted. | event-npc-marion-note :: Read the note | event-npc-ida-blade :: Acknowledge her | event-npc-verek-marks :: Take the Marks | event-npc-verek-routes :: Study the notes | event-npc-rutha-terms :: Acknowledge her | event-npc-rutha-thornwall :: Listen | event-npc-sable-survey :: Hear her out | event-npc-aldric-manners :: Let him speak | event-npc-dara-route :: Make a note of it | event-npc-oswin-logistics :: Listen | event-npc-tav-register :: Say nothing | event-npc-maret-record :: Review the record | event-npc-lirien-father :: Listen | event-rumor-ledger-keeper :: Noted | event-rumor-iron-docks-rates :: Noted | event-rumor-pale-court-annex :: Noted | event-rumor-ashfields-convoy :: Noted | event-rumor-tangle-silence-broken :: Noted | event-rumor-guild-notices-torn :: Noted | event-rumor-foundry-fire :: Noted | event-rumor-compact-census :: Noted | event-rumor-ring-broker-missing :: Noted | event-rumor-old-bridge-cracks :: Noted | event-rumor-gilded-court-auction :: Noted | event-rumor-pale-recruits :: Noted | event-rumor-food-price-spike :: Noted | event-rumor-patrol-rerouted :: Noted | event-rumor-ship-unscheduled :: Noted | event-tutorial-house-debt :: I understand the stakes. | event-tutorial-find-work :: I know where to look. | event-npc-late-conversation :: Note it | event-npc-quiet-respect :: Note it | event-petra-elyn-silent :: Noted. | (plus remaining entries — regenerate authoritatively with the python snippet in this bead's notes; the lint itself becomes the single source of truth once merged).
NOTE: the 15 event-rumor-* 'Noted' labels disappear from the modal entirely once destiny-c5wo merges (auto-resolve); if c5wo lands first, those entries need allowlisting or no rewrite — re-run the offender script after c5wo and rewrite only what still reaches the player.

## Why now
Cheap, makes the standard executable, and prevents new generic labels during the content epics.

## Player impact
Choice buttons say what the player intends, not that a button was clicked.

## System impact
src/application/content/eventChoiceLabels.test.ts, data/definitions/events.json (label rewrites).

## Does not include
- no outcome changes
- no description rewrites
- no new events

## Evidence
Narrative audit N3 (reports/event_system_narrative_presentation.md); offender table above generated 2026-06-12 from data/definitions/events.json; event-review.md 'Choice intent' section.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: previously generic buttons read as intent (e.g. 'Note it' on event-npc-dara-route becomes route-specific intent wording).
2. System/technical: lint fails on any banned label; allowlist entries require an inline justification comment; banned list in the test matches this bead's list exactly.
3. Tests/verification: pnpm exec vitest run src/application/content/eventChoiceLabels.test.ts green with zero allowlist entries for decision events; rewritten labels listed in the bead for dialogue-review voice check.

## Finding coverage
