Every file there fails with 'No tsconfigRootDir was set, and multiple candidate TSConfigRootDirs are present' — 1611 errors, exit 1. Source files in src/ lint clean when targeted directly. CLAUDE.md already warns about worktree mirrors for test discovery; lint has the same hygiene problem and currently blocks the documented quality gate.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Ensure .claude/worktrees/ contains agent-* directories (present on 2026-06-10).
2. Run pnpm lint → 1611 parsing errors, all under .claude/worktrees/.

## What
Exclude .claude/worktrees/ (and any generated mirrors) from the ESLint flat config ignores, or set tsconfigRootDir explicitly. Verify pnpm lint passes on a clean tree afterwards.

## Why now
The session-completion workflow mandates quality gates; a red lint that nobody reads trains everyone to ignore it.

## Player impact
None directly; protects the quality gate that protects players.

## System impact
eslint config only.

## Non-goals
No rule changes, no fixing lint findings inside the mirrors, no worktree lifecycle management.

## Acceptance Criteria
- pnpm lint exits 0 with stale worktree mirrors present.
- A targeted run on src/ reports the same findings before and after (no rule behavior change).

## Finding coverage
F1 lint traverses worktree mirrors; F2 quality gate red for unrelated reasons.
notes: Parked mid-flight 2026-06-11 (user pivoted to quest review). Approach already scoped: eslint.config.js uses defineConfig with globalIgnores(['dist','coverage','.tmp-vite']) — add '.claude/worktrees' (and consider '.claude') to that list, then verify pnpm lint exits 0 and a targeted run on src/ reports unchanged findings. One-line fix + verification; suitable for a low-tier model with this note as the contract.
close_reason: Excluded .claude/worktrees from ESLint globalIgnores; lint no longer traverses stale agent mirrors

title: District maps: per-district SVG map with clickable POIs, player and NPC markers
description:
## Why
Player request 2026-06-10: districts should be readable as places — one map per district where POIs can be approached directly, with markers for the player and NPCs. Today the district interior is a card list; NPC presence and spatial layout are invisible.

## What
A DistrictMap SVG component rendered on DistrictInteriorScreen: hand-authored POI positions per district (all 74 POIs), POI nodes typed by icon, click navigates to the POI route (same gating as the existing Enter location button), player presence shown when currentDistrictId matches, NPC markers from selectWorldNpcViewsByDistrict placed at their current POI. District-specific texture (coast for Harbor Ward/Ash Quay, river bank, slag canal for Ironworks, hatching for The Below) via per-district theme tokens.

## Why now
Companion slice to destiny-b2oh (city overview map); together they fulfil the player's map request. Geometry data is authored once and reused by both.

## Player impact
The player can see a district as a place, find POIs spatially, see who is there, and enter a location with one click.

## System impact
New src/ui/screens/maps/ geometry modules + components + CSS; DistrictInteriorScreen integration; no domain changes; uses existing selectors.

## Non-goals
No pathfinding or movement animation, no POI-level player position in GameState, no painted raster art (schematic ink-on-parchment style in theme colors).

## Fiction contract
Each district map reads like a page from the house surveyor's folio: ink outlines, labeled landmarks, notes in the margin.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every district renders a map with all of its POIs positioned and labeled; a geometry integrity test fails if a POI lacks coordinates.
- Clicking a POI node while present in the district navigates to that POI screen; while absent it does not navigate and communicates why.
- World/story NPCs present in the current time slot appear as markers at their POI; the player's presence is visible.
- Screen test covers POI click navigation and marker rendering.

## Finding coverage
F1 district interior is a list, not a place; F2 NPC presence invisible; F3 no spatial POI navigation.
close_reason: Per-district surveyor-plate maps shipped: all 74 POIs positioned with geometry integrity test, click-to-enter with presence gating, player presence indicator, NPC markers (POI-anchored + district-presence line). Screen tests cover navigation, gating, and markers. Commit 31bc5ea.

title: Dialogue screen: scene framing, stakes, and consistent choice voice
description:
## Why
Verified 2026-06-10 (Marion, day 1): the dialogue screen is a portrait, one italic NPC line, and 3-5 centered buttons on a void. No scene location, no body language beyond the single line, no hint which choices advance a bond or carry cost, and inconsistent voice — one option quoted ("What do we do if the debt breaks us?"), others unquoted imperatives ('Talk about the books.'). 'Leave her to it.' and 'Leave' appear together.

## What
Frame the scene (location line + short stage direction), differentiate choice types visually (ask / push / commit / leave), one consistent voice convention for options (first-person spoken lines OR intents, not both), and show after-effects per docs/workflows/dialogue-review.md (a changed topic list, a bond note — something visible).

## Why now
Dialogue is where NPC stories live; player feedback names 'stories, the npcs' (2026-06-10). Presentation is the blocker before more dialogue content is authored onto a weak frame.

## Player impact
Conversations read as scenes with stakes instead of button quizzes; the player can tell what kind of move each option is.

## System impact
DialogueScreen component; dialogues.json may need a scene/location field and choice-type tags; dialogue selectors.

## Non-goals
No rewriting of existing dialogue trees' content beyond voice normalization; no new dialogue mechanics (skill checks etc.).

## Fiction contract
Each dialogue is a scene in a room with a person who has their own agenda; the UI is the camera, not a form.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every dialogue shows where it happens and one line of staging.
- All options across dialogues.json follow one voice convention; duplicate leave options removed.
- After a choice, at least one visible change (new topic, mood shift, journal beat) — verified in a screen test.

## Finding coverage
F1 no scene framing; F2 mixed option voice; F3 duplicate leave options; F4 invisible consequence after choices.

title: Roster screen: repair layout and make NPC interaction legible
description:
## Why
Verified 2026-06-10 with Marion selected: right column renders 'Offer Gift' and 'Court' as tall vertical slivers with rotated text; 'Primary: — None / Secondary: — None / Armor: — None' float as unlabeled boxes; the status legend (Available/Working/Training/Recovering) looks like four disabled buttons; the bio is a long italic gold block competing with attribute bars. The screen answers 'what are this person's numbers' but not 'what can I do with them and what do they need from me'.

## What
Restructure the NPC detail panel: identity block (portrait, name, role, current state) → 'what they want / what they fear' narrative line → actions as a normal horizontal button row (Talk, Assign, Gift, Court) → equipment slots labeled as a group → attributes/skills tabs as-is. Status chips read as states, not buttons.

## Why now
Roster handling is explicit player feedback (2026-06-10); the layout defects look like bugs and erode trust in the whole UI.

## Player impact
Managing people feels like leading people: each NPC shows state, desire, and available actions at a glance.

## System impact
RosterScreen / NpcDetailPanel components and CSS; selector may need to expose desire/fear line from npc-arcs definitions. No domain changes.

## Non-goals
No new interaction mechanics (courtship depth is destiny-jed7), no portrait production (destiny-z492).

## Fiction contract
The roster is the lord's muster book: people, their condition, their temper, and what the house owes them — not a stat sheet.

## Acceptance Criteria
- No vertical/rotated button text anywhere on the screen; equipment slots are grouped under a visible 'Equipment' label.
- Status chips are visually distinct from buttons (screenshot-verified via Playwright).
- Each roster NPC shows one want/fear line sourced from definitions.
- Fresh-eyes check: 'what can I do with Marion right now' answerable in one glance.

## Finding coverage
F1 rotated/sliver action buttons; F2 floating unlabeled equipment slots; F3 status legend mimics buttons; F4 no want/fear surfacing.
close_reason: Muster Roll shipped (commit ed24a34): broken flex layout fixed (no more sliver buttons / floating None boxes), register-style roll with condition tallies and wax-red selection, personnel leaf with framed portrait, prominent Wants/Needs motivation line, horizontal action row, segmented duty selector, labeled Arms & Armor slots with condition. All acceptance criteria met; 5952 tests + build + golden green; screenshot-verified at 1440px.

title: Day-turn report: replace stacked event modals with a morning report
description:
## Why
Verified 2026-06-10: first End Day produced 8 sequential modal popups ('Noted', 'I understand the stakes', '+7 more events pending'). The living world arrives as a dismissal chore; by modal 4 the player is clicking through. The Journal ('The Record') then shows the same facts as flat system log lines, so neither surface tells the day as a story.

## What
A single morning-report surface at day turn: grouped sections (Your house / Your contracts / The city), each item one line with an expand affordance; events that demand a decision stay as modals, pure information never does. Journal entries get category + actor framing instead of 'system' badges.

## Why now
This is where 'living city' is felt or lost; it is the only recurring narrative beat every player sees every day. Complements destiny-73ji (event meaning) and destiny-ioyb (world simulation) — without this surface, simulation work stays invisible.

## Player impact
The day turn becomes the rhythm beat: read the city's pulse in one view, drill into what matters, decide only when a decision exists.

## System impact
Event presentation layer (EventModal usage, new report screen/section), event queue selector to classify decision vs information events. Event generation unchanged.

## Non-goals
No new event content, no simulation changes, no removal of decision modals.

## Fiction contract
Marion (or the steward role) compiles the morning report; the voice is hers — terse, ledger-minded, flagging what she thinks the lord ignores at their peril.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Ending day 1 yields at most 1 modal (if a decision event exists); all informational events land in one grouped report.
- Each report line names the actor or place that changed and links to the relevant screen.
- Journal no longer labels world events with a 'system' badge.

## Finding coverage
F1 modal stack on day turn; F2 information events forced through decision UI; F3 journal reads as debug log.

comments.author: André Dittrich
comments.text: 2026-06-12 audit update: now blocked by destiny-nflm (firingMode) — the decision-vs-information classification this report needs becomes a schema fact there instead of a heuristic. Scope note: the acceptance line about Journal badges moves to the Chronicle epic destiny-n4rs (journal rework owns badges/categories); this bead keeps the morning-report surface itself. Audit evidence reinforcing this bead: live day-2 run produced 8 stacked modals incl. 3 context-free bond-service scenes.

title: Quest lead cards: narrative hook first, dossier second
description:
## Why
Verified 2026-06-10 (lead 'A Word with Brennik'): the lead card renders nine labeled metadata rows — Issuer, Payer, Origin, Stakeholder, Why now, What they want, Likely fallout, Reward, Time limit, Surfaced, Withdraws after, Found in, District — in uniform italic. It reads like the project's internal task contract, not a job offer. After acceptance the active card repeats the same briefing twice (card body + Quest Journal entry 1).

## What
Redesign the lead/contract card: a 2-3 sentence in-world pitch (who wants what done and why it smells) up top, stakes and reward as scannable chips, and the full dossier behind a 'Look closer' expansion. Remove duplicate briefing text on active contracts; the journal shows progress beats only.

## Why now
Leads are the core loop's storefront; player feedback names quests and UI narrative directly (2026-06-10). Relates to destiny-73ji F8/F9 (who pays / stakeholder legibility) — this bead is the presentation slice.

## Player impact
A lead can be judged in five seconds; detail is there when wanted; accepted work reads as a story in progress, not a record dump.

## System impact
ContractBoardScreen card components and selectors; quest definition fields unchanged.

## Non-goals
No quest content rewrite, no generation changes, no new quest types.

## Fiction contract
A lead is a voice — somebody asking the house for help with an agenda behind it. The card should sound like that person, with the clerk's annotations beneath.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Lead card shows hook (≤3 sentences), reward, deadline, district without scrolling; everything else expands.
- Active contract shows current objective + next step once, not the briefing twice.
- Screen test: fresh player can answer 'what am I being asked, by whom, for how much' from the collapsed card.

## Finding coverage
F1 metadata-dump lead card; F2 duplicated briefing on active card; F3 uniform italic styling makes hierarchy unreadable.
close_reason: Redesigned quest lead cards: narrative hook first, reward/deadline chips, full dossier expandable; removed duplicate briefing from active contracts

title: City map: replace district card list with a spatial map
description:
## Why
/district-map is titled 'The City' but is a scrolling list of cards — explicit player feedback: 'the city map is actually not a map' (2026-06-10). Adjacency, routes, restricted passages, and where-am-I are invisible; the header copy even says 'Six districts' while ~11 cards render.

## What
A spatial city map view: districts positioned geographically, adjacency edges with passage state (open / restricted / condemned), you-are-here marker, and per-district badges (work posted, tension, faction control). Cards remain as a secondary detail view on select.

## Why now
Travel decisions and the 'city watches who passes' fantasy depend on reading space; the card list flattens it. Also blocks the living-city work from being visible (NPC movement has no surface to show on).

## Player impact
The player can answer: where am I, what borders what, which crossing is dangerous, where is work — without scrolling cards.

## System impact
New map component in src/ui, districts.json may need coordinates, selector for map view model. Domain unchanged except optional district position data.

## Non-goals
No pathfinding, no real-time NPC dots in slice 1, no art-quality cartography (schematic node-edge map is acceptable first).

## Fiction contract
The city is a contested geography; the map should read like a watch captain's wall chart, not an index.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Player sees all districts spatially with adjacency edges and passage states.
- 'You are here' is visible and updates on travel.
- Header copy matches the real district count.
- Travel can be initiated from the map and the time cost is visible before confirming.

## Finding coverage
F1 list-not-map; F2 'Six districts' vs 11 cards copy bug; F3 adjacency/passage state buried in per-district detail page.
close_reason: Spatial city map shipped: districts on two banks, border-type crossings, you-are-here/house markers, environ markers, travel from map with visible time cost, district count copy fixed dynamically. Adjacency island bug in content fixed (Mouth Ferry + Ledger Span). Commit 31bc5ea.

title: Art direction pass: give every player surface a visual identity
description:
## Why
The game is almost entirely typographic. Opening screen art is a near-black flat silhouette; only 5 of ~11 districts have card art (Gilded Heights, The Hollows, Ash Quay, Mistward, Cinder Row, The Northbank, The Relict have none); POI, House, Work Board, Journal, Factions screens have zero imagery; only 4 portraits exist (ida-rhys, marion-vale, player, verek-sorn) while the roster/enemy cast is larger. Empty panels (e.g. right column of the POI screen) read as missing content.

## What
A coherent art direction: complete district art set, POI category illustrations or strong iconography, portrait coverage for all named NPCs (or a styled fallback), a visible opening illustration, and a rule for what every screen shows when there is no image.

## Why now
'Graphics' is explicit player feedback (2026-06-10). Each new screen added without an art rule deepens the text-wall impression.

## Player impact
Districts and people become recognizable at a glance; screens stop feeling like an admin tool.

## System impact
public/ assets, district/POI/NPC card components, EmptyState component. No domain changes.

## Non-goals
No engine/renderer change, no animation system, no replacing the dark-gold theme (it is consistent and good).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every district card on /district-map has art.
- Every named NPC shows a portrait or an intentional styled fallback (house sigil/silhouette), never a bare box.
- POI screens show no empty panels: each panel has content or is removed.
- Opening illustration is readable at first glance (silhouette contrast raised or replaced).

## Success Criteria
- A fresh player can name two districts by their image alone after one session.
- No screen in the first hour shows a bare empty panel or missing-image box.

## Finding coverage
F1 opening art unreadable; F2 district art incomplete; F3 POI/house screens imageless; F4 portrait coverage gap; F5 empty right panel on POI screen.
notes: Consistency audit 2026-06-11: the epic's premise is partially overtaken by the folio map system. District card art is OBSOLETE as an acceptance item — the card grid was removed (db08d43) and districts are now drawn as survey plates with street fabric (645a5cb, c6f44ea); the 5 jpgs in public/districts/ are unused dead assets (delete or repurpose as POI-screen vignettes). STILL OPEN and valid: portrait coverage (4 portraits vs full cast — biggest remaining gap), DistrictPoiScreen's empty right panel, opening-screen illustration contrast, and the no-bare-empty-panel rule. Acceptance should be re-anchored to those four when this epic is next claimed.

title: Fix bd prime session close protocol - add missing quality gates
description:
## Why
The `bd prime` output shows an incomplete session close protocol. It only shows `[ ] bd close <id>` but misses critical quality gates that should run before pushing work.

Current protocol:
```
[ ] bd close <id1> <id2> ...   (close completed issues)
```

Missing:
- `bd lint` - Check issue quality before close
- `bd preflight` - Full pre-PR checks (lint + stale + orphans)
- `git pull --rebase` - Sync with remote before push
- `git push` - Push code (remote is local-only, but command should exist)

This gap means agents may push incomplete or low-quality issue descriptions.

## What
Update the session close protocol displayed by `bd prime` to include all quality gates in the correct order.

## Why now
Every session close risks leaving weak beads or broken state without these gates. This is workflow infrastructure that should be solid before more work accumulates.

## Player impact
None directly. This is agent workflow quality.

## System impact
- `.claude/settings.json` - Add SessionEnd hook to enforce protocol
- Potentially `.beads/hooks/` if hook script needs updating

## Does not include
- Changing the `bd prime` command itself (that's beads CLI)
- Adding git remote configuration

## Evidence
- `bd prime` output shows incomplete checklist
- CLAUDE.md has more complete protocol but not enforced via hooks

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible**: N/A (agent workflow)
2. **System/technical**: SessionEnd hook runs `bd preflight` and reports results before session ends
3. **Tests/verification**: Hook executes without error, shows lint + stale + orphans status

## Finding coverage
From `bd prime` analysis: Session Close Protocol incomplete, missing quality gates.

## Fiction contract
N/A
close_reason: SessionEnd hook added to .claude/settings.json, CLAUDE.md updated with correct protocol

title: Inventory documents: make Open action actually surface document contents
description:
## Why
Document items currently advertise `Open` as their primary inventory action, but the storage UI does not route that action anywhere. This makes documents look interactive while doing nothing when clicked.

## What
Implement a real player-facing handling path for the `Open` inventory action on document items, or replace the action with a truthful alternative surface.

## Why now
The house clue clarity pass made document descriptions and evidence roles more explicit, which also exposed that the existing `Open` affordance is dead.

## Player impact
Players will no longer click an inventory document expecting to read it and get no response.

## System impact
Touches inventory action routing, document presentation, and tests for item interactions.

## Does not include
- no full codex/journal redesign
- no content rewrite for every document in the game

## Evidence
- `selectItemActions` exposes `Open` for document items
- `HouseStoragePanel` and `MissionPackPanel` do not dispatch any handler for `open`

## Acceptance Criteria
### Player-visible acceptance
Clicking the primary action on a document shows meaningful document content or a truthful next surface.
### System / technical acceptance
The inventory action contract no longer exposes dead `open` actions.
### Tests / verification acceptance
Coverage proves document primary actions are actionable in the inventory UI.

## Fiction contract
A document the player can carry should not pretend to be readable while silently doing nothing.
notes:
Repro:
1. Open Inventory / House Storage.
2. Find any document item such as the House Debt Ledger or Bureau Ledger.
3. Click the primary `Open` action.
4. Observe that no document-reading surface or meaningful response appears.

title: Add pre-commit hook and CI step for typecheck
description:
## Why
Pre-existing TypeScript errors (TS2367) in ExpeditionPrepScreen survived for multiple sessions undetected because `pnpm typecheck` was not enforced before commits. Only discovered when a session ran it manually. No CI exists. No pre-commit hook exists.

## What
1. Add a pre-commit hook via `simple-git-hooks` or `husky` that runs `pnpm typecheck` before every commit.
2. Add a GitHub Actions CI workflow (or extend copilot-setup-steps.yml) that runs typecheck + test:run on push/PR.

## Why Now
KI Retro 2026-06-02 identified this as the reason pre-existing TS errors go unnoticed for multiple sessions. The fix is mechanical and has no game-logic risk.

## Player Impact
None visible — quality gate. Prevents broken builds from accumulating.

## System Impact
- New devDependency (simple-git-hooks or husky)
- New package.json scripts or .husky/ directory
- New or extended .github/workflows/ file

## Non-Goals
- Not adding lint-staged for now (can be separate bead)
- Not enforcing formatting in hook (use pnpm format separately)

## Acceptance Criteria
- `git commit` on a branch with TS errors is blocked with a clear error message
- `pnpm typecheck` runs in CI on push to main and on PRs
- `pnpm test:run` runs in CI on push to main and on PRs
- Existing passing commits are unaffected
- Hook setup documented in README or engineering-standards.md

## Finding coverage
KI Retro 2026-06-02 F4, F3

title: Extract isDeployable() helper to centralize NPC deployment guard
description:
## Why
Deployment eligibility is checked in 4+ locations (addNpcToSelectedSquad, startCombatEncounter, selectMissionPrepSummary, ExpeditionPrepScreen) with slightly different conditions per site. Each site is a potential divergence point. A 'working NPC deployed' bug was filed (destiny-krtc) and fixed ad-hoc per location — the root cause (no shared helper) remains.

## What
Extract a pure function `isDeployable(npc: NpcRuntimeState): boolean` in `src/domain/npc/` (or `src/application/commands/`) and replace all per-location guards with calls to it.

## Why Now
destiny-krtc is closed but the structural problem it exposed is not. Next deployment bug will require the same scatter-fix again.

## Player Impact
None visible — correctness fix. Prevents future 'working NPCs still deployable' regressions.

## System Impact
- New pure function in domain or application layer
- 4+ call sites updated
- Existing deployment guard tests refactored to call through isDeployable()
- No schema change, no state change

## Non-Goals
- Not changing deployment rules themselves
- Not adding new constraints

## Acceptance Criteria
- `isDeployable(npc)` exists as an exported pure function
- All deployment guard sites use it: addNpcToSelectedSquad, startCombatEncounter, selectMissionPrepSummary, ExpeditionPrepScreen
- Unit tests for isDeployable() cover: idle✓, working✗, training✗, recovering✗, assigned_title✗, transferred✗, below-min-health✗
- All existing deployment tests pass unchanged
- No ad-hoc `assignment !== 'working'` guards remain outside isDeployable()

## Finding coverage
KI Retro 2026-06-02 F6

title: Install and configure Storybook for isolated component development
description:
## Why
AI agents cannot see the UI they produce. Storybook provides an isolated sandbox for developing and reviewing components without running the full app. Combined with Playwright MCP screenshots, this closes the visual feedback gap.

## What
Install and configure Storybook (Vite + React) for the project. Create initial stories for the highest-reuse components (action buttons, EmptyState, NpcCard, TimeCostBadge).

## Why Now
Follows Playwright MCP setup. Together they form the minimum viable visual dev loop for agents.

## Player Impact
Indirect: faster UI iteration → fewer layout regressions shipped to players.

## System Impact
- Adds storybook devDependencies
- Adds `.storybook/` config directory
- Adds `*.stories.tsx` files alongside components
- No changes to app runtime or test suite

## Non-Goals
- Not replacing vitest/testing-library tests
- Not visual regression CI (separate bead if needed)
- Not full story coverage for all components on day one

## Acceptance Criteria
- `pnpm storybook` starts without errors
- At least 4 component stories exist
- Existing test suite still passes after install
- `pnpm build` still passes

## Suggested First Stories
- `CombatActionButton`
- `EmptyState` (destiny-35ri)
- `TimeCostBadge`
- `NpcStatusBadge`

title: Add house/institution sigil icons (seal-stamp style SVG)
description:
## Why
Noble houses and institutions have no visual identity in the UI. Players interact with House Sorn, House Merrow, Salt Ledger Hall etc. purely through text. A minimal icon system would ground them as distinct entities in the world.

## What
Design and implement a set of monochrome SVG sigil icons — one per house/institution — in the style of worn administrative seals/stamps. Displayed as small identifiers in lists, screen headers, and dialogue attribution.

## Why Now
Follows from the district image quality discussion (destiny-9myj). House identity is the next layer of visual worldbuilding after locations.

## Setting Constraints (from art-direction.md)
- Material language: institutional brass, worn metal, paper records
- Avoid: heraldic fantasy animals, glossy icons, ornamental crests
- Style: monochrome SVG contour, slightly worn, seal/stamp aesthetic

## Proposed Symbol Mapping
| Entity | Symbol |
|--------|--------|
| House Valdris (player) | Key — access, property, locked ledgers |
| House Sorn | Anchor ring — old lines, legitimacy gate |
| House Merrow | Rope knot / chain link — dock leverage |
| Lantern Vale Inn | Oil lamp — information, anonymity |
| Chapel of Saint Vey | Open book / ink blot — birth registry, confession |
| Salt Ledger Hall | Scales / sealed document — contracts, debt |
| House Sable-Cairn | Broken seal — fallen prestige |

## Non-Goals
- No color fills, no gradients
- Not a full heraldry system
- Not replacing portrait images

## Acceptance Criteria
- SVG icons exist for all 7 entities
- Icons render cleanly at 24px and 48px
- Style is consistent across all icons (same stroke weight, same worn-seal aesthetic)
- At least one screen uses the icons (e.g. HouseScreen header or workboard list)
- Art direction doc updated with icon style rules

## Player Impact
Houses feel like distinct power blocs with visual identity, not just text labels. World reads as more grounded and politically legible.
close_reason: Sigil SVGs created, HouseSigil component implemented, integrated into HouseScreen

title: Replace placeholder district images with quality artwork
description:
## Why
The current district images (the-city.jpg, the-pale.jpg, iron-docks.jpg, etc.) are placeholder AI-generated or stock images. The first image players see (district-harbor → the-city.jpg) looks outdated and low-quality.

## What
Replace the 5 district images in public/districts/ with polished, consistent artwork that matches the game's tone.

## Why Now
First impressions matter. The district map is one of the first screens players interact with. Substandard imagery undermines trust in the product.

## Player Impact
Players' first visual impression of the world is set by these images. High-quality art communicates world-building intent and tonal consistency.

## System Impact
Drop-in replacement: same filenames, same /districts/{slug}.jpg convention. No code changes required.
Slots: the-city.jpg, the-pale.jpg, iron-docks.jpg, the-tangle.jpg, ashfields.jpg

## Non-Goals
- Not redesigning the district map layout
- Not adding animated or dynamic backgrounds

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 5 district images replaced with quality artwork
- Images are consistent in style and tone
- No player can mistake them for AI placeholder art
- File format: jpg, same naming convention

## Notes
There is currently NO image for House Valdris specifically — districts map to city areas (harbor, warrens, etc.), not to houses. If a house-specific visual is desired, that is a separate bead.

## Generation Command (for reference)
Use pollinations.ai API:
curl -L --max-time 90 -s -o public/districts/{district-id}.jpg 'https://image.pollinations.ai/prompt/{URL-ENCODED-PROMPT}?width=800&height=600&nologo=true&model=flux&seed={N}'

Prompt template: 'dark fantasy medieval {district character} district, atmospheric wide landscape, {key visual elements}, oil painting, moody lighting, 800x600'

LABELS: art-direction
close_reason: Defers to manual art generation; prompt template documented for future work

title: Conclude and Return to Work Board from on-site contract combat does not properly settle or surface the contract on the board
description:
## Why
After on-site combat for a linked contract, the "Conclude and Return to Work Board" button (shown when outcome resolved) dispatches concludeCombatEncounter and navigates to /contracts, but the contract is not properly "returned" or the board does not present a clear "aftermath" or "conclude the contract" step. The settlement (settleQuestSuccess / branch / setback) happens, but the Work Board (ContractBoardScreen) does not have special handling for combat-returned contracts (no dedicated "handle combat aftermath" or "claim" flow for 'resolved' or 'branch-aftermath' stage from combat). Users report the conclude flow "funktioniert nicht".

This breaks the intended contract lifecycle for on-site combat: take -> on-site prep -> combat -> return to board for resolution/branch/claim.

## What
The conclude button in CombatScreen for resolved linked combat should reliably:
- Call the settlement logic (which it does via the command).
- Navigate to the board in a state where the contract shows as "back from combat" with clear next player action (handle branch, claim reward, see full aftermath).

Currently the board lists it based on runtime (with objectiveMet or stage), but no dedicated UI or prompt for the "return" conclusion.

The inactive/resolved state in CombatScreen also has a direct "Return to Work Board" button that only navigates (no dispatch).

## Why now
This is a core contract flow bug. On-site combat is a primary way contracts resolve (especially the deep investigation and intrigue ones). If the return doesn't work, the player cannot properly conclude contracts started this way, breaking progression, rewards, and the "return to operations" promise in the UI copy.

It also affects the new correspondence/intrigue cluster: many deep investigations (the ones Codex just made persistent) will end in combat, and the return must work to deliver the "aftermath" (including discovered letters etc.).

## Player impact
Player takes a contract, goes on-site, fights, wins or loses, clicks "Conclude and Return to Work Board", lands on the board but the contract doesn't feel "concluded" or no clear step to finalize the combat outcome on the board. Rewards may not be claimed, branches not chosen, the contract may linger in active with confusing state.

## System impact
- CombatScreen.tsx (the conclude click handler and resolved panel copy)
- ContractBoardScreen.tsx (needs handling for combat-returned states: 'resolved', 'setback', 'branch-aftermath' from combat conclude)
- Possibly questReducers or board selectors to surface "combat aftermath pending" or special actions.
- The concludeCombatEncounter already does the settlement for linked; the gap is the board not consuming/presenting the post-combat state.

## Does not include
- Full redesign of contract board.
- Changes to non-linked (free) combat.
- UI for the correspondence system itself.

## Evidence
- User report: "Conclude and return to workboard" nach einem Kampf funktioniert nicht.
- CombatScreen.tsx and test show the button and navigation expectation to /contracts for linked.
- In concludeCombatEncounter: for linked it calls settle with specific objectiveLabel mentioning "Return to the Work Board for the aftermath."
- ContractBoardScreen shows activeQuests with objectiveLabel and "Next step", but no special case for combat stages or "return from combat".
- Ties to quest-authoring audit "runtime too shallow" for deep contracts that now use combat more.
- Recent investigation work makes more contracts resolve via combat, increasing the visibility of this bug.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: For a linked contract that goes to on-site combat, after fight resolution, clicking "Conclude and Return to Work Board" navigates to the board, and the contract appears with clear indication that it is back from combat (e.g. special badge "Combat aftermath" or the stage-specific objectiveLabel is prominent, and there is a way to "finalize" or see the full settlement on the board if needed).
2. System/technical: The conclude handler + settlement must leave the runtime in a state the board can render as "post-combat return". The board must at minimum not lose the contract and must display the post-combat objectiveLabel and any branch/aftermath info.
3. Tests: Extend CombatScreen.test and ContractBoardScreen.test (or integration) to cover the full take -> on-site -> combat -> conclude -> board return flow for both victory and branch-aftermath defeat. The board test should assert the contract is listed with the expected post-combat copy.

## Finding coverage
Addresses user-reported break in the on-site contract -> combat -> return flow, which is critical for the deep investigation contracts that are being made persistent.
notes: Critical analysis: the conclude button in CombatScreen dispatches + navigates unconditionally. For linked contracts the settlement happens in the command (settleQuestSuccess etc), but ContractBoardScreen has no special 'post-combat return' or 'handle branch-aftermath from combat' UI — it just renders the runtime with whatever objectiveLabel was set. This is why 'return to workboard' feels like it does nothing visible. The flow relies on the board already knowing how to present post-combat states, which it doesn't for the combat return path. Ties to the quest board not having a 'finalize combat' step.

title: Audit named quest and event stakeholders so surfaced people resolve to real entities or explicit abstractions
description:
## Why
When contract or event copy names a person, witness, debtor, victim, patron, or rival, the player cannot currently tell whether that figure is a real authored NPC, an off-screen abstraction, or a dangling reference. This weakens trust in both the world and the work board.

## What
Audit named stakeholders in authored quests/events and add a truth contract so surfaced names either resolve to real entities, explicit off-screen abstractions, or documented non-character placeholders. Add guardrails where needed so presentation does not imply entity-level continuity that the data does not support.

## Why now
This question came directly from player feedback and becomes more important once contracts and events begin surfacing payer/stakeholder truth more explicitly.

## Player impact
Players can trust that named people in contracts/events either exist as meaningful world entities or are clearly framed as off-screen actors rather than implied interactable NPCs.

## System impact
Touches content audit rules, reference/integrity validation expectations, quest/event presentation metadata, and possibly authoring docs.

## Does not include
- no requirement that every named person become a fully interactive NPC
- no broad procedural social-simulation expansion

## Evidence
Player feedback: "Existieren alle in den Quest erwähnten NPCs überhaupt?" Existing integrity epic `destiny-2c2q` covers broken IDs and silent failures, but not the player-facing truth contract around named stakeholders.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** Touched contract/event surfaces do not imply a named stakeholder is a trackable NPC unless the data supports that claim.
2. **System / technical:** Authoring rules distinguish real entity references from off-screen stakeholder abstractions and prevent ambiguous presentation where possible.
3. **Tests / verification:** Add validation or fixture coverage for the chosen truth contract; `pnpm test:run`, `pnpm typecheck`, and `pnpm build` must pass for code changes.

## Finding coverage
F10 — named NPC/stakeholder truth.

## Fiction contract
Valdenmoor may mention unseen people, but the UI must not imply a persistent social actor where only a narrative abstraction exists.
notes:
Coordinate with destiny-2c2q. This bead covers player-facing truth about named stakeholders, not all catalog integrity or procedural quest generation.
QuestPresentation now carries typed actor references for issuer, payer, and stakeholder: npc, faction, district, or offscreen. ContractBoardScreen continues to show the readable labels, while src/application/content/questPresentation.test.ts validates that entity-backed refs resolve against the content catalog and that non-entity Harborwatch stakeholders are marked as explicit off-screen actors rather than implied NPCs. Updated docs/workflows/narrative-content.md and docs/workflows/event-review.md to document the truth contract.

title: Contracts and leads: surface payer, stakeholder, and likely consequence before acceptance
description:
## Why
Contracts and leads currently surface rewards and some metadata, but they do not consistently answer the player's real questions: who is paying, who benefits, who is harmed, and what kind of downstream impact the work may have on NPCs, factions, districts, or the city.

## What
Define and implement a contract/lead presentation contract that surfaces payer, issuer, stakeholder, and likely consequence before acceptance and preserves important consequence visibility after resolution.

## Why now
The work board is one of the game's primary decision surfaces. If it cannot explain what kind of work the player is taking, quest choice remains shallow even when backend consequences exist.

## Player impact
Players can make informed decisions about what kind of work they take, who they are serving, and what sort of fallout to expect.

## System impact
Touches quest presentation metadata, selectors, work-board or lead presentation surfaces, and follow-through consequence surfaces after resolution.

## Does not include
- no procedural quest generation expansion
- no full rewrite of every quest text in one pass

## Evidence
Player feedback asking who pays rewards, what impact quests have on NPCs and the city, and whether the mentioned stakeholders are real entities with consequences.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** At least one contract/lead surface explicitly communicates payer/issuer and likely stakeholder impact before acceptance.
2. **System / technical:** A documented distinction exists between payer, issuer, narrative stakeholder, and downstream consequence, and the relevant selector/view-model surfaces expose those fields.
3. **Tests / verification:** Add selector/screen or route-level verification for contract readability; `pnpm test:run`, `pnpm typecheck`, and `pnpm build` must pass.

## Finding coverage
F7, F8, F9 — contract and lead impact readability.

## Fiction contract
Contracts should read like work commissioned by real people or powers in Valdenmoor, not anonymous reward dispensers.
notes:
Coordination note: this is about readability and attribution, not wage math or reward balancing. If implementation exposes missing metadata for payer/issuer/stakeholder truth, create follow-up data beads rather than over-expanding this slice.
ContractBoardScreen now surfaces Issuer, Payer, Stakeholder, Why now, What they want, and Likely fallout before acceptance. QuestPresentation exposes payerLabel, stakeholderLabel, and likelyConsequence with authored overrides where the contract truth matters most and generic fallbacks based on reward standing, city dials, debt, relationship deltas, and unlocks. The distinction is documented in docs/workflows/narrative-content.md under Contract Readability Rule.

title: Rework tutorial guidance delivery so system teaching is not hidden inside weak narrative events
description:
## Why
Tutorial guidance such as end-of-day rhythm currently appears through weak narrative event wrappers, which blurs the line between story content and system instruction. This makes onboarding feel misplaced and less trustworthy.

## What
Audit tutorial-tagged events and rework delivery so system teaching appears in the right surface: event, overlay, dashboard instruction, helper panel, or other contextual guidance.

## Why now
Tutorial timing and delivery affect first-hour comprehension directly. Misplaced teaching increases confusion across the rest of the game.

## Player impact
Players learn systems when they need them, in the right presentation layer, with less narrative dissonance.

## System impact
Touches tutorial event authoring, onboarding presentation rules, and possibly dashboard/help surfaces and tests.

## Does not include
- no full onboarding redesign in one bead
- no broad rewrite of all first-hour content

## Evidence
Player feedback questioning why the end-of-day tutorial appears as an event and whether that delivery is appropriate at all.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** At least the end-of-day rhythm tutorial is delivered in a surface appropriate to its purpose rather than only because the event system exists.
2. **System / technical:** Tutorial-tagged guidance is classified by delivery type with a documented rule for when narrative-event wrappers are and are not allowed.
3. **Tests / verification:** Add first-hour flow verification for the touched tutorial path; `pnpm test:run`, `pnpm typecheck`, and `pnpm build` must pass for code changes.

## Finding coverage
F5, F6 — tutorial delivery and event-system consolidation.

## Fiction contract
Instructional guidance should not pretend to be a dramatic scene unless the fiction genuinely carries that teaching moment.
notes:
Anchor case: event-tutorial-end-day-rhythm. Decide whether this specific teaching moment belongs in an event, overlay, dashboard prompt, or help surface, then generalize the rule.
Removed event-tutorial-end-day-rhythm from the authored event queue. Replaced it with a day-2 GlobalStatusBar guidance surface tied to the actual End Day control, and documented the rule in event-review: button/rhythm teaching belongs in local UI guidance, not a dramatic event wrapper.

title: Audit and retune Marion events for context, trigger truth, and consequence readability
description:
## Why
Current Marion events are a concrete trust break: thin context, unclear trigger truth, vague choice text, and social framing that the player cannot place. Scenes like the warning, first-success, and milestone conversation read as if prior context exists when the surface does not establish it.

## What
Audit Marion-authored events and retune their trigger conditions, event bodies, and immediate consequence readability so each scene clearly communicates who is present, why the scene is happening now, and what the player's response means.

## Why now
This is the most concrete reported example of the broader event-legibility problem and it affects one of the game's most important early relationships.

## Player impact
Marion scenes feel intentional, grounded, and emotionally legible rather than arbitrary interruptions.

## System impact
Touches authored event content in `data/definitions/events.json`, trigger conditions, and supporting tests or playthrough coverage.

## Does not include
- no full Marion dialogue-tree rewrite
- no global rewrite of all companion events

## Evidence
Player feedback on `event-npc-marion-warning`, `event-marion-first-success`, and `event-marion-milestone-motivation` being unclear or contextless.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** Each touched Marion event makes clear who is present, why the scene fires now, and what each response option means without requiring the log.
2. **System / technical:** Trigger conditions are tightened where necessary so relationship beats do not fire without their implied setup.
3. **Tests / verification:** Add verification for at least one Marion event trigger and one player-visible copy/result path; `pnpm test:run`, `pnpm typecheck`, and `pnpm build` must pass.

## Finding coverage
F1, F2, F3 — direct Marion event coverage.

## Fiction contract
Marion is guarded, observant, and deliberate. Her scenes should feel specific and earned, not generic flavor interruptions.
notes:
Start with at least: event-npc-marion-warning, event-marion-first-success, event-marion-milestone-motivation. If more Marion scenes need retune, file follow-up beads instead of silently broadening scope.
Retuned Marion's warning, first-success, and milestone scenes for context clarity and explicit player-intent labels. Tightened milestone trigger truth with trust/completed-work gating, prevented generic rescheduling drift, and stabilized the economy-first playthrough fixture so it no longer depends on fragile first-run/event RNG behavior.

title: Define canonical event context, choice-label, and consequence-readability contract
description:
## Why
Several authored events assume context the player does not have. Event bodies, choice labels, and trigger timing do not reliably answer who is acting, why now, what changed, or what the player is agreeing to by clicking a choice.

## What
Define a reusable event presentation and context contract for authored events. The contract must specify required situational grounding, choice-label rules, when consequence copy must be surfaced directly, and when a scene is too instructional to remain wrapped as narrative flavor.

## Why now
Without a contract, any event rewrites will remain inconsistent and future authored scenes will reproduce the same confusion in new forms.

## Player impact
Players can read an event and understand its premise before choosing a response.

## System impact
Touches docs/workflows, authoring standards, and acceptance expectations for future event beads.

## Does not include
- no bulk rewrite of all event content
- no UI component implementation by itself

## Evidence
Player feedback on Marion scenes and tutorial-event confusion. Adjacent fiction-standard work exists in `destiny-rhx6`, but this specific event-context contract is not currently captured.

## Decision
Project Destiny will treat event comprehension as an explicit contract. Every player-facing event must communicate who is acting, why the scene is happening now, what changed, and what each choice means before the player commits to it.

## Rationale
This pushes the system toward player legibility instead of relying on invisible state changes or activity-log backfill. It also gives future event rewrites a stable bar for acceptance and prevents new authored content from reproducing the same context debt.

## Alternatives Considered
- Keep event context as a pure authoring/style issue. Rejected because that does not create an enforceable acceptance contract.
- Solve clarity only through a larger result screen. Rejected because unclear scene premise must be fixed before outcome presentation.
- Treat tutorial delivery separately from event authoring. Rejected because onboarding confusion is one of the clearest examples of the same presentation-contract problem.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** The contract explicitly requires player-facing events to answer who, why now, what changed, and what each choice means.
2. **System / technical:** The contract is documented in a stable workflow or authoring doc referenced by future event beads.
3. **Tests / verification:** Follow-up event beads reference the contract and include player-visible acceptance derived from it.

## Finding coverage
F1, F2, F3, F5, F6 — context and delivery contract coverage, not implementation.

## Fiction contract
A scene must stand on its own diegetic meaning; the activity log may deepen context but must not be required to explain the scene's basic premise.
notes:
Overlap: destiny-rhx6 already tracks fiction/authoring standards broadly. This bead is the event-specific contract slice for context, choice intent, and consequence readability.
Implemented event-review workflow in docs/workflows/event-review.md and linked it from AGENTS.md, design-review, and narrative-content. This is now the canonical contract for trigger context, choice intent, consequence readability, surface fit, and stakeholder truth for future event beads.

title: Add explicit event consequence surfaces beyond the activity log
description:
## Why
Important event outcomes are often only understandable through the activity log. This hides consequence, weakens emotional payoff, and makes events feel inert or arbitrary even when state changes are technically correct.

## What
Add an explicit event result surface or equivalent in-flow consequence pattern that distinguishes immediate player impact, NPC relationship impact, and faction/city/world impact for important authored events.

## Why now
This is the clearest player-facing gap after basic event pacing. If outcomes stay buried in the log, richer event content will continue to land as noise.

## Player impact
Players can tell what changed immediately after resolving an event without having to inspect the log for basic meaning.

## System impact
Touches event modal/result presentation, event outcome formatting, selectors/view-models for consequence summaries, and tests.

## Does not include
- no full redesign of the activity log
- no rewrite of every event outcome in one pass

## Evidence
Player feedback: "Bei allen Events muss ich erst in das Log schauen was das Outcome ist." Existing UI/UX umbrella `destiny-1knq` covers missing result surfaces broadly, but not this event-specific consequence contract.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** At least one real authored event shows immediate readable consequence in-surface without requiring the player to inspect the activity log.
2. **System / technical:** A reusable event consequence summary contract exists and distinguishes at least player, NPC, and faction/city/world impact layers where present.
3. **Tests / verification:** Add selector/screen or route-level verification proving that consequence information appears in-surface; `pnpm test:run`, `pnpm typecheck`, and `pnpm build` must pass.

## Finding coverage
F4, F5, F7 — outcome readability and impact legibility.

## Fiction contract
Consequences should read as something that happened in the world, not as a hidden state mutation discovered later in a log.
notes:
Overlap: destiny-1knq is the broader missing-result-screen umbrella. This bead narrows that scope to event consequence readability and should not silently absorb confirmation-modal or generic feedback-persistence work.
Implemented a reusable lastResolvedEventSummary surface in GameState, wired it through resolveEvent, exposed it via selectors, and rendered it in EventModal with explicit Player/NPC/World impact sections plus dismiss action. Added reducer and component tests, plus schema and initial-state updates.

title: Events UX/Meaning Pass: Trigger Context, Presentation, and Consequence Readability
description:
## Why
Player feedback shows that the current event system can be mechanically correct while still failing comprehension. Character events trigger without enough situational grounding, choice labels can be too thin to communicate intent, tutorial guidance is wrapped as weak story events, and consequences are often readable only in the activity log. Contracts and leads also fail to consistently tell the player who is paying, who benefits, and what impact the work has on named NPCs or the wider city.

## What
Define and implement a canonical player-facing contract for events, tutorial guidance events, and contract/lead consequence readability so authored content is legible in-surface without forcing the player to reverse-engineer intent from the log.

## Why now
This is no longer isolated wording debt. The user explicitly reported confusion around Marion scenes, end-of-day tutorial delivery, event outcome readability, and contract/lead meaning. If this remains implicit, more authored content will accumulate on an unreadable presentation contract.

## Player impact
The player can understand why an event is happening, what a choice means, and what changed afterward. Contracts and leads become legible as world work with visible payers, stakeholders, and consequences instead of generic reward stubs.

## System impact
Touches event authoring rules, event presentation copy, trigger expectations, result surfaces, tutorial delivery strategy, contract/lead selectors and UI, and verification workflows for player-facing readability.

## Does not include
- no full rewrite of every event and quest in the game
- no procedural generation expansion beyond what current readability work requires
- no economics rebalance beyond visibility and attribution surfaces

## Evidence
Player feedback on 2026-06-01/2026-06-02 covering unclear Marion events, tutorial placement, event outcome opacity, and contract/lead impact ambiguity. Existing overlap points: `destiny-1knq`, `destiny-rhx6`, `destiny-2c2q`, and `destiny-k988` partially touch adjacent concerns but do not consolidate the player-facing event/contract comprehension model.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** At least one event slice and one contract/lead slice demonstrate that the player can understand trigger context, choice intent, and immediate consequence without relying on the activity log for basic comprehension.
2. **System / technical:** A documented event/contract presentation contract exists and the implementation surfaces payer/stakeholder/consequence truth through selectors or view-model contracts rather than ad hoc copy only.
3. **Tests / verification:** Follow-up beads add route-, selector-, or screen-level verification for context readability and post-action consequence readability; `pnpm test:run`, `pnpm typecheck`, and `pnpm build` must pass for code slices.

## Success Criteria
- Event scenes communicate trigger context and choice intent without log archaeology.
- Event outcomes become legible in-surface for at least one meaningful slice.
- Contracts/leads explain payer and stakeholder meaning before acceptance.
- Named stakeholders no longer create false expectations about who is a real world entity.

## Finding coverage
F1 Marion warning event meaning is unclear.
F2 Marion first-success scene lacks situational context.
F3 Marion milestone scene references undefined "others".
F4 Event outcomes are readable only via the activity log.
F5 Event system needs consolidation around trigger context, presentation, and player pickup.
F6 Tutorial end-of-day rhythm delivery feels misplaced as a narrative event.
F7 Event impact on player, NPCs, city, and world is insufficiently legible.
F8 Contracts/leads do not clearly say who pays.
F9 Contracts/leads do not clearly surface stakeholder or consequence impact.
F10 It is unclear whether named NPCs mentioned in quests/events exist as real entities or only flavor abstractions.

## Fiction contract
Events should feel like specific world or character reactions to the player's situation, not detached UI popups that require log archaeology to understand why they matter.
notes: 2026-06-11 quest review: presentation slices destiny-9a3c (lead cards) and destiny-i702 (day report) created 2026-06-10 cover F4/F8/F9 surfaces. New narrative-substance beads complement this epic without overlapping it: destiny-wt34 (Mira chain), destiny-wc25 (quest givers), destiny-bd44 (antagonist agency), destiny-tedu (narrative floor + catalog test). This epic stays the umbrella for event/contract COMPREHENSION; the new beads add the story substance to comprehend.

comments.author: André Dittrich
comments.text: 2026-06-12 event/journal audit update: this epic stays the COMPREHENSION umbrella. Engine defects moved to new epic destiny-fidq (firingMode, burn fix, instance unification) — presentation slices here depend on it. New children added: destiny-muy6 (summary after every resolution, audit C3), destiny-bpnw (typology/kicker/actor chip; also resolves dead presentationFlavour field, audit N1), destiny-wwp6 (label lint, audit N3), destiny-i702 reparented here. New audit findings folded into coverage: N1 (identical presentation for all event types), N3 (68 generic labels), C3 (only last queued summary shown). Full reports: Plan_Event-System_Journal_Epic.md, journal_report.md.

title: Private Correspondence as Intimacy, Sensual, and Intrigue Vector (4th bead in cluster)
description:
## Why
There is no modeled private correspondence system. Information, desire, threats, and manipulation currently travel only through direct dialogue, events, or rumors. This leaves a major gap in how intimacy, power, and intrigue actually function in Valdenmoor: letters are mentioned in flavor but have no mechanical existence, persistence, or exploitability. NPCs cannot meaningfully communicate privately with each other or the player in ways that can be discovered, intercepted, forged, or weaponized. The living world and the "intimacy into obligation" principle (narrative.md + GDD) are undercut because private words have no teeth.

## What
A first-class, persistent private correspondence system is added. Named NPCs (roster + world) send letters to each other and to the player. Letters are generated from composable authored modules, carry provenance and sensitivity, exist independently of the player, and can be discovered, read, intercepted, forged, withheld, or used for blackmail (especially intimate/compromise content). Exploitation of correspondence becomes a real, first-class vector for power, manipulation, and consequence that feeds back into relationships, memories, and (later) politics.

## Why now
The bond/power-dynamics cluster (destiny-dbk6, destiny-bw2h, destiny-lfxq) and politics intrigue work (destiny-rjyy + destiny-58n5) are currently missing the actual medium through which much of Valdenmoor's blackmail, seduction, alliance, and betrayal travels. Adding correspondence now creates the missing connective tissue before those systems are heavily consumed. It also directly enables the sensual/intimacy pillar at the world level (NPC↔NPC) rather than only player-facing.

## Player impact
- The player can discover, read, and weaponize real private correspondence between NPCs (and between NPCs and themselves).
- Intimate or compromising letters become high-stakes objects: sources of leverage, risk of exposure, or emotional/political consequence.
- The world feels more alive and dangerous because NPCs are having private conversations the player can steal or disrupt.
- New meaningful choices around information control (withhold, leak, forge, deliver, blackmail).

## System impact
- New domain: `src/domain/correspondence/contracts.ts` + pure composition logic.
- New primary command file: `src/application/commands/correspondence.ts` (generate, delivery, interception, read, blackmail, forgery).
- Narrow touches: `src/domain/game/contracts.ts` (add `privateCorrespondence` array), `src/application/commands/applyNpcAgency.ts`, `src/application/commands/applyNpcPairing.ts` (hooks for generation), `src/application/commands/endDay.ts` (light orchestration), `src/domain/events/contracts.ts` + `applyEventOutcome.ts` (optional outcome types).
- New data: `data/definitions/correspondence-templates.json` (modules + seed families).
- Reuses heavily: `getRelationship` / `adjustRelationshipDelta` / `writeNpcMemory` / `appendActivityLogEntry`, existing RNG seeding, npcMemory system.
- No changes to bondStatus schema, politics voting logic yet, or UI surfaces (those are follow-up beads).

## Does not include
- No dedicated inbox/journal UI or physical mail items.
- No full courier simulation or delivery scheduling.
- No political vote modifiers or deep integration into applyPolitics (that comes after the leverage queries in the cluster).
- No new pregnancy/legacy/household mechanics.
- No authored letter content beyond the modular template system (Narrative role owns seed families later).
- No balance pass on how powerful blackmail is.

## Evidence
- User directive (this exact 4th bead request + strong living-world + exploitation vectors + templates/textbausteine for direct/sensual tone).
- GDD §3.5 Relationships + Sensual pillar (earned intimacy, politics + relationships affect each other, intimacy as obligation/leverage).
- docs/content-policy.md (post-2026-06-01 consensual intimate section: stark andeutend default, earned direct allowed, model aftermath, character-driven).
- docs/narrative.md ("intimacy into obligation", blackmail/favor networks in the city).
- Existing patterns: event-rumor-templates.json (modular precedent), applyNpcAgency, applyNpcPairing (intimacy hooks), bond power work (destiny-dbk6), intrigue events (destiny-58n5).
- Closed foundational work: destiny-13si (directed relationships), destiny-l7zl (intimacyStage + power fiction contract).
- Living-world specialist panel findings and prime memories on NPCs as independent agents.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible**: NPCs generate and "send" private letters to each other and the player. The player can discover, read, and trigger exploitation (interception, blackmail, forgery, withholding) on real correspondence. Intimate/compromise letters only appear when relationship depth justifies them. Consequences (relationship deltas, memories, activity log echoes, potential political ripples) are visible.
2. **System/technical**: Pure composer function generates deterministic text from modules + relationship context. Generation respects earned sensual rules (default stark andeutend; direct only at high committed depth + narrative justification per policy + GDD). All exploitation paths go through existing `adjustRelationshipDelta` + `writeNpcMemory`. No direct mutation of axes outside relationship-owned code. Schema addition is minimal and follows the full checklist.
3. **Tests/verification**: Co-located test file with ≥6-8 cases covering: generation at different intimacy levels (mundane vs intimate vs compromising), NPC↔NPC letters being created, interception success/failure paths, blackmail effect matrix (including exposure risk/backfire), forgery detectability, and composer refusing intimate modules below threshold. Full `pnpm test:run` passes with count ≥333. Typecheck clean.

## Finding coverage
- Directly fulfills the 4th bead request in the sensual/intimacy/intrigue cluster.
- Covers living-world gap (NPC↔NPC private communication as proactive agents).
- Provides the missing medium for the power-dynamics and intrigue work already in flight (dbk6, rjyy/58n5, bond cluster).
- Aligns with content-policy update (destiny-vvfh) and GDD sensual/relationships pillars.

## Fiction contract
Private correspondence in Valdenmoor is a physical, traceable, and weaponizable medium. Named NPCs write letters to one another — not only to the player — as part of pursuing their own agendas: desire, alliance, grievance, ambition, revenge, or simple need for connection in a dangerous city. These letters exist and move (or fail to move) independently of player action; the player may discover them, commission forgeries, intercept deliveries, withhold them as leverage, or become the target of their contents.

The emotional and sensual register of any letter is strictly determined by the actual directed relationship depth, history, and nature between its specific sender and recipient. It is never generic, never front-loaded, and never applied uniformly. When relationship depth and context permit, letters default to the starkly suggestive register ("stark andeutend") with visible emotional, political, relational, or mechanical consequences for the correspondents and any third party who learns the contents. More direct or sinnlich consensual language is permissible only in moments where preceding investment, specific character voice, shared history, and narrative necessity for the consequence have made the directness earned, tonally coherent with the dark fantasy setting, and necessary rather than decorative.

Every letter carries provenance (sender, recipient or intended recipient, approximate timing or triggering event, delivery vector or courier) that makes forgery, selective delivery, and blackmail first-class interactions. Withholding a letter or threatening exposure is a recognized power move within the city's information and favor economy. NPCs treat correspondence as both intimate channel and political asset; they write, read, and react to letters between third parties when they can.

The system must preserve two-way agency even in text: both parties in a consensual intimate exchange demonstrate preference, memory, and the capacity to initiate, refuse, or alter the terms. When letters are manipulative, the calculation must be legible in subtext or in the later consequence the player (or another NPC) can trigger. Letters never override character competence or primary identity; they reveal and complicate it.

This contract applies to all future authored seed letters, procedural generation rules, discovery surfaces, and exploitation mechanics. It is the diegetic truth against which all implementation and content will be measured.
notes:
=== ENRICHMENT FROM ROLE-BASED SUBAGENTS (2026-06-01) ===

This note contains the detailed Architecture Proposal (Systems role) and Text Generation / Tone guidance (Narrative role) for the private correspondence system. These were developed specifically to make this bead (and follow-up implementation beads) concrete and safe for more restricted agents (Codex, Claude, Copilot, etc.).

--- 1. ARCHITECTURE PROPOSAL (Systems Role) ---

**Domain Model (proposed new file: src/domain/correspondence/contracts.ts)**

export const correspondenceSensitivitySchema = z.enum([
  'mundane', 'political', 'intimate', 'compromising'
])
export const correspondenceStatusSchema = z.enum([
  'sent', 'delivered', 'intercepted', 'read', 'suppressed', 'forged'
])

export const correspondenceMessageSchema = z.object({
  id: entityIdSchema,
  fromId: z.string().min(1),
  toId: z.string().min(1),
  sentOnDay: positiveIntegerSchema,
  deliveredOnDay: positiveIntegerSchema.nullable(),
  text: z.string().min(1),
  modulesUsed: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
  templateFamily: z.string().optional(),
  intimacyStageAtSend: intimacyStageSchema.optional(),
  sensitivity: correspondenceSensitivitySchema,
  authenticity: z.number().min(0).max(100).default(100),
  knownBy: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
  interceptedBy: z.string().nullable(),
  consequenceApplied: z.boolean().default(false),
})

GameState addition (minimal):
privateCorrespondence: z.array(correspondenceMessageSchema).default([])

Pure helpers (examples):
- buildCorrespondenceKey
- getCorrespondenceBetween
- findBlackmailableCorrespondence (for exploitation)

**Key Commands (new file: src/application/commands/correspondence.ts)**

Recommended thin-slice commands:
- generateAndRecordCorrespondence(state, fromId, toId, context, rng)
- processMailDelivery(state, rng)
- applyInterception(state, interceptorId, messageId)
- applyReadLetter(state, readerId, messageId)
- applyBlackmailLeverage(state, holderId, messageId, targetId)
- applyForgery(state, forgerId, templateMessageId, ...)

**Integration Points (narrow & safe)**

- applyNpcAgency.ts → new AgencyAction variant "correspondence"
- applyNpcPairing.ts → on intimacyStage advancement to attachment/committed → generate one earned intimate letter
- endDay.ts → one narrow call to processMailDelivery + consequence drift
- bondService.ts (applyBondServiceEffects) → optional low-prob "leverage note" generation for high power/fear bonds (ties directly to destiny-dbk6)
- events: extend eventOutcomeTypeSchema with 'sendCorrespondence' | 'discoverCorrespondence' | 'interceptMail'

**Exploitation as First-Class (user priority)**

- Interception/Discovery, Forgery, Blackmail/Withholding all designed as first-class mechanics.
- All paths must go through existing adjustRelationshipDelta + writeNpcMemory.

**Clean Boundaries & Risks**

- Terminology: use "correspondence" / "private letter" to avoid collision with bond terminology (see destiny-h3ba).
- All intimate/sensual module selection enforced in the pure composer.

--- 2. TEXT GENERATION & TONE GUIDANCE (Narrative Role) ---

**Core Principle**
Use authored modules + textbausteine for variety and replayability. The composer must strictly enforce the content policy and GDD Sensual Pillar.

**Recommended Generation Approach**

Data: data/definitions/correspondence-templates.json
- Module banks tagged by depthLevel, nature, and function.

Assembly rules (pure function):
1. Compute intimacy ceiling from directed relationship.
2. Filter modules by ceiling + tags + context.
3. Apply writer voice transforms.
4. Combine modules + light glue.
5. Allow one earned direct/sinnlich kernel only at high depth + justified narrative purpose.
6. Output final text + metadata.

**Definition of "direct/sinnlich" allowed register**

Specific, character-voiced, references concrete private details, carries emotional/political weight, preserves two-way agency and aftermath, grounded in the world's dark fantasy register. Never generic or front-loaded.

**Tone Guidelines by Relationship Type** (with good/bad examples in the full subagent output)

--- 3. RECOMMENDED NEXT BEAD SPLIT ---

This bead (destiny-fnhz) = capability + standards contract.

Natural follow-ups:
- Content bead: Author first module banks + seed letters (Narrative).
- Implementation bead: Contracts + composer + generation hooks (Systems).
- Exploitation bead: Full interception + blackmail mechanics.

--- 4. CROSS-REFERENCES ---

Strongly related to:
- destiny-rjyy + destiny-58n5
- destiny-dbk6, destiny-bw2h, destiny-lfxq
- destiny-vvfh
- destiny-h3ba

All exploitation paths should eventually feed the political leverage queries.


This bead is part of a larger P2 cluster addressing relationship power, intimacy, and intrigue. Related/connected work: destiny-rjyy + destiny-58n5 (politics + intrigue events), destiny-dbk6 (bond power dynamics), destiny-bw2h (bond UI), destiny-lfxq (bond creation), destiny-h3ba (terminology hygiene). The goal is to make private communication a first-class, exploitable vector for both the player and the living world.
Cross-references within cluster: destiny-e5c6 (political leverage queries should consume correspondence data) and destiny-6sx6 (holder consequences amplified by compromising letters). Full cluster: rjyy, 58n5, dbk6, bw2h, lfxq, h3ba, e5c6, 6sx6, vvfh.
Drift guard from 2026-06-02 review: this system must remain downstream of relationship depth and must not become the primary path by which players first access intimate or compromising content. Correspondence may carry, expose, or weaponize intimacy, but it must not substitute for courtship or household progression. Coordinate with destiny-jed7, destiny-tr29, and destiny-68j4.

title: Narrative capability: private correspondence (letters) as vector for intimacy, sensual content, and intrigue/manipulation (4th bead)
description:
## Why

Private correspondence appears only as incidental flavor text (events.json: Marion's discreet offers discovered as letters in the kitchen fire; pois.json: 'Sealed correspondence and small parcels moved between the noble estates' as district texture). There is no system contract, no generation model, and no authoring standard for letters as a living, interceptable channel. NPCs never write to each other; all intimate, political, or personal text is either static authored prose or flows only through the player. This starves the living-world principle, the GDD Sensual Presentation pillar (earned intimacy must have delivery vectors), the blackmail/favor networks called out in docs/narrative.md, and the bond/politics leverage work (dbk6/bw2h/lfxq/h3ba/rjyy cluster) that needs private channels for power moves and emotional consequence.

## What

This bead delivers the complete design contract and authoring standards for a private correspondence capability:
- A strong, reusable Fiction Contract that future narrative and systems work must preserve.
- A high-level proposal for modular text generation using templates + combinable textbausteine (modules) that scale variety without hand-authoring thousands of letters, while gating sensual/intimate register strictly by relationship depth and context.
- Precise definition of what 'direct/sinnlich' consensual content feels like in this world, strictly within the 2026-06-01 content-policy update (destiny-vvfh) and GDD §16.
- Tone and voice guidelines with concrete examples for three relationship dynamics: consensual deep intimacy, power-imbalanced but consensual bonds, and purely manipulative use of intimate language.

The bead itself authors only the contract and guidelines (recorded here and in bd remember keys for standards reuse). It creates no runtime, no data files, no UI, and no example letter texts.

## Why now

Direct follow-up to the 2026-06-01 consensual sensual policy clarification (destiny-vvfh) and the yellow bond/politics/sensual-leverage session that explicitly called for thin slices in the intimacy-as-political-tool space. Private letters are the lowest-friction, highest-fiction vector for:
- NPC-to-NPC communication that makes the world breathe without the player.
- Delivery of character-specific, depth-gated sensual text that feels earned rather than injected.
- Exploitation gameplay (interception, selective withholding, forgery, blackmail) that turns private words into public weapons with lasting relational and political consequences.

It also feeds the P1 fiction authoring standards epic (destiny-rhx6) by establishing a reusable modular pattern for one important text type. Filing the contract now prevents later content beads from inventing incompatible rules.

## Player impact

Players will encounter (and create) private letters whose content is unmistakably personal, voice-distinct, and tied to the actual relationship history between specific named characters. Discovery of an NPC-to-NPC intimate letter will feel like genuine intrusion into a living world. The ability to weaponize (or be victimized by) such letters will make relationship investment and political maneuvering feel risky and consequential. Earned sensual tone in correspondence will land as meaningful character expression and emotional/political payload, never as generic reward content.

## System impact

This bead: updates/expands the bead description and bd remember keys with the contract + guidelines (narrative standards layer).
Future dependent work (separate beads):
- data/definitions/ (new letter-modules/ or letters.json combinatorial banks; seeded authored NPC-to-NPC examples for key story relationships).
- domain/ (possible new contracts for CorrespondenceArtifact, LetterProvenance; integration with existing directed relationship / bondStatus models once those stabilize).
- application/ (pure commands for generateLetter, attemptDelivery, interceptLetter, forgeLetter, useAsBlackmailLeverage; wiring into endDay applyNpcAgency or event resolution for autonomous NPC letter-writing).
- gameState (transient or persisted pending/delivered/intercepted correspondence pools or intel ledger entries; schema change checklist applies if new top-level collections).
- selectors + UI (surfaces for reading discovered letters, perhaps a 'Correspondence' tab or journal section; provenance display for forgery risk).
- events/outcomes (new outcome types or choice consequences involving letters).

No schema or command changes in this bead.

## Does not include

- Implementation of any letter generation, delivery, state tracking, or exploitation commands.
- Creation of actual text module libraries or hand-authored seed letters (follow-up content bead after this contract exists).
- Any UI surfaces, modals, or journal integrations for correspondence.
- Concrete mechanics or balance for interception success rates, forgery difficulty, blackmail mechanical effects, or withholding penalties (those are thin-slice follow-ups).
- Changes to relationship schema, bondStatus, or directed bond graph (this bead assumes those exist or are in flight via the P2 cluster).
- New item families, art assets, or portrait changes.
- Expansion into dialogue trees or quest content that uses letters.

## Evidence

- Direct user request (Narrative role, 2026-06-01): '4th bead on private correspondence (letters/messages) as a vector for intimacy, sensual content, and intrigue/manipulation' with four explicit requirements: (1) strong living world — NPCs send letters to each other, not only to the player; (2) intensity per policy — stark andeutend + Konsequenzen default, manchmal auch direkt/sinnlich; (3) text generation via templates + text modules/textbausteine supporting sensual tone at appropriate depth; (4) exploitation vectors — interception, forgery, blackmail using intimate content, withholding messages as power moves.
- Precedent narrative standards bead: destiny-vvfh (added consensual sensual subsection to content-policy.md with matching Fiction Contract style).
- RPG audit recorded under key 'rpg-audit-2026-06-01-private-correspondence-bead-prep': Yellow outcome.
