- src/application/selectors/world.ts: add district selectors
- No GameState changes (districts are content definitions)

## Does not include
- Runtime district reputation changes (future mission-consequence bead)
- Player's district presence tracking
- Per-district mission board UI
- District-specific event tables

## Evidence
WB+GD world NPC social panel: 5 named districts with character; district-gated simulation as core architecture; socialDensity + rumorClimate as key district variables.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: district social profile queryable; rumorClimate visible to simulation layer; socialDensity used as tick modifier
2. System / technical: districts.json with 5 entries; all parse against districtSchema; contentCatalog loads them; selectDistrictById works
3. Tests / verification: districtSchema parses all 5 entries; contentCatalog integration test; test count ≥403

## Finding coverage
WB panel: 5 districts as social organisms. GD panel: district-gated tick design. SA panel: districtId as anchor for world entities.

title: Author 5 hidden pre-existing world NPC bonds: Vael/Maret, Doyle/Holst, Vale/Rhys, patronage, rivalry
description:
## Why
The 5 world NPC hidden relationships identified by the panel are the most important authored bonds in the game — because the player does not know about them yet. Sister Vael and Old Maret are both pre-Breach survivors who deal in information, and they have a shared witness bond. Garet Doyle sheltered Verek Holst in a winter crisis. Marion Vale's supply chain runs through a noble hand. These are not simulation outputs — they are authored facts about who this city was before the player arrived. Without authoring them into npcs.json bonds, the social simulation has no seed and the world feels constructed around the player rather than pre-existing.

## What
Author the 5 panel-confirmed hidden NPC bonds in data/definitions/npcs.json. These are hardBond entries (authored, persistent) — not soft bonds generated by simulation.

### Bond 1: Sister Vael ↔ Old Maret — shared witness
- On npc-sister-vael: { type: 'witness', targetId: 'npc-old-maret', note: 'Both present at the same Breach event. Vael does not acknowledge this publicly. She confesses it when Maret is threatened.', visibility: 'hidden' }
- On npc-old-maret: { type: 'witness', targetId: 'npc-sister-vael', note: 'Knows what Vael saw. Has kept silence for years. Calls it a debt she has not collected.', visibility: 'hidden' }

### Bond 2: Garet Doyle ↔ Verek Holst — winter debt
- On npc-garet-doyle: { type: 'old-comrade', targetId: 'npc-verek-holst', note: 'Sheltered Verek from a creditor during the Breach winter. Verek has never fully repaid it.', visibility: 'rumored' }
- On npc-verek-holst: { type: 'debtor', targetId: 'npc-garet-doyle', note: 'Owes Garet silence and a favor. Statutes he recites under his breath include one about shelter debts.', visibility: 'hidden' }

### Bond 3: Marion Vale ↔ Ida Rhys — mentorship
- On npc-marion-vale: { type: 'old-comrade', targetId: 'npc-ida-rhys', note: 'Ida learned dock-reading, household discipline, and exit-cataloguing from Marion before either of them joined the house.', visibility: 'hidden' }
- On npc-ida-rhys: { type: 'old-comrade', targetId: 'npc-marion-vale', note: 'Learned everything about not trusting a structure until you have tapped it from Marion. Will not say this directly.', visibility: 'hidden' }

### Bond 4: Marion Vale ↔ Lady Sorn's steward — quiet patronage
- On npc-marion-vale: { type: 'handler', targetId: 'npc-ghost-sorn-steward', note: 'Marion's supply chain runs partly through a noble hand she will not name. The arrangement predates her dock work.', visibility: 'hidden' }
Note: npc-ghost-sorn-steward is a ghost NPC reference — a named non-roster person. Add to a ghost NPC references list or use canonical ghost-ID prefix.

### Bond 5: House Merrow runner ↔ Lady Sorn's steward — territorial rivalry
- On npc-enemy-lady-sorn: { type: 'rival', targetId: 'npc-ghost-merrow-runner', note: 'Sorn and Merrow cooperate only when Valdris is the shared target. Beneath that, their harbor-access rivalry runs through their respective functionaries.', visibility: 'rumored' }

### Discovery mechanics (authored, not simulated)
Each hidden bond has a discoverable path:
- Sister Vael/Old Maret: fires when Maret is threatened in a mission or event
- Garet/Verek: surfaces when player asks Garet about Verek's background or when Verek owes the player a favor
- Marion/Ida: surfaces in a private dialogue when both are assigned to the same mission
- Marion/Sorn's steward: surfaces when player investigates Marion's supply chain
- Merrow/Sorn rivalry: surfaces when player reaches exteriorState tier 3 (faction visitor event)

## Why now
These bonds are the seeds of the world NPC social simulation (destiny-c3sh). Without them, the simulation starts from zero and Valdenmoor feels improvised rather than inhabited. They also directly feed: the Mira lineage quest (Vael+Maret share a Breach witness bond), the player-NPC relationship arcs (Marion+Ida), and the faction conflict model (Merrow/Sorn).

## Player impact
- The world feels pre-existing: NPCs had lives before the player arrived
- Discovery moments are earned through play, not told at the start
- Each discovered bond opens a new information path, quest hook, or leverage opportunity
- Marion's patronage bond is a potential complication for the player's trust in her

## System impact
- data/definitions/npcs.json: 5 bond pairs added to relevant NPCs (10 bond entries total)
- Ghost NPC reference prefix: npc-ghost- for non-roster named persons (Sorn's steward, Merrow runner)
- No schema changes (bond structure already defined in destiny-gxl5 and destiny-nw4m)
- visibility field added to bond entries (hardBond extension from destiny-c3sh)

## Does not include
- Dialogue authoring for discovery scenes (separate content bead per NPC)
- Runtime bond discovery tracking (future quest state bead)
- New NPC creation for ghost references (ghost IDs are string references only)
- More than the 5 panel-confirmed bonds

## Evidence
WB panel: 5 specific hidden relationships named with authored notes. ND panel: discovery methods (consequence-first, overheard, third-party report). GD panel: authored bonds as seeds for social simulation.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: bonds do not appear in NPC detail panel until visibility transitions to 'known' or 'rumored'; discovery trigger conditions authored as quest/event hooks
2. System / technical: all 5 bond pairs present in npcs.json; ghost NPC IDs use npc-ghost- prefix; bonds parse against npcDefinitionSchema; visibility field present on all 5
3. Tests / verification: npcs.json parses without error; test count does not drop below 403

## Dependencies
destiny-gxl5 (bond schema — already completed)
destiny-nw4m (bond authoring bead — this extends its scope)
destiny-c3sh (world NPC social simulation — uses these as hard bond seeds)

## Finding coverage
WB+ND world NPC social panel: 5 specific hidden pre-existing relationships with discovery paths
close_reason: Added visibility field to npcBondSchema; authored all 5 bond pairs: Vael/Maret witness, Doyle/Holst shelter debt, Vale/Rhys mentorship, Marion/Sorn patronage, Sorn/Merrow functionary rivalry. Updated Holst's existing Doyle debtor bond with winter-shelter specifics.

title: World household/establishment entity schema and 6 authored Valdenmoor locations
description:
## Why
Other significant locations in Valdenmoor (Lady Sorn's estate, House Merrow headquarters, Garet Doyle's inn, Sister Vael's chapel) have no schema representation. They exist as NPC background text only. The player cannot infiltrate them, investigate them, observe their stability, or use them as leverage. Rival households have no staff models, no resources, no conflict heat. The city's social fabric is invisible because its major nodes are unmodeled.

## What
Define a world household/establishment entity schema and author the 6 key Valdenmoor locations.

### HouseholdEntity schema (new, not part of GameState — separate world definition record)

```ts
worldHouseholdSchema = z.object({
  id: z.string(),
  name: z.string(),
  kind: z.enum(['household', 'establishment', 'faction_seat']),
  ownerNpcId: z.string().nullable().default(null),
  controllingFactionId: z.string().nullable().default(null),
  districtId: z.string(),
  memberNpcIds: z.array(z.object({
    npcId: z.string(),
    role: z.string(),
    status: z.enum(['active', 'absent', 'captive', 'dead']).default('active'),
  })).default([]),
  rooms: z.array(z.object({
    id: z.string(),
    function: z.string().nullable(),
    capacity: z.number().int().positive(),
  })).optional(),
  tags: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
  stability: z.number().int().min(0).max(100).default(50),
  reputation: z.number().int().min(0).max(100).default(50),
  security: z.number().int().min(0).max(100).default(30),
  resources: z.object({
    coin: z.number().int().nonneg().default(0),
    food: z.number().int().nonneg().default(0),
    favors: z.number().int().nonneg().default(0),
    secrets: z.number().int().nonneg().default(0),
  }).default({}),
  activeConflicts: z.array(z.object({
    targetId: z.string(),
    type: z.string(),
    severity: z.number().int().min(0).max(5).default(1),
  })).default([]),
})
```

### 6 authored world households

**1. House Sorn (household)**
- id: world-house-sorn
- districtId: district-northbank
- owner: npc-enemy-lady-sorn
- tags: ['old-money', 'legitimacy-gate', 'disappearances']
- stability: 70, reputation: 80, security: 65
- rooms: reception hall, archive wing, servant quarters, walled garden, locked cellar
- fiction: publicly elegant, privately a machine for maintaining secrets. Half its staff are there by choice. Half are not.

**2. House Merrow, Ash Quay (household)**
- id: world-house-merrow
- districtId: district-ash-quay
- owner: faction house-merrow
- tags: ['rival-lineage', 'dock-leverage', 'legitimacy-claim']
- stability: 65, reputation: 55, security: 50
- rooms: counting room, dock office, staff quarters, archive room
- fiction: looks like a successful merchant house. Functions as a bureaucratic claim-machine.

**3. The Lantern Vale Inn (establishment)**
- id: world-lantern-vale
- districtId: district-cinder-row
- owner: npc-garet-doyle
- tags: ['information-hub', 'debt-collection', 'rumor-market', 'safe-house-informal']
- stability: 55, reputation: 60, security: 20
- fiction: Garet inherited it from his father. Everyone passes through. Nothing said here stays only here.

**4. Chapel of Saint Vey (establishment)**
- id: world-chapel-saint-vey
- districtId: district-the-pale
- owner: npc-sister-vael
- tags: ['records', 'sanctuary', 'confession-network', 'birth-registry-adjacent']
- stability: 75, reputation: 70, security: 30
- fiction: the only building in the Pale that has not changed hands since before the Breach. Sister Vael's infirmary is one room. Her archive is three.

**5. The Salt Ledger Hall (faction_seat)**
- id: world-salt-ledger-hall
- districtId: district-northbank
- controllingFactionId: faction-civic-compact
- tags: ['contracts', 'warrants', 'legal-choke-point', 'debt-registry']
- stability: 80, reputation: 65, security: 60
- fiction: if a name appears in this building, it exists legally. If it does not appear, it is dead or hidden.

**6. House Sable-Cairn (household)**
- id: world-house-sable-cairn
- districtId: district-northbank
- tags: ['fallen-prestige', 'social-bridge', 'debt-hidden', 'seal-gatekeeping']
- stability: 35, reputation: 75, security: 40
- fiction: publicly devout, privately indebted. Half-servants, half-prisoners. Everyone in Valdenmoor wants their seal, their silence, or their ruin.

### Storage location
data/definitions/worldHouseholds.json — array of worldHouseholdSchema objects
Loaded via contentCatalog (same pattern as NPCs and items)

### Player interaction hooks
- Infiltration: send crew member as mission target to a world household (reads security score)
- Recruitment: poach an NPC from memberNpcIds (reads stability score)
- Destabilize: missions that reduce stability or reputation of a rival household
- Observe: selectHouseholdStatus returns public-facing stability and reputation tier

## Why now
World households give the social simulation (destiny-c3sh) physical anchors. NPC social ties need locations. Rival households give the player targets beyond individual NPCs. The player currently cannot act against House Sorn or House Merrow as organizations — only as named individuals.

## Player impact
- Rival households visible on district map with stability/reputation indicator
- Missions can target world households (infiltrate, destabilize, recruit from)
- House Sorn's stability affects legitimacy contest difficulty for the Valdris heir
- Salt Ledger Hall registration is the legal gate for Valdris heir recognition

## System impact
- data/definitions/worldHouseholds.json: create with 6 entries
- src/domain/world/contracts.ts: worldHouseholdSchema (new domain file)
- src/application/content/contentCatalog.ts: add worldHouseholds loader
- src/application/selectors/world.ts: selectHouseholdById, selectHouseholdsByDistrict, selectHouseholdStatus
- No GameState changes (world households are content definitions, not runtime state — stability/reputation changes are future mission consequence bead)

## Does not include
- Full room-level simulation for world households
- Player entering and exploring world households as locations
- Household combat mechanics
- Dynamic stability changes (future mission-consequence bead)
- Household NPC daily scheduling (that is destiny-5hsc)

## Evidence
SA panel: three entity kinds (household/establishment/faction_seat); only key places need room-level simulation. WB panel: 5-6 named world locations with authored descriptions. ND panel: House Sable-Cairn as most interesting other household. GD panel: stability + conflict heat as key household state.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: world households queryable by district; selectHouseholdStatus returns stability/reputation tier; infiltration missions read security score
2. System / technical: worldHouseholds.json with 6 entries parses against worldHouseholdSchema; contentCatalog loads it; selectHouseholdsByDistrict returns correct list
3. Tests / verification: worldHouseholdSchema parses all 6 entries; contentCatalog integration test; test count does not drop below 403

## Finding coverage
GD+ND+SA+WB world NPC social panel: three entity kinds, 6 authored locations, schema design, player interaction hooks
close_reason: Created worldHouseholdSchema in domain/world/contracts.ts, authored 6 Valdenmoor world households in data/definitions/worldHouseholds.json (Sorn, Merrow, Lantern Vale, Chapel, Salt Ledger Hall, Sable-Cairn), loaded via contentCatalog, with selectHouseholdById/selectHouseholdsByDistrict/selectHouseholdStatus selectors and 10 passing tests.

title: World NPC autonomous social simulation: soft bonds, district-gated, endDay tick
description:
## Why
World NPCs (Sister Vael, Old Maret, Garet Doyle, etc.) exist in isolation. They have no bonds to each other despite living in the same city for decades. Two elder women who both survived the Breach and both deal in information have no authored or simulated connection. The directed bond graph (destiny-13si) has the infrastructure but no world-NPC simulation logic. Valdenmoor feels like a set of disconnected individuals rather than a city with a social fabric.

## What
Add a sparse, local, event-led world NPC social simulation layer. This runs on the endDay tick and produces soft bonds between world NPCs over time. It does NOT simulate all possible pairs — it simulates only co-located, routine-overlapping, or event-adjacent NPCs.

### Soft vs hard bonds
Extend the directed bond graph (destiny-13si) with a visibility/durability layer:
- hardBond: authored, persistent, high-impact (lineage, blackmail, sworn patronage, blood-debt)
- softBond: auto-generated, ephemeral, low-cost (friendship, rivalry, grudge, debt, shared secret, romance)

softBond additions:
- strength: 0-100 (increases with proximity and shared events, decays without them)
- since: day number
- visibility: 'hidden' | 'rumored' | 'known' (how discoverable it is to the player)

### Relationship types that auto-form
friendship/trust, rivalry, grudge/enmity, mentorship, debt-bond, shared_secret, territorial_conflict, romantic (rare)

Relationship types that are authored-only (never auto-generated):
blackmail, lineage_tie, sworn_patronage, blood_debt

### Auto-formation conditions (evaluated in endDay, sparse)
Only evaluate NPC pairs that share at least one of:
- same districtId
- overlapping timeSlot routines (from destiny-5hsc)
- involvement in the same recent event (questTag or eventTag overlap)

Per eligible pair: check compatibility (trait overlap threshold), existing bond presence, district pulse roll. If threshold met: create or strengthen softBond. Max 5 active soft bonds per NPC. Oldest/weakest bond displaced if cap exceeded.

### World NPC romance (same system, rare)
Romance can auto-form between world NPCs via the same intimacyStage progression (destiny-l7zl schema). Conditions are looser than player-house rules:
- trust-equivalent strength >= 50 (lower than player house threshold of 70)
- No fear/resentment block
- No captivity state
- Shared routine overlap > 7 days
- Rare probability roll (lower than crew pairing)

The player never sees this directly — they discover it through consequence (overheard dialogue, availability shift, a third NPC's report, a price change).

### Player-facing effects of world NPC social bonds
- Availability: NPC refuses/accepts meetings based on active tensions
- Price shifts: vendors, innkeepers, ferrymen adjust based on district relationship climate
- Quest hooks: betrayals, rescue jobs, leverage, rumors surface from bond events
- Faction shifts: district trust, temple access, militia scrutiny
- Discovery: overheard dialogue, third-party report, consequence-first (door closes, price rises, witness vanishes — player learns why later)

### endDay social tick (lightweight)
1. Collect eligible NPC pairs (district + routine overlap filter)
2. For each pair: check existing bond, run formation/strengthening/decay
3. Cap: max 5 soft bonds per NPC
4. Rare event: new bond formation creates a small district rumor event
5. Romance check: only if both NPCs at strength >= 50 with no block

## Why now
destiny-pws9 (world NPC runtime state) and destiny-5hsc (district routines) are prerequisites. Once those exist, the social simulation layer can be added to endDay without new schema — only soft bond records on the graph.

## Player impact
- The world feels inhabited: NPCs have histories the player didn't cause
- Discovery is consequence-first: the player learns about bonds through effects, not a relationship UI
- Some bonds become quest hooks or mission complications
- World NPC romance is discoverable but never predicted or optimized

## System impact
- src/domain/npc/contracts.ts: add softBond flag (strength, since, visibility) to bond edge schema
- src/application/commands/endDay.ts: world NPC social tick (district-filtered pair evaluation)
- src/application/selectors/npcs.ts: selectNpcBonds, selectDiscoverableBonds (visibility=known or rumored)
- No new schema entities required beyond bond edge extension

## Does not include
- Full social AI or continuous simulation
- All-pairs relationship evaluation
- Player-controlled world NPC relationship management
- Romance between world NPCs producing heirs (world NPC pregnancy is a future content bead)
- Authored-only bond types (those are authored in npcs.json via destiny-nw4m)

## Evidence
GD panel: 3-5 active ties per NPC max, district co-presence and routine overlap as filter, event-driven not continuous. SA panel: same bond graph, soft/hard layer. WB panel: Valdenmoor runs on debt, witness, reputation, lodging, church charity, dock access.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: world NPC bonds surfaceable through consequence events; selectDiscoverableBonds returns known/rumored bonds; no direct bond UI (discovery only)
2. System / technical: softBond fields on bond edge schema; endDay social tick evaluates district-filtered pairs only; max 5 soft bonds per NPC enforced; romance check at correct threshold
3. Tests / verification: endDay social tick does not evaluate cross-district NPC pairs; bond cap displaces weakest; romance formation blocked under threshold; test count does not drop below 403

## Dependencies
destiny-13si (directed bond graph substrate)
destiny-pws9 (world NPC runtime state)
destiny-5hsc (district routines: overlap detection)
destiny-l7zl (intimacyStage schema for world NPC romance)

## Finding coverage
GD+ND+SA+WB world NPC social panel: auto-formation logic, soft/hard bond layers, relationship taxonomy, discovery methods, player effects

title: Heir legitimacy, trait inheritance, and world reaction to a Valdris heir
description:
## Why
destiny-et7w defined the heirSchema for wards. But it does not distinguish biological children from wards, has no parentRefs, no legitimacyStatus, and no trait inheritance model. A biological child of the player character is the strongest possible Valdris legitimacy claim — directly relevant to the maternity ledger quest, the rival factions, and the succession crisis. Without legitimacyStatus and parentRefs, biological heirs and taken-in wards are mechanically indistinguishable, which removes the political weight that makes lineage matter.

## What
Extend heirSchema and add the world-reaction system for when a Valdris heir becomes known.

### heirSchema extensions
Add to existing heirSchema (destiny-et7w):
- origin: z.enum(['biological', 'ward']).default('ward')
- parentRefs: z.array(z.string()).max(2).default([])  // NPC IDs, empty for ward
- legitimacyStatus: z.enum(['recognized', 'contested', 'hidden', 'unknown']).default('unknown')
- birthContext: z.string().nullable().default(null)  // authored one-liner: 'Born Day 14, mother: Marion Vale'

### Trait inheritance (biological only)
When origin is 'biological': on formalization to adult NPC, inherit 1-2 trait echoes from parentRefs:
- Pick highest trait from each parent above 60
- Apply with +/- 15 variance
- Add one possible flaw: if either parent has a stress-elevated trait (fear > 50, resentment > 50), child may inherit a mirrored vulnerability

This is computed at formalization time, not at birth — the child's potential is unknown until they grow.

### legitimacyStatus effects
- recognized: full Valdris claim weight; factions react; district notices
- contested: the claim exists but is disputed — legal battle, evidence required
- hidden: player conceals the heir; lower faction threat, lower political leverage
- unknown: default for new heirs; becomes recognized or contested through quest events

### World reaction system: the Heir Announcement scene
When legitimacyStatus transitions from hidden/unknown to recognized, an authored milestone event fires — parallel to 'three men at the gate':

'On the morning the city learns House Valdris has an heir, four things happen before noon. A Merrow functionary files a legitimacy challenge at the Compact Registry. A debt collector from Voss arrives with updated terms. Lady Sorn sends a note that says only: we should speak. And in the Hollows, Old Maret lights a candle she has kept unlit for years.'

This is the authored district reaction moment. It fires once, at first recognition. Its mechanical effects: faction hostility modifiers update, renown increases, House Merrow enters active opposition state.

### Faction responses to a recognized Valdris heir
- House Merrow: legitimacy challenge filed (new quest event)
- Harlen Voss: debt terms updated (pressure increases)
- Lady Sorn: direct contact initiated (political scene unlocked)
- The Hollows district: positive reputation shift (Old Maret's candle = district mood marker)
- Civic Compact: birth registration check (Orven Pell and Cessa Rill become relevant)

## Why now
The maternity ledger quest (destiny-qx1v), the romance arc (destiny-l7zl), and the NPC pairing system (destiny-gxov) all produce heirs. Without legitimacyStatus and the world-reaction system, those heirs land silently — no faction cares, no district reacts, no political weight is produced. This bead makes the heir the narrative event it should be.

## Player impact
- Biological heir shows parentRefs and birthContext in house panel
- legitimacyStatus is player-visible and changeable (hidden vs recognized is a choice)
- Choosing to recognize the heir triggers the authored district reaction scene
- Factions visibly respond: Merrow challenge, Voss pressure, Sorn contact
- Trait inheritance visible when heir is formalized to adult

## System impact
- src/domain/game/contracts.ts: extend heirSchema with origin, parentRefs, legitimacyStatus, birthContext
- data/runtime/initial-game-state.json: houseHeirs entries updated with new fields (all default safe)
- src/application/commands/recognizeHeir.ts: new command; transitions legitimacyStatus; fires world-reaction event
- src/application/commands/formalizeHeir.ts: extended to apply trait inheritance from parentRefs
- src/application/selectors/house.ts: selectHeirLegitimacyWeight: returns political weight modifier
- Authored milestone event: 'The Heir Announcement' scene (four reactions, one per faction/district)

## Does not include
- Full succession law mechanics
- Multiple competing heir claims
- Heir-specific skill trees before adulthood
- Marriage/partnership formalization
- More than 2 heirs in houseHeirs

## Evidence
GD panel: legitimacyStatus, parentRefs, birthContext on heirSchema; same schema for biological and ward. ND panel: a child is a claim made flesh — weaponized continuity. WB panel: birth registration matters, legitimacy can be contested, faction reactions are specific and named. SA panel: partial trait inheritance at formalization time.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: houseHeirs panel shows origin, parentRefs, legitimacyStatus, birthContext; recognizeHeir triggers authored district reaction scene; factions respond with named reactions; trait inheritance shown at formalization
2. System / technical: heirSchema extended with all 4 new fields; recognizeHeir command transitions status and fires event; formalizeHeir applies trait inheritance from parentRefs; selectHeirLegitimacyWeight returns correct modifier; initial-game-state.json updated with safe defaults
3. Tests / verification: heirSchema parses with new fields; recognizeHeir blocked if already recognized; trait inheritance uses correct parent trait values; test count does not drop below 403

## Dependencies
destiny-et7w (base heirSchema and houseHeirs — this bead extends it)
destiny-l7zl (player romance arc — biological heirs arrive from here)
destiny-gxov (NPC-NPC pairing — biological heirs arrive from here)
destiny-4n2j (pregnancyState — resolution triggers heir creation)
destiny-h2vi (Orven Pell, Cessa Rill — birth registration witnesses)
destiny-9lfm (House Merrow — legitimacy challenge source)

## Finding coverage
GD+ND+SA+WB lineage panel: heir schema extension, legitimacyStatus, trait inheritance, world-reaction scene, faction responses

## Fiction contract
Recognizing a Valdris heir is not a warm family moment. It is a political declaration. It tells every enemy of the house that the line will not end with you. It also tells them they have to act — and they will.
close_reason: Heir legitimacy system: heirSchema extended with legitimacyStatus+birthContext, recognizeHeir command fires heir-announcement event with faction consequences, selectHeirLegitimacyWeight selector, formalizeHeir command with trait inheritance. 17 tests pass.

title: NPC-to-NPC autonomous relationships: house policy, pairing, and biological heirs
description:
## Why
NPCs living together in House Valdris have no model for autonomous relationship formation. Two crew members who share meals, watches, and danger cannot develop an attachment the game acknowledges. There is no NPC-to-NPC romance bond type, no house policy for the player to set, and no authored moment when an NPC pairing produces a child. The house feels like a staffing roster, not a household. People who live this way fall for each other. The game should know that.

## What
Add NPC-to-NPC autonomous relationship formation and a player house policy that governs it.

### How NPC-NPC intimacy forms (auto, time-gated)
NPCs develop romantic bonds autonomously over time if:
- Both are assigned to the house (not on field missions)
- Shared location days above threshold (default: 14 days proximity)
- Compatible trait check: high empathy x high empathy, or high loyalty x high loyalty, or curiosity x curiosity overlap — at least one axis compatibility
- No blocking conditions: dominance imbalance above threshold, active fear bond between them, incompatible faction enmity
- Player house policy is not 'forbidden'
- Neither NPC is in an active captivity aftermath state

Bond progression: affinity -> attachment -> committed (same intimacyStage model as player romance arc).

### House policy
Add npcPairingPolicy to houseStateSchema:
npcPairingPolicy: z.enum(['open', 'discouraged', 'forbidden']).default('open')

open: NPC pairings can form and progress naturally
discouraged: NPC pairings can form but pregnancy window never opens
forbidden: NPC bond type 'romantic' is blocked from forming

Player can change policy via house management action.

### NPC-NPC pregnancy
When two NPCs reach 'committed' and npcPairingPolicy is 'open':
- Pregnancy probability check runs (low, not exposed to player, not predictable)
- If triggered: pregnancyState set on one NPC (context: 'consensual')
- Player discovers through authored house event (midwife, the NPCs themselves, or a third crew member)
- Player choices: support / assign protected duty / prepare space / no response

### Pregnancy resolution
On birth: houseHeirs entry created with:
- origin: 'biological'
- parentRefs: [npcId1, npcId2]
- legitimacyStatus: 'recognized' (if both parents are crew) or 'hidden' (if players sets policy to discouraged)
- stage: 'child'

Max houseHeirs still applies (max 2). If at capacity, birth event still fires but heir is not added — player must make space or the child is not raised in the house.

### Crew effects of NPC pairings
- Paired NPCs: morale/loyalty modifier when working in same location
- A breakup (bondType broken): trust/resentment shift, possible temporary duty disruption
- A pregnancy: morale ripple through crew — hope, envy, protectiveness, or worry depending on individual NPC traits
- A birth: one-time morale event for the whole house

## Why now
NPC-NPC relationships give the house texture and consequence. Crew morale becomes personal. Player house policy becomes a real governance choice. The heir system (destiny-et7w) needs biological children from NPC pairings to be fully populated. This is also the 'natural depth' the creative lead identified: people who share survival fall for each other.

## Player impact
- NPC detail panel shows intimacyStage when NPCs are in a pairing
- House policy toggle visible in house management view
- Player discovers NPC pregnancy through authored event, not a notification
- Crew morale visibly affected by pairings and births
- Heir from NPC pairing enters houseHeirs with parents named

## System impact
- src/domain/game/contracts.ts: add npcPairingPolicy to houseStateSchema
- data/runtime/initial-game-state.json: add npcPairingPolicy: 'open'
- src/domain/npc/contracts.ts: 'romantic' bond type and intimacyStage (from player romance arc bead — shared schema)
- src/application/commands/endDay.ts: NPC proximity check + bond progression tick; pregnancy probability check at committed + open policy
- src/application/commands/setHousePolicy.ts: new command for npcPairingPolicy change
- Authored house events: minimum 2 (NPC pairing acknowledged by third crew member; birth announcement)

## Does not include
- Player controlling which specific NPCs pair (house policy is categorical, not per-pair)
- NPC divorce or separation mechanics
- More than 2 heirs in houseHeirs regardless of NPC pairings
- Detailed simulation of NPC intimacy
- Pregnancy guaranteed at committed stage (probability check, not certainty)

## Evidence
GD panel: auto-enabled after player authorization via policy; encourage/discourage/forbid as house actions. ND panel: dangerous tenderness under pressure; crew morale effects of pairings. SA panel: time-gated + proximity-gated + trait compatibility check. WB panel: people living this way fall for each other.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: NPC intimacyStage visible on detail panel; house policy toggle in management view; NPC pregnancy discovered through authored event; birth creates houseHeirs entry with both parents named
2. System / technical: npcPairingPolicy on houseStateSchema and initial-game-state.json; endDay runs proximity + bond progression + pregnancy check; setHousePolicy command; pregnancyState set with context 'consensual'; heirSchema entry on birth with origin 'biological' and parentRefs
3. Tests / verification: endDay advances intimacyStage at threshold; pregnancy check blocked when policy is 'forbidden'; heir created with correct parentRefs; test count does not drop below 403

## Dependencies
destiny-13si (directed bond graph: romantic bond type and intimacyStage)
destiny-4n2j (pregnancyState schema)
destiny-et7w (heirSchema: biological origin, parentRefs)
destiny-l7zl (player romance arc: shared 'romantic' bond type and intimacyStage schema)
destiny-4wlm (room functions: quarters/barracks determine proximity/shared-location model)

## Finding coverage
GD+ND+SA+WB lineage panel: NPC-NPC autonomous relationship formation, house policy, pregnancy resolution, crew effects

## Fiction contract
NPCs who share this house are not staff. They are people surviving the same ruin. Some of them will find each other in it. The player governs whether that is allowed to matter — and what it costs the house when it does.
notes:
## Update (2026-05-14): compatibility system integration + content policy compliance

**Dependency chain clarification:**
destiny-gxov depends on destiny-z11o (calculateBaseCompatibility) being shipped first. The NPC pairing conditions in this bead should reference the compatibility score as a gate — two NPCs with compatibility score < -10 should not autonomously form a romantic bond even if proximity thresholds are met. Add this to the formation conditions:
- compatibilityScore(A, B) >= -10 (calculated via calculateBaseCompatibility)

**Content policy compliance audit (docs/content-policy.md, round 2 panel):**

The current bead description says: "Pregnancy probability check runs (low, not exposed to player, not predictable)". This is compliant with the OUT clause ("pregnancy as player-optimizable mechanic"). However the following language must be added explicitly:

POLICY GATES (non-negotiable):
1. The pregnancy probability value must never be surfaced in any UI, tooltip, or NPC detail panel — not even indirectly via "relationship depth" meters that players can read as a fertility indicator.
2. The house policy 'discouraged' prevents the pregnancy window from opening — but its existence must not be described to the player as a "pregnancy prevention" tool. It should be framed as "relationship depth is not pursued to that stage" — not as contraception.
3. If two NPCs reach 'committed' and policy is 'open', pregnancy may eventually occur — but not every committed pair produces a pregnancy. The probability must be low enough that it feels like an event, not an outcome.
4. pregnancyState.context must be set to 'consensual' for all cases originating from this system.

**Failure mode audit:**
- Women in Refrigerators: NPC pairings produce their own story, not motivation for the player character. The birth event centers the parents, not the player's feelings.
- Pregnancy as Moral Cudgel: The birth event must author the parents' responses (joy, fear, uncertainty) and not simply present the event as player pressure.

**House policy framing guidance (for authored UI text):**
- 'open': "The house does not intervene in personal bonds."
- 'discouraged': "Deep bonds are expected to remain private and uncomplicated."
- 'forbidden': "The house requires professional distance between its members."

None of these should reference pregnancy directly. The player understands the implication; the house states its policy in organizational terms.
close_reason: NPC-to-NPC autonomous relationship formation implemented: npcPairingPolicy on houseStateSchema, applyNpcPairing command (proximity+compatibility+fear gates, stage progression, pregnancy check), setNpcPairingPolicy command, partnerNpcId on pregnancyState for heir tracking, 2 authored events, 15 tests pass.

title: Player-to-NPC romance arc: intimacy stages, commitment scene, legacy window
description:
## Why
The player has no way to form a romantic relationship with an NPC. The directed bond graph (destiny-13si) has no 'romantic' bond type. There is no intimacy arc, no commitment scene, no pregnancy window for deliberate player choice. An heir born to the player character and an NPC would carry the strongest possible legitimacy claim in the Valdris succession context — but no system exists to model this. The lineage system is incomplete without intentional player-driven lineage.

## What
Add a player-to-NPC romance arc system: a gated relationship progression ending in a commitment scene that opens a pregnancy window if both parties are willing.

### Bond type addition
Add 'romantic' to the directed bond type enum (destiny-13si schema).

### Intimacy stage on bond edge
Add intimacyStage to directed relationship records:
intimacyStage: z.enum(['none', 'affinity', 'attachment', 'committed']).default('none')

### Conditions to reach 'committed' stage
- trust >= 70
- loyalty >= 40
- fear < 35
- resentment < 25
- bondType: 'romantic' established on both directed edges
- at least one authored commitment scene completed
- no active crisis lockout (active raid, captivity, critical mission)

### The 4 authored romance beats (mandatory for any NPC romance arc)
1. Recognition: the NPC notices the player as more than employer/debtor/ally. Authored as a small dialogue moment tied to a gameplay event.
2. Exposure: private scene — shared vulnerability, incompatible duties, or a risk survived together. Player has a choice that deepens or ends the arc.
3. Choice: player commits, hesitates, or weaponizes the bond. This is the commitment scene gate.
4. Consequence: intimacy window opens (pregnancyState becomes possible), the bond deepens, or it fractures. Authored differently per NPC.

### Pregnancy window
After committed stage: pregnancyState can be set on the NPC with context: 'consensual'. This is player-initiated via explicit choice action — never silent auto-trigger. The player chooses 'pursue legacy' or equivalent. Result: pregnancyState.daysElapsed begins ticking. On resolution: houseHeirs entry created with origin: 'biological'.

### Suitable candidates
Romance arcs must be authored per NPC. Not all NPCs are romantic candidates. Excluded: wards, pure dependents, fear-bond-only NPCs, NPCs in active captivity aftermath state, NPCs whose tone would collapse the game's dark register into wish-fulfillment. Included: NPCs with sufficient agency, moral complexity, and authored reason to entangle with the player's survival arc.

## Why now
An heir born to the player character is the strongest possible Valdris legitimacy claim — directly relevant to the maternity ledger quest (destiny-qx1v) and the rival factions (House Merrow, Lady Sorn, Harlen Voss). Without this system, the lineage theme of the main quest has no personal embodiment for the player.

## Player impact
- Player can pursue a relationship with an appropriate NPC across 4 authored beats
- intimacyStage visible on NPC detail panel at 'attachment' and 'committed' stages
- Commitment unlocks a 'pursue legacy' choice (explicit, not passive)
- Pregnancy: player sees pregnancyState update; heir enters houseHeirs after birth event
- Heir has parentRefs linking to both parents, legitimacyStatus: 'recognized' (strongest claim), partial trait inheritance

## System impact
- src/domain/npc/contracts.ts: add 'romantic' to bond type enum; add intimacyStage to bond edge schema
- src/domain/game/contracts.ts: no change (houseHeirs already in destiny-et7w)
- src/application/commands/advanceRomanceArc.ts: new command, validates conditions, advances intimacyStage
- src/application/commands/pursuePlayerLegacy.ts: new command, sets pregnancyState on NPC (context: 'consensual')
- data/definitions/npcs.json: romance arc eligibility flag per NPC (romanceEligible: boolean)
- Authored scene scripts: minimum 4 beats per eligible NPC (content bead per NPC)

## Does not include
- Romance as a dialogue tree or relationship sim
- More than 4 authored beats per NPC
- Guarantees of pregnancy (window opens, not guaranteed)
- Marriage mechanics or formal partnership system
- Unlocking romance for NPCs not authored for it

## Evidence
GD panel: gated relationship arc, player-led, explicit opt-in at pregnancy window. ND panel: 4 beats, no bloat, tied to gameplay pressure. WB panel: heir is weaponized continuity, legally significant, faction-threatening.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: intimacyStage shown at attachment/committed on NPC panel; commitment scene triggers authored text; 'pursue legacy' action available at committed stage; heir appears in houseHeirs after birth
2. System / technical: 'romantic' bond type and intimacyStage in schema; advanceRomanceArc validates all conditions; pursuePlayerLegacy sets pregnancyState with context 'consensual'; heirSchema entry created on resolution with origin 'biological' and parentRefs
3. Tests / verification: advanceRomanceArc rejects when conditions not met; pursuePlayerLegacy blocked below committed stage; heir created correctly on resolution; test count does not drop below 403

## Dependencies
destiny-13si (directed bond graph: romantic bond type and intimacyStage live on bond edges)
destiny-4n2j (pregnancyState schema)
destiny-et7w (heirSchema: biological origin and parentRefs)

## Finding coverage
GD+ND+SA+WB lineage panel: player romance arc conditions, 4 beats, pregnancy window, trait inheritance, faction implications

## Fiction contract
A romance in House Valdris is not warmth in a cold world. It is an alliance, a risk, a debt of a different kind. The player choosing legacy is choosing to make someone else responsible for continuing what may not deserve to continue. That weight must be present in every authored beat.
notes:
## Update (2026-05-14): content policy tension + policy compliance gates

**Policy tension (requires design resolution before implementation):**

The current bead describes a "pursue legacy" player-initiated action that sets pregnancyState on an NPC. The content policy (docs/content-policy.md) lists as OUT: "Pregnancy as player-triggered or player-optimizable outcome."

These are in direct tension. Resolution options:

Option A (player-initiated, policy-compatible framing):
The "pursue legacy" action is not framed as "try to conceive." It is framed as a commitment scene where both parties express willingness to build a life together. The pregnancyState then emerges from the world simulation — not as an immediate response to the action, but as something that may happen over the weeks and months following the commitment. The probability is low, not exposed, and not repeatable until conditions reset. The player chose commitment; the world decided the rest.

Option B (fully emergent):
Remove the "pursue legacy" explicit choice entirely. At committed stage, the world may generate pregnancyState with context: 'consensual' — same as NPC-to-NPC pairing. The player did not press a button. They built a relationship. The world responded.

**Recommended: Option A.** The commitment scene is narratively important and should be an authored beat. But the pregnancy must not be the immediate outcome of pressing a button. The scene culminates in commitment; pregnancy emerges from the simulation afterward, at low probability, over time.

**Required policy compliance language for this bead's implementation:**
- pregnancyState is set by the world simulation, not directly by the pursuePlayerLegacy command
- pursuePlayerLegacy sets a flag `legacyIntentActive: true` on the relationship edge; the pregnancy probability check runs monthly when this flag is set
- The probability check is never surfaced to the player
- legacyIntentActive flag does not appear in any UI as a "trying for a child" status
- pregnancyState.context = 'consensual' for all player-romance-origin pregnancies

**Compatibility dependency:**
This bead should also depend on destiny-z11o (calculateBaseCompatibility). The eligible NPC list for player romance arcs should include a compatibility check: the arc is more likely to develop with an NPC the personality system would predict chemistry with. Not a hard gate (some friction can be romantic), but a factor in the authored beat design.

**Four failure mode audit:**
- Stockholm Syndrome Shortcut: eligible NPC list must not include any NPC whose primary relationship history with the player was coercive or fear-based. Fear axis > 40 on the NPC's directed edge toward the player should be a hard exclusion gate.
- Women in Refrigerators: the romance arc must be as much about the NPC's arc as the player's. Each of the 4 authored beats must include authored content from the NPC's perspective — what this means for them, not just for the player's legacy goals.
close_reason: Romance arc implemented: intimacyStage + bondType + legacyIntentActive on relationship edges; advanceRomanceArc and pursuePlayerLegacy commands; tickPregnancyProgress creates biological heir; Ida and Marion marked romanceEligible; 1016 tests passing

title: Author content policy document: dark themes, captivity, sexual content
description:
## Why
Three multi-role panels produced a content policy for dark themes in Project Destiny. That policy exists only in chat history. Future writers, designers, and agents working on quest content, NPC dialogue, and captivity events have no written reference for what is allowed, what must be implied only, and what is out.

## What
Author docs/content-policy.md — the authoritative content policy for the writing room.

### The principle
Model aftermath, not exploitation. Dark content earns its place through consequence and player choice — not through what it depicts, but through what it forces the player to confront. If a state does not create a new decision, it probably should not exist.

### Content tiers

IN (fully modeled, player-facing):
- Imprisonment and captivity states (captivityState schema)
- Psychological damage and trauma debuffs
- Ideological and political corruption
- Trauma bonding (damaged attachment / survival behavior, NOT romance framing)
- Consensual relationships formed after captivity has ended
- Captivity degradation over time (condition worsening by timeHeldDays)
- NPC coercion-risk model: player protects vulnerable NPCs, not the reverse
- Death, grief, bereavement
- Raid, extortion, blackmail, debt enforcement
- Moral compromise with visible costs
- Pregnancy from free consensual relationship (rare, sustained narrative weight required)
- Pregnancy as rare world-generated captivity aftermath (context: 'unknown', never player-triggered or optimized, discovered through authored event only, player choices: protect/investigate/conceal/confront)

IMPLIED ONLY (state exists, player is told something happened, no active mechanics):
- Past sexual violence as NPC backstory (brief, indirect, consequence-focused, never a twist or collectible)
- Coercive intimacy (state and dialogue, not dramatized as romance or mechanic)
- Captivity aftereffects that imply sustained abuse without simulation
- The coercion linkage behind a pregnancyState.context = 'unknown' (never surfaced as label)

OUT (never in any bead, content, or mechanic):
- Sexual violence as an active, repeatable gameplay system
- Pregnancy as a player-triggered or player-optimizable outcome
- Probability of captivity pregnancy exposed in any UI, tooltip, or stat
- Any feedback loop where sending an NPC into captivity produces an expected pregnancy outcome
- Coerced-consent as a player-facing label or mechanic category
- Trauma bonding presented as romance or desirable outcome
- Any mechanic that rewards captivity, coercion, or abuse
- Graphic depiction of assault
- Women as pure motivation objects for the protagonist (women in refrigerators)
- Stockholm syndrome as a lazy narrative explanation

### The authored discovery moment (mandatory for pregnancy aftermath)
Discovery of pregnancy aftermath must be: quiet, somber, consequence-first. Through a healer, guard report, or the NPC herself. Immediate player choices: protect / investigate / conceal / confront captor. No reward framing. No optimization signal. No "you handled this correctly" feedback.

### Content warnings (mandatory)
Upfront before play + optional detailed list in settings.
Warn for: captivity, coercion, psychological trauma, implied sexual violence, corruption/body horror, forced dependency, manipulation, pregnancy themes.

### The four failure modes (named — every writer must know these)
1. Women in refrigerators: violating a woman to motivate the protagonist. The world stops being about her.
2. Torture as aesthetic: lingered suffering, elegant misery, implied eroticization. Asking the player to admire abuse.
3. Stockholm syndrome shortcut: she stayed so she must love him. Collapses power imbalance into melodrama.
4. Pregnancy as moral cudgel: used only to force pity, shame, or plot convenience. The character becomes a container for stakes, not a person.

### Cross-references
- Mira arc: corruption + captivity primary, coercive bond secondary. See destiny-qx1v.
- captivityState + pregnancyState schema: see destiny-4n2j.
- NPC vulnerability / coercion-risk protection: see destiny-w4nr.

## Why now
Every quest bead, NPC content bead, and captivity event going forward needs this reference. Without it, dark content decisions will be re-litigated in every content bead.

## Player impact
None directly. Team-facing document. Indirectly: the game maintains a consistent, trustworthy tone because every writer works from the same policy.

## System impact
- docs/content-policy.md: create this file
- No code or schema changes

## Does not include
- Legal compliance review
- Age-rating assessment
- Marketing copy

## Evidence
Three full panel discussions: NPC captivity panel (ND+GD+TC round 1), coerced-consent panel (ND+GD+SA+TC), captivity pregnancy world-state panel (ND+GD+SA+TC round 2). Round 2 revised the pregnancy verdict: world-generated aftermath IN as implied state; player-driven mechanic remains OUT.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: N/A (team document)
2. System / technical: docs/content-policy.md exists; covers all four tiers; names the four failure modes; documents the mandatory discovery moment pattern; cross-references destiny-qx1v, destiny-4n2j, destiny-w4nr
3. Tests / verification: N/A (documentation)

## Finding coverage
All captivity and dark-theme panels (rounds 1 and 2): full content policy including revised pregnancy verdict

title: NPC vulnerability and coercion-risk protection system
description:
## Why
NPCs with low resolve, high fear, or high dependency are more susceptible to psychological harm and exploitation during captivity. Currently the game has no model for this susceptibility and no player-facing protection mechanic. Vulnerability is invisible, so it cannot create meaningful management choices.

## What
Add a stat-driven coercion-risk model. This is a PROTECTION system — the player's job is to reduce exposure to risk, not to exploit vulnerability.

Vulnerability score (computed, not stored):
coercionRisk = ((100 - resolve) x 0.4 + fear x 0.3 + (100 - dominance) x 0.2 + loyalty x 0.1) / 100
Result: 0.0 (resilient) to 1.0 (highly vulnerable)

When coercion-risk matters:
High coercionRisk NPCs in captivity degrade faster (condition step threshold halved), shift bondType toward fear/dependency faster, and break compliance sooner. Also: coercionRisk is used as one factor in the rare world-generated aftermath check in rescueNpc (see destiny-4n2j). The probability is NEVER exposed to the player. No UI shows it. No action targets it.

Player-facing protection choices:
1. Assignment: High-risk NPCs can be assigned protected duty (house interior only, no field missions)
2. Resolve building: Training or rest actions raise resolve over time
3. Trust building: High player-NPC trust acts as partial protection modifier
4. Recovery: After rescue, vulnerable NPCs take longer to recover; debuffs last longer

What a weak moment means in Valdenmoor fiction (ND): exhaustion, grief, captivity aftereffects, magical coercion, blackmail, isolation, manipulative obligation — not a mechanical stat switch that makes an NPC sexually available. The model maps to psychological resilience under sustained threat.

## Pregnancy as world-generated aftermath — the revised panel position
Panel round 2 (ND+GD+SA+TC) revised the prior verdict. The distinction that holds:

OUT (unchanged): pregnancy as a player-driven mechanic. Player cannot trigger, optimize, or farm captivity pregnancy. No UI. No probability display. No feedback loop.

IN (revised): pregnancy as a rare world-generated aftermath of captivity exploitation. Set only by event resolution in rescueNpc. coercionRisk is one contributing factor (alongside timeHeldDays and captorType). The result uses pregnancyState.context = 'unknown' — the coercion link is never surfaced as a player-facing label.

The authored discovery moment: healer report, guard observation, or the NPC herself. Quiet, somber, consequence-first. Player choices: protect / investigate / conceal / confront captor. No reward framing. No optimization language. Rare.

## Why now
captivityState (destiny-4n2j) models the what. coercionRisk models the how fast and contributes to the aftermath probability. Without this, all captive NPCs degrade identically regardless of character — which removes the meaning of NPC traits in the survival layer.

## Player impact
- High-resolve NPCs resist captivity longer and return with shallower damage
- Resilience label on NPC detail panel: resilient / vulnerable / at risk (label only, no number)
- Assigning a vulnerable NPC to a high-exposure mission is a visible management risk
- Recovery time after rescue is visibly longer for vulnerable NPCs
- Pregnancy aftermath, if present: discovered through authored event, never predicted or optimized

## System impact
- src/application/selectors/npcs.ts: selectNpcCoercionRisk(npc) computed score
- src/application/commands/endDay.ts: captivity degradation uses coercionRisk as modifier
- src/application/commands/rescueNpc.ts: rare aftermath check uses coercionRisk as one input factor
- src/ui/: NPC detail panel shows resilience label only
- No schema additions (computed from existing trait fields)

## Does not include
- Player-triggered pregnancy
- Probability exposure or any UI around pregnancy likelihood
- coerced-consent as a player-facing label
- Any mechanic that rewards exploiting vulnerable NPCs
- Vulnerability as a stat players min-max for non-protective purposes

## Evidence
GD+ND+SA+TC panel round 1: protection system, not exploitation mechanic. Round 2 revision: world-generated aftermath is a valid dark fantasy state when non-optimizable, non-exposed, and emotionally restrained. TC: rare, brief, sober, no reward loop, no player instruction around causing it.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: resilience label on NPC detail panel; vulnerable NPCs degrade faster in captivity; protective assignment prevents field exposure; pregnancy aftermath discovered through authored event only
2. System / technical: selectNpcCoercionRisk computed from existing trait fields; endDay captivity tick uses modifier; rescueNpc aftermath check uses coercionRisk as one factor with probability never exposed; no new schema fields
3. Tests / verification: selectNpcCoercionRisk returns correct relative values; high-resolve NPC degrades slower than low-resolve in captivity; test count does not drop below 403

## Dependencies
destiny-4n2j (captivityState + pregnancyState schema — coercionRisk modifies degradation pipeline and feeds aftermath check)

## Finding coverage
GD+ND+TC+SA captivity panels (both rounds): stat-driven vulnerability, protection mechanic, revised pregnancy verdict

title: captivityState schema on npcRuntimeState
description:
## Why
NPCs can go missing, be captured during missions, be held by enemy factions, or return changed. Currently npcRuntimeState has no model for this. There is no captive status, no condition field, no compliance axis, no bond type, and no model for what captivity leaves behind. The Mira arc (destiny-qx1v) requires captivityState to model her discovery state. World NPC captivity during missions has no mechanical representation — outcomes are flat (rescued/not rescued) with no NPC-level consequence.

## What
Add captivityState and pregnancyState as optional nested objects on npcRuntimeStateSchema.

### captivityState

```ts
export const captivityStateSchema = z.object({
  status: z.enum(['missing', 'captive', 'rescued', 'returned', 'dead']),
  holderId: z.string().nullable().default(null),
  condition: z.enum(['healthy', 'hurt', 'broken', 'altered']).default('healthy'),
  compliance: z.enum(['resistant', 'conflicted', 'compliant']).default('resistant'),
  bondType: z.enum(['none', 'fear', 'dependency', 'affection', 'coercion']).default('none'),
  timeHeldDays: z.number().int().nonneg().default(0),
  questTag: z.string().nullable().default(null),
}).optional()
```

### pregnancyState

```ts
export const pregnancyStateSchema = z.object({
  context: z.enum(['consensual', 'unknown']),
  daysElapsed: z.number().int().nonneg().default(0),
  questTag: z.string().nullable().default(null),
}).optional()
```

context: 'unknown' is used when pregnancy results from captivity. The coercion linkage is never surfaced as a player-facing label — it lives only in hidden provenance (questTag or internal event log). context: 'consensual' is for freely chosen relationships.

### Recovery states after rescue
- trauma debuff: temporary skill penalties (existing modifier system)
- loyalty shift: trust axis drops
- fear shift: fear axis rises
- condition-to-modifier mapping: 'broken' = -20 all skills temporary; 'altered' = changed trait values

### Captivity degradation over time
Each day captive: condition can worsen at threshold (every 7 days without rescue = condition step down). At 'broken': rescue difficulty rises. Checked in endDay processing pipeline.

### Pregnancy as world-generated aftermath (PANEL DECISION)
pregnancyState may be set when an NPC is rescued from captivity, as a rare world-generated consequence — never as a player-triggered or player-optimizable outcome. Probability is NEVER exposed to the player. No UI or action increases it. No feedback loop where sending an NPC into captivity produces an expected pregnancy outcome. Discovery occurs through healer, guard report, or the NPC herself. Player choices on discovery: protect / investigate / conceal / confront captor.

pregnancyState is NEVER set by direct player action. It is set only by event resolution logic as a rare aftermath flag.

## Why now
destiny-qx1v (Mira arc) needs captivityState to model her discovery state. destiny-pws9 (world NPC runtime state) is the parent schema. Without this, any mission that results in NPC capture has no state to write to and no aftermath to model.

## Player impact
- Missing NPCs show a 'missing' badge with days elapsed
- Rescued NPCs carry visible recovery state (debuffs) with authored explanation
- Time pressure: the player sees condition worsen if rescue is delayed
- Pregnancy state, if present: discovered through authored event, not a UI flag. Player faces support/investigation/protection choices.
- No explicit simulation of captivity events — player sees state, not acts

## System impact
- src/domain/npc/contracts.ts: add captivityStateSchema, pregnancyStateSchema, add both optional fields to npcRuntimeStateSchema
- data/runtime/initial-game-state.json: no change (both fields optional, default undefined)
- src/application/commands/endDay.ts: captivity degradation tick; coercionRisk modifier applied
- src/application/commands/rescueNpc.ts: clears captivityState, applies recovery modifiers, runs rare aftermath check (pregnancyState may be set here as world event)
- src/application/selectors/npcs.ts: selectCaptiveNpcs, selectNpcCaptivityState

## Does not include
- Player-triggered pregnancy mechanics
- Probability exposure or UI around pregnancy likelihood
- Detailed simulation of captivity acts
- Assault depicted graphically
- Any mechanic that rewards captivity or exploitation
- coerced-consent as a player-facing label or category

## Evidence
GD+ND+SA+TC captivity panel (round 2): world-generated captivity aftermath pregnancy is a valid dark fantasy state. Mechanic-driven pregnancy remains OUT. Schema: pregnancyState optional with context enum. Probability never exposed.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: Missing NPCs show status and days elapsed; rescued NPCs show recovery debuffs with duration; pregnancy state discovered through authored event only, never a direct UI flag
2. System / technical: captivityStateSchema and pregnancyStateSchema added to npc/contracts.ts; both optional on npcRuntimeStateSchema; endDay ticks condition degradation using coercionRisk modifier; rescueNpc clears captivityState and applies recovery; pregnancyState set only by event resolution, never direct player action
3. Tests / verification: captivityState parses correctly; endDay degrades condition at threshold; rescueNpc clears state; pregnancyState requires context field; test count does not drop below 403

## Dependencies
destiny-pws9 (world NPC runtime state — captivityState lives here)

## Finding coverage
GD+ND+TC+SA captivity panels (both rounds): captivityState schema, recovery states, degradation model, pregnancy as rare world-generated aftermath
notes:
## Update (2026-05-14): pregnancyState schema addition

This bead is extended to also deliver `pregnancyState` on `NpcRuntimeState`, in addition to `captivityState`.

**Reason:** The content policy (docs/content-policy.md, round 2 panel) now explicitly IN-lists pregnancy from both consensual and non-consensual relationships as a modeled world state. The pregnancyState schema must exist before the Breeding Register events (destiny-3vyy) or the Mandatory Discovery Moment (destiny-jcrj) can function.

**pregnancyState schema addition to NpcRuntimeState:**
```ts
pregnancyState: z.object({
  conceptionDay: z.number(),
  daysElapsed: z.number().default(0),
  otherParentId: z.string().nullable(),  // npcId, 'player', or null (unknown)
  context: z.enum(['consensual', 'unknown']),  // 'unknown' = captivity aftermath; never surfaced to player as label
  registrationStatus: z.enum(['unregistered', 'registered', 'concealed', 'assessed']).default('unregistered'),
  permitObtained: z.boolean().default(false),
}).nullable().default(null)
```

**Policy constraints on `context: 'unknown'`:**
- The string 'unknown' is an internal code. It must never be displayed to the player in any UI element, tooltip, or label.
- The Mandatory Discovery Moment (destiny-jcrj) is the only authored path through which the player learns about this state.
- No UI panel may show `pregnancyState` at all before the discovery event has fired for that NPC.

**Duration:** pregnancyState ticks `daysElapsed` each day via endDay. At daysElapsed = 270 (standard gestation at 1:1 day ratio) OR daysElapsed = 90 (compressed — configurable), the birth event fires.

**`registrationStatus` flow:**
unregistered → registered (player registers, first child free or permit paid)
unregistered → concealed (player chooses concealment)
concealed → registered (player pays after deciding to come clean)
concealed → assessed (Compact discovers; player fails to pay)
any → assessed (player chooses Option E — let be assessed)

**Schema change checklist applies.**

title: House Merrow: author rival lineage faction as world entry
description:
## Why
House Valdris is returning to a city that has redistributed its power. No faction currently models the specific resentment of a house that quietly claimed Valdris authority during the Breach and now sees that claim threatened. Harlen Voss is a creditor enemy and Lady Sorn is an old-money structural enemy — but neither models the 'quietly nursing a legitimacy claim' archetype. Without a named rival lineage, the ambient hostility the player faces has no coherent origin.

## What
Author House Merrow of Ash Quay as a world-level rival lineage entry. This is a CONTENT and WORLD BUILDING bead — no new faction schema, no new faction mechanics. House Merrow is authored as a named entity that existing and new NPCs can reference.

### House Merrow — Authored Definition

**Full name:** House Merrow of the Ash Quay
**Type:** Civic-mercantile dynasty, not old nobility
**Origin:** Once dock inspectors and berth-masters in Harbor Ward. Became customs brokers and marriage witnesses after the Breach.
**Claim:** During the Breach, a city emergency order recognized the Merrow seal as the lawful replacement for Valdris authority over district tolls and succession disputes. They have held that de facto authority for years.
**Why they haven't moved openly:** The claim is shaky without the right records. A returning House Valdris means the original authority documents may still exist. They prefer delay, purchase, and sabotage over direct confrontation.
**Methods:** Debt acquisition, document purchase, quiet discrediting, arranged marriages into Compact positions, and — when necessary — contracts with people like Harlen Voss.
**What they fear:** That Orven Pell's ledger, Torvald Messe's copy, or Brannic Thule's testimony exist and can be authenticated.

### Authored world entries to create/update:
1. Add a `factionNote` or world lore entry for House Merrow (location: docs or data/definitions/)
2. Update Harlen Voss's bonds: add { type:'handler', targetId:'house-merrow', note:'Voss debt operations serve Merrow interests — not always by contract, but by alignment' }
3. Update Lady Sorn's bonds: add { type:'rival', targetId:'house-merrow', note:'Sorn and Merrow compete for the same legitimacy space. They cooperate only when Valdris is the target.' }
4. Optional: add one minor Merrow-affiliated NPC as a 'face' contact (not a named enemy — a functionary who delivers Merrow pressure indirectly)

## Why now
Several new NPCs (Orven Pell, Alis Vey, Veyran Malk) reference Merrow-adjacent pressure without naming it. destiny-4hiq (exteriorState faction reactions) needs a named faction to react to house tier advances. House Merrow is the named source of the 'faction visitor at tier 3' milestone.

## Player impact
- The ambient hostility the player faces is legible: House Merrow is the quiet hand behind several setbacks
- Faction visitor at exteriorState tier 3 is now authored: a Merrow functionary, not a generic envoy
- The player can investigate and eventually confront Merrow's claim directly

## System impact
- data/definitions/ or docs/ — House Merrow world entry (JSON lore entry or markdown)
- data/definitions/npcs.json — bonds updates for Harlen Voss and Lady Sorn (Merrow-aligned)
- No new faction schema required unless Merrow is added to factions.json (evaluate at implementation)

## Does not include
- Merrow as a full faction with faction mechanics (future bead if Merrow becomes a major faction)
- Merrow combat encounters
- Full Merrow NPC roster
- Resolution of the Merrow claim (late-game quest content)

## Evidence
WB panel section 6 (rival lineage): 'House Merrow of the Ash Quay — prefers delay, purchase, and sabotage over direct confrontation.' ND synthesis: 'the faction visitor at tier 3 now has a name.' Panel consensus: the ambient hostility needs a coherent origin.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** House Merrow referenced by name in at least one authored world entry or NPC background; Merrow's claim described in accessible lore
2. **System / technical:** Merrow world entry authored; Voss and Sorn bonds updated with Merrow references; no schema changes required
3. **Tests / verification:** npcs.json parses without error after bond updates; test count does not drop below 403

## Finding coverage
Lineage panel — WB section 6 (rival lineage problem), ND synthesis (ambient hostility origin)

## Fiction contract
House Merrow is not a villain faction. They are a bureaucratic dynasty that filled a legitimacy vacuum and now cannot afford to give it back. They do not hate the player — they simply cannot afford for the player to succeed.

title: Author lineage backstories for 9 existing NPCs in npcs.json
description:
## Why
Nine existing NPCs have backgrounds that imply generational depth but state nothing specific. Their `motivation` fields have generic publicGoal/privateNeed text that doesn't reference their connection to House Valdris, the Breach, or each other. The lineage panel produced specific, authoritative backstory decisions for each of them. These decisions must be written into npcs.json now — before destiny-nw4m (bond authoring) is implemented — because the bonds reference these backstories directly.

## What
Update the following 9 NPCs in data/definitions/npcs.json:

### 1. Old Maret (npc-old-maret)
- background: Update to specify she was lower household accounts keeper for House Valdris. Survived by knowing who was starving before they admitted it. Falsified grain ledgers during a pre-Breach winter riot to keep the house fed. Last living witness to the Valdris servants hall.
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To give her granddaughter the ledger before she dies — without admitting she knows she has it.'
- quirks: retain existing, optionally add: 'refers to the old Valdris hall rooms by name as if she still works there'

### 2. Sister Vael (npc-sister-vael)
- background: Update to specify her 'foreknowledge' comes from reading confessions, not visions. Has a dead sister erased from temple records after the Breach — smuggled out under plague blankets. Her information network is built entirely on confessions she was never meant to keep.
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To find the name of the patron who arranged her sister's erasure before she runs out of time to matter.'

### 3. Lira Ashcroft (npc-lira-ashcroft)
- background: Had Lirien outside a sanctioned marriage. The Ashcroft name was kept through a debt-forgiveness bargain with a surviving noble patron. Left the old district to protect Lirien from whatever the family owes. Knows why the Ashcrofts were allowed to keep their name when others were stripped.
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To die before Lirien finds out what name they were almost given instead.'

### 4. Lirien Ashcroft (npc-lirien-ashcroft)
- background: Lira's daughter, born outside sanctioned marriage. The wrist-marking is a survival tally — dates of deaths in her family, debt notices, or names she is not supposed to remember. Refuses family discussion in groups because she knows the real scandal is not the name but the blood.
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To find out whether she is heiress or evidence — and whether it matters either way.'

### 5. Maret Sunne (npc-maret-sunne)
- background: Bastard granddaughter of Old Maret, raised under a different surname to hide paternity. Became a clerk/archivist because she inherited Old Maret's memory for prices, names, and omissions. Holds a ledger that proves who profited from the fall of House Valdris. Does not know Old Maret knows she has it.
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To understand what the ledger means before using it — and who she would become if she did.'

### 6. Garet Doyle (npc-garet-doyle)
- background: Son of an innkeeper whose establishment served Valdris retainers before the Breach. The locked box behind the bar contains service tokens, ring fragments, and unpaid writs from the old house. Memorises orders because his father memorised noble table habits for a Valdris patron who later vanished.
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To return the box to someone who will know what to do with it without making him responsible for what happens next.'

### 7. Marion Vale (npc-marion-vale)
- background: 'Before the docks' means she survived the taking of the old harbor. Her family handled illicit cargo for a noble house and knows which house shipped people — not goods — out of Valdenmoor during the first panic. The dock work is legitimate cover for a network built on that original contract.
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To never be the person who made that cargo run again — and to never admit she still could.'

### 8. Torvald Messe (npc-torvald-messe)
- background: Son of a municipal notary who copied all noble grants before the Breach. Three copies of every document exists because his family lost everything to forged records. Knows which legal form erased House Valdris the first time — and has kept a copy of that form.
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To hand the copy to someone who can use it before his nerve fails. He has been waiting for the right person for years.'

### 9. Harlen Voss (npc-enemy-harlen-voss)
- background: Generational creditor lineage. His house loaned into ruin before the Breach and survived by purchasing Valdris arrears at collapse price. He is the descendant of the man who purchased the Valdris default and turned it into a standing claim. His wealth began with a coerced signature under duress — making his entire claim legally void if anyone can prove the circumstances.
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To complete the foreclosure before anyone finds the coercion papers.'

Lady Sorn update is intentionally NOT in this bead — her background is already hostile and specific. A separate enemy content bead should handle her if needed.

## Why now
destiny-nw4m (bond authoring) references these backstories directly — the bonds only make sense if the backing narrative is in the NPC definition. These updates must precede bond authoring.

## Player impact
- NPC backgrounds in the detail panel carry specific, credible history
- Old Maret's connection to House Valdris becomes discoverable through dialogue
- The Ashcroft mother/daughter relationship is readable from the detail panel
- Torvald Messe's copy of the Valdris erasure document becomes a quest hook

## System impact
- data/definitions/npcs.json — background, motivation.privateNeed, and optionally quirks updates for 9 NPCs
- No schema changes
- No runtime changes

## Does not include
- Bond array authoring (destiny-nw4m)
- New NPC creation (destiny-h2vi)
- Dialogue authoring for these NPCs
- Any changes to stats or traits

## Evidence
Lineage panel ND Part 1 (existing NPC lineage audit): specific authored backstory decisions for each of the 9 NPCs listed. All confirmed as authoritative decisions, not suggestions.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** NPC detail panel backgrounds read with specific Valdenmoor history; Old Maret's Valdris connection is present; Lira/Lirien relationship is readable
2. **System / technical:** All 9 NPC definitions updated in npcs.json; npcDefinitionSchema.parse() passes on full file; no field removals
3. **Tests / verification:** npcs.json parses without errors; test count does not drop below 403

## Finding coverage
Lineage panel — ND Part 1 (Old Maret, Sister Vael, Lira Ashcroft, Lirien Ashcroft, Maret Sunne, Garet Doyle, Marion Vale, Torvald Messe, Harlen Voss)

title: Author 5 new lineage NPCs: Orven Pell, Alis Vey, Brannic Thule, Veyran Malk, Cessa Rill
description:
## Why
Five specific archetypes are absent from the current NPC roster that a generationally credible Valdenmoor requires. These are not generic additions — each one holds a piece of the city's written, oral, legal, or grudge-memory that no existing NPC covers. Without them, the Mira lineage quest has no supporting cast, the legal erasure of House Valdris has no witness, and the generational enemy logic has no named face.

## What
Add 5 new NPCs to data/definitions/npcs.json with full authored definitions (all required schema fields including ageBand, sex, appearanceTags, quirks, motivation, bonds):

### 1. Orven Pell — The Archivist Who Kept the Ledger
- id: npc-orven-pell
- ageBand: elder | sex: man
- origin: Archive district (municipal quarter)
- npcType: world
- background: A bent record-keeper who can recite old property lines and burial permissions from memory. Survived the Breach by being too useful to remove.
- appearanceTags: ['ink-yellowed fingers', 'coat with too many interior pockets', 'never closes a door completely']
- quirks: ['answers questions about records with the filing date first', 'has memorised which ledgers burned and which merely vanished']
- motivation.publicGoal: 'Retirement. Obscurity. One more quiet season.'
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To give the ledger to someone who will use it before he dies.'
- bonds: [{ type:'witness', targetId:'house-valdris-fall', note:'Kept the one unburned ledger naming which house benefited from the Valdris erasure' }]
- Connection: Knows exactly how House Valdris was legally erased. Holds the unburned ledger.

### 2. Alis Vey — The Dispossessed Ally
- id: npc-alis-vey
- ageBand: middle | sex: woman
- origin: Former noble quarter
- npcType: world
- background: Her house once served as a cadet ally to House Valdris. Sold Valdris property under coercion during the Breach. Now lives like a clerk and claims no servants. Wants restitution before she can confess.
- appearanceTags: ['widow black without mourning status', 'good boots worn past their use', 'a crest ring worn face-inward']
- quirks: ['speaks of her house in the past tense without affect', 'keeps a record of every favor she has received since the Breach']
- motivation.publicGoal: 'To clear a name she no longer uses.'
- motivation.privateNeed: 'Restitution before confession. She must give something back before she can speak.'
- bonds: [{ type:'rival', targetId:'npc-enemy-lady-sorn', note:'Sorn pressured the sale. Alis has not forgiven it.' }, { type:'witness', targetId:'house-valdris-fall', note:'Sold Valdris property under coercion. Holds the original transfer documents.' }]

### 3. Brannic Thule — The Old Steward
- id: npc-brannic-thule
- ageBand: elder | sex: man
- origin: Old household quarter
- npcType: world
- background: Former steward's assistant at House Valdris. Now works as a repairman who recognises silver by touch and timber by smell. The only living person who served in the Valdris halls and is still in Valdenmoor.
- appearanceTags: ['heavy hands that move carefully around anything old', 'a steward badge worn under his shirt', 'stops to assess every room he enters']
- quirks: ['addresses the player by the correct honorific from the first meeting', 'names every room in a building by its old function before its current one']
- motivation.publicGoal: 'Useful work. A dry room. Nothing owed.'
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To deliver the artifacts before he dies. He swore an oath.'
- bonds: [{ type:'witness', targetId:'house-valdris-fall', note:'Served in Valdris halls before the fall. Knows where family seals and heir artifacts were hidden.' }]
