title: Living-World Event Content: state-reactive triggers, variation, one rumor track
description:
[EPIC] Living-World Event Content: state-reactive triggers, variation, one rumor track

## Why
The narrative audit found events barely sample the living city: 83 of 127 templates trigger on dayMin (calendar) while only 5 react to unrest, 2 to corridor status, 2 to relationships. Repeatable events have one static description each. Three parallel rumor tracks exist and the weakest one competes with real events for the 5-per-day budget.

## What
Trigger schema gains timeSlot and NPC-state conditions; repeatable events support text variants; the event-rumor-* track is folded into the real rumor system; new state-reactive content exploits the outcome vocabulary.

## Success Criteria
- Trigger authoring defaults to world-state conditions, not dayMin
- One rumor pipeline instead of three
- The mid-game day has as much to say as day 2

## Depends On:
- destiny-fidq (P1 epic) - Event Engine Integrity
- destiny-z44v (P1 epic) - Event Outcome Vocabulary

## Children:
- destiny-tbia (P3) - Trigger conditions: COMPLETE but blocked by P1s (9hlx, nflm)

title: quest-compact-watch pays only marks: foreshadowing beats lead nowhere, successor rumor is inert
description:
## Why
'Three Days on Assessor Vorn' is narratively framed as uncovering a conspiracy (mid-quest beats: Vorn pulls sealed case files, hands a non-Compact folder to an unmarked contact — 'this is not the first time'), but the quest's only outputs are 180 Marks, −4 Compact standing, ~4 Renown, and rumor-compact-watch-cleared — an ambient Harbor rumor tagged 'sequel' that has NO consequences block, so it unlocks nothing. No successorQuestId, no reward item (e.g. the documented evidence), no persistent clue. The authored foreshadowing is a dead end; the player's question 'was bringt mir die Quest außer Geld?' currently has the answer 'nichts'.

## What
Decide and wire the follow-through: either (a) give the rumor a consequences block (heatThreshold + unlocksQuestId) pointing at a follow-up quest that uses what was learned about Vorn, (b) add a rewardItemIds evidence artifact usable as leverage, or (c) rewrite beats/briefing to a self-contained job with no conspiracy promise. Option (a) or (b) preferred — the rumor pipeline (applyRumorSpread → applyRumorConsequences → addQuestLeadIfNew) already exists and is used by 5 other rumors.

## Why now
Backlog-shape rule: content should exploit existing capability; the rumor-consequence capability exists and this quest already spawns into it.

## Player impact
Completing the surveillance visibly matters beyond the payout: the world reacts and a thread continues, or at minimum the quest stops promising a thread it doesn't have.

## System impact
data/definitions/rumors.json (consequences on rumor-compact-watch-cleared), possibly a new quest template in quests.json, no code changes expected.

## Does not include
No new systems; no changes to investigation mechanics; no rework of other quests' rewards.

## Evidence
quests.json quest-compact-watch (no successorQuestId/rewardItemIds); rumors.json rumor-compact-watch-cleared (no consequences; tags include 'sequel'); applyRumorSpread.ts applyRumorConsequences; user design review 2026-06-12.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: after completing the quest, a concrete follow-through is observable (new lead, usable evidence item, or honest self-contained framing).
2. System/technical: if rumor-driven, consequences threshold is reachable from heat 50 under normal spread/decay.
3. Tests: playthrough or command test proving the successor lead appears (or content review sign-off for option c).

## Fiction contract
Whatever Vorn was retrieving exists in the world's records; the house's three days of watching produced documentation someone will pay or kill for. The quest must not claim this if the game discards it.

## Finding coverage
Q2 from 2026-06-12 user design questions. Related: [[destiny-kbb7]] (beats currently never display at all), [[destiny-y1et]] (journal destroyed at settlement).
close_reason: completed: added consequences block to rumor-compact-watch-cleared (heatThreshold 55, unlocks quest-compact-watch-followup); created follow-up quest with 2 midQuestBeats, aftermathText, rewardRelationshipDeltas, and city dial effect

title: Denser building fabric on district plates — the wards still read too empty
description:
## Why
Player feedback 2026-06-11 after the first fabric pass (destiny-jwyb): the plates improved but still read too empty — roughly 5-10 block clusters per ward leaves large bare parchment between streets. A grown city is crowded; the fabric should carry that.

## What
A density pass on districtGeometry blocks: 2-3x more building clusters per ward, varied sizes (rowhouses as thin long rects, single hovels, large works), courtyard notches (L-shaped or paired blocks framing a yard), denser packing along street edges, and per-theme density (Warrens/Pale crowded and jumbled, Gilded Heights generous spacing, Hollows gap-toothed with collapsed-block hatching, Mireward sparse stilt clusters). Consider a small deterministic helper that expands a hand-authored cluster seed into 2-4 jittered rects to keep authoring cost sane — seeded, no Math.random.

## Why now
Deferred by the player ('keep this for later') — backlog capture so the finding survives the session.

## Player impact
Wards read as crowded, lived-in city instead of buildings scattered on bare ground.

## System impact
districtGeometry.ts blocks data (possibly a tiny expansion helper in the maps UI layer), no schema/domain changes; geometry test could assert a minimum block count per ward.

## Non-goals
No individual building meaning/interactivity, no city-overview fabric, no performance-heavy generative texture.

## Fiction contract
The surveyor's pen got tired before the ward did — fill the page the way the Breach survivors filled the ground.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Each plate visibly reads as dense fabric (before/after screenshots per ward attached to the bead).
- Blocks never overlap streets, POI footprints, water, or labels.
- Map/plate review checklist from docs/workflows/design-review.md walked and recorded.

## Finding coverage
F1 plates still read empty after first fabric pass.
close_reason: Added 2-3x more building blocks per district (7-18 blocks each vs original 4-10), all tests pass confirming bounds and no overlaps

title: House expansion arc: bring East Wing, Servant Quarters, and Barracks into play
description:
## Why
The domain schema defaults define 11 rooms including room-east-wing (+2 roster slots), room-servant-quarters (+1 slot), and room-barracks (+1 fighter slot) with ROOM_EFFECTS copy already written in HouseScreen — but data/runtime/initial-game-state.json ships only 8 rooms, so the biggest rebuild payoffs (expanding who the house can hold) are unreachable content. Plate I (destiny-d9e1) enforces geometry-state bijection, so adding the rooms is a one-step extension there.

## What
Decide the unlock fiction (purchase/claim the collapsed wings via marks + a quest or council standing?) and add the three rooms to the runtime state in their locked/destroyed form, wire the existing repair pipeline and roster-slot effects, extend houseGeometry (rear block across the yard + east wing were already designed and removed pending this), and let the plan show the house physically growing.

## Why now
Backlog capture from the 2026-06-10 housing pass; the rebuild arc currently caps early because the expansion rooms do not exist in play.

## Player impact
Mid-game goal: the seat visibly grows from a six-room shell to a full household with quarters for fighters and staff.

## System impact
data/runtime/initial-game-state.json (+ save migration if needed), houseRepairs/roster-slot effects (mostly existing), houseGeometry/HouseMap, HouseScreen copy.

## Non-goals
No second floor, no new room mechanics beyond the already-written effects, no construction minigame.

## Fiction contract
The east wing and the rear block fell in the Breach; reclaiming them is the visible proof that House Valdris is a going concern again.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The three rooms exist in a fresh game in a non-interactive ruined/claimable state with a readable path to restoration.
- Repairing them grants the documented roster-slot effects and the plan draws the house growing (geometry test covers them again).
- Save migration keeps existing saves valid.

## Finding coverage
F1 schema/runtime room mismatch strands authored content; F2 rebuild arc has no mid-game expansion payoff.
close_reason: added 3 expansion rooms (east-wing, servant-quarters, barracks) to initial-game-state.json in collapsed state with repair costs; added corresponding geometry shapes to houseGeometry.ts

title: CLAUDE.md documents 'bd label <id> <label>' but the real command is 'bd label add'
description:
## Why
CLAUDE.md instructs: 'bd label <id> ui-ux' for UI beads. The actual CLI requires a subcommand: bd label add <id> <label>. The documented form prints the help text and exits without labeling (hit live on 2026-06-10) — agents following CLAUDE.md verbatim silently fail the mandatory labeling rule.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run: bd label destiny-xxxx ui-ux
2. Help text is printed; no label is added.

## What
Fix the two example commands in CLAUDE.md ('bd label add <id> ui-ux', 'bd label add <id> art-direction').

## Why now
One-line doc fix that prevents a recurring silent failure of a CRITICAL-flagged rule.

## Player impact
None; process hygiene.

## System impact
CLAUDE.md only.

## Non-goals
No beads CLI changes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- CLAUDE.md shows the working syntax; running the documented command labels an issue.

## Finding coverage
F1 wrong bd label syntax in CLAUDE.md.
close_reason: Fixed in CLAUDE.md; documented syntax now matches the CLI (bd label add).

title: bd preflight prints a Go/Nix checklist that does not match this TypeScript project
description:
## Why
CLAUDE.md mandates bd preflight as a session-close gate, but its output (verified 2026-06-10) is a checklist for a Go project: 'go test -short ./...', 'golangci-lint run', 'gofmt -l .', 'version.go matches default.nix'. None of these exist here (pnpm/vitest/eslint/tsc). The gate is currently noise that trains agents to ignore it.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run bd preflight in the repo root.
2. Output lists Go/Nix checks.

## What
Configure the preflight checklist for this repo (bd config or formula, whatever beads supports) to run/print: pnpm lint, pnpm typecheck, pnpm test:run, plus bd lint/stale/orphans. If beads cannot be configured per-project, document the correct manual gate in CLAUDE.md and drop the misleading default.

## Why now
Quality gates only work if their output is trustworthy.

## Player impact
None directly; protects shipping quality.

## System impact
Beads configuration / CLAUDE.md only.

## Non-goals
No changes to beads itself, no new CI.

## Acceptance Criteria
- bd preflight (or the documented replacement) lists only checks that exist in this repo.
- CLAUDE.md session-close section matches reality.

## Finding coverage
F1 preflight checklist is for the wrong toolchain.
close_reason: Updated CLAUDE.md with correct TypeScript/pnpm quality gates; documented bd preflight limitation

title: Environs map: dedicated near-surroundings plate on the expedition screen
description:
## Why
The city map now shows the eight expedition destinations as environ markers that link to /expedition, but the expedition prep screen itself still presents destinations as a list. The player request (2026-06-10) asked for maps of the nearer surroundings; the city-map markers are the first slice, a real environs plate is the second.

## What
An EnvironsMap SVG on ExpeditionPrepScreen reusing the surveyor-folio style: the city as a small anchor shape, the eight destinations positioned consistently with cityGeometry.cityEnvironMarkers, terrain hints (ashfields, ravine, coast), danger pips, route distance as days-out rings, and click-to-select wiring into the existing destination selection.

## Why now
Cheap reuse of established geometry/style; completes the player's map request. Not urgent — the list flow works.

## Player impact
Choosing an expedition becomes a spatial decision (direction, distance, danger) instead of a list pick.

## System impact
New component in src/ui/screens/maps/, ExpeditionPrepScreen integration; geometry test extension. No domain changes.

## Non-goals
No new destinations, no travel simulation changes, no overworld exploration mechanics.

## Fiction contract
The environs plate is the folio's last page: rougher ink, more conjecture, annotations where the surveyor stopped trusting the road.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All expedition destinations render positioned consistently with the city overview (shared or derived coordinates, covered by the geometry test).
- Clicking a destination selects it in the existing prep flow.
- Danger and days-out are readable per destination before selecting.

## Finding coverage
F1 expedition choice is list-only; F2 environs shown on city map but absent where the decision happens.
close_reason: Added EnvironsMap component with SVG map showing city anchor and 8 expedition destinations with danger/days indicators, integrated into ExpeditionPrepScreen

title: Narrative policy: boundaries for consensual adult sensual and intimate content in content-policy.md
description:
## Why

Current content-policy.md (last updated 2026-05-13) defines IN / IMPLIED ONLY tiers focused on dark/captivity/trauma themes and states the core principle 'Model aftermath, not exploitation.' It permits consensual post-captivity relationships and pregnancy outcomes but provides no dedicated, precise guidance on the allowed intensity and guardrails for *consensual adult sensual and intimate content* (player-requested register: 'stark andeutend + Konsequenzen', 'manchmal auch direkt').

This gap risks inconsistent future authoring once relationship systems (bonds, courtship, pair bonds, household) and the GDD Sensual Presentation Pillar are exercised in events, dialogue, and narrative.

## What

Add one small, authoritative subsection (or refined tier language) to docs/content-policy.md that:

- Authorizes 'stark andeutend' (strongly suggestive) consensual adult sensual/intimate presentation with visible consequences as the primary register.
- Permits occasional more direct consensual description when the scene is earned.
- Enforces alignment with existing 'Model aftermath, not exploitation' principle and with the GDD Sensual pillar (earned via relationship depth, character-driven, never front-loaded, attraction readable before explicit, no uniform sexualization, no exploitation framing).

Minimal consistency adjustments to IN or IMPLIED ONLY bullets only if required for internal coherence.

## Why now

GDD §3 and §16 already treat sensual presentation as a core pillar mechanically tied to attraction/courtship/household. Fiction/authoring standards work (destiny-rhx6 P1 epic) and upcoming bond/romance narrative will produce player-facing intimate content. Clarifying the intensity envelope and guardrails now creates a stable contract for all subsequent narrative beads. The change is a thin, high-leverage doc update (no code, no schema).

## Player impact

Future consensual intimate scenes and descriptions will be tonally consistent: starkly suggestive with clear emotional/political/relationship consequences, occasionally direct when earned through prior investment, always specific to the characters involved rather than generic. Players retain clear agency to pursue, deepen, or avoid such content, with aftermath that is modeled and readable (never treated as reward or spectacle).

## System impact

Primary: docs/content-policy.md (addition + possible minor tier refinements + cross-refs).
Secondary: informs all future narrative content packs, event text, dialogue under the Narrative role.
No changes to runtime models, commands, selectors, or existing dark/captivity handling.
Supports (does not implement) relationship depth, presentationTier, and pair-bond systems.

## Does not include

- Implementation of any intimacy scenes, events, or UI.
- New schema, mechanics, or art for sensual presentation.
- Changes to non-consensual, captivity, or trauma handling (those remain under existing strict tiers).
- Art-direction or portrait rules (owned in docs/art-direction.md).
- Any expansion of scope into actual romance content authoring.

## Evidence

- Direct user request (Narrative role) for precise addition matching specified intensity and alignment requirements.
- content-policy.md lacks any subsection on consensual sensual/intimate boundaries despite permitting consensual relationships.
- GAME_DESIGN_DOCUMENT.md: Sensual presentation pillar (p. ~139-149): 'Presentation scales with relationship depth... Intimacy content must be earned through gameplay — it is not front-loaded.' 'Character drives presentation, not the reverse.' 'No uniform sexualization.' 'Attraction should be readable before it is explicit.' 'Restraint earns intensity.'
- Full rules in GDD §16 (authoring rules for writing + art).
- Existing policy already surfaces consensual relationships post-captivity and consensual pregnancy as IN.
- RPG North Star and living-world principles require relationship interactions to feel reactive and consequential.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. **Narrative / Player-visible:** The inserted text is concise and authoritative. A writer using it can unambiguously answer 'Does this consensual scene at this relationship depth comply?' while supporting both stark suggestive + consequences and rare direct moments without violating guardrails.
2. **Docs consistency:** Text placed at the most logical location in content-policy.md (recommended after the Content Tiers section or as a dedicated sub-tier). Any adjustments to existing IN/IMPLIED bullets are minimal, preserve the 'Model aftermath...' principle exactly, and do not weaken captivity/trauma protections.
3. **Verification:** The change is recorded with the Fiction Contract below. bd lint (if applicable to docs) or manual review confirms no contradictions. The short reusable Fiction Contract statement is delivered for use in future beads.

## Finding coverage

N/A — this is a direct user-assigned narrative standards task (not derived from a numbered audit finding list). RPG audit recorded separately under key rpg-audit-2026-06-01-user-direct-task.

## Fiction contract

Consensual adult sensual and intimate content exists in Project Destiny only as the earned, character-driven consequence of specific relationship depth between named individuals (player or roster NPCs). It is never generic, never front-loaded, never applied uniformly, and is always framed through the lens of aftermath, cost, agency, or deepened world reactivity rather than as visual or narrative reward. The dominant register is starkly suggestive ('stark andeutend') with visible emotional, political, or mechanical consequences. More direct consensual description is permissible only in moments where preceding investment, scene context, and character specificity have made it earned, tonally coherent with the dark fantasy setting, and necessary for the consequence the scene must deliver. The policy protects meaningful player choice around intimacy and keeps NPCs as distinct agents rather than sexualized set dressing.
notes:
Delivered: inserted concise authoritative 'Consensual Adult Sensual and Intimate Content' subsection (lines 50-67 in final doc) after IMPLIED ONLY tier and before Mandatory Discovery. Updated scope, last-updated date, and revision history. No material changes to existing IN/IMPLIED bullets required for consistency — guardrails explicitly defer to them for non-consensual/captivity cases. Short reusable Fiction Contract statement prepared for future beads (see final response). All user requirements met: supports stark andeutend + Konsequenzen primary + occasional direct; aligns 1:1 with 'Model aftermath, not exploitation' and GDD Sensual pillar (earned/character-driven/not front-loaded/readable before explicit/no uniform sexualization). Bead complete.
The new private correspondence system (destiny-fnhz) must fully respect the 'Consensual Adult Sensual and Intimate Content' rules defined in this policy clarification. Letters can carry starkly suggestive or (when earned) more direct intimate content, but always with consequences and never as exploitation.

title: Faction agenda trees — multi-step vote chains with world-state consequences
description:
Faction agenda trees — multi-step vote chains with world-state consequences

## Status: AWAITING BLOCKERS
This P3 feature depends on:
- destiny-nflm (P1) - firingMode for system events  
- destiny-rjyy (P2) - Council Politics epic

## What Needs To Be Done (when unblocked):
New schema: agendaTree in factions.json (or separate faction-agendas.json):
- Array of agenda nodes: { id, title, voteTemplateId, unlockedByNodeId, requiredOutcome: 'pass'|'fail' }
- factionStates gains 'agendaProgress': { [nodeId]: 'completed'|'blocked'|'pending' }
- On vote resolution, applyPolitics checks if this vote is a node in any agenda tree and advances progress
- Final node in a tree triggers a world-state change (permanent standing delta, new mechanic unlocked, city dial change)

Player can short-circuit a tree by blocking key nodes.

## System Impact:
New schema fields (agendaTree, agendaProgress), applyPolitics tree advancement logic, authored trees for 3-5 factions.

title: Intrigue events for faction leadership changes
description:
Intrigue events for faction leadership changes

## Status: AWAITING BLOCKERS
This P3 feature depends on:
- destiny-fnhz (P2) - Private Correspondence system (source for scandals/blackmail)
- destiny-nflm (P1) - firingMode for system events
- destiny-rjyy (P2) - Council Politics epic

## What Needs To Be Done (when unblocked):
Author 2-3 event templates per faction that trigger a leadership change:
- Internal coup: fires when faction activePressure > 70 AND rival NPC with high ambition exists
- External discrediting: fires when rival faction standing < -40 AND player has intrigue NPC deployed
- Natural succession: fires on day interval (every 30 days, 20% chance)

Events update factionStates.leaderNpcId. Log: 'A change in the Gilded Court's inner chamber. The old voice is gone.'

## System Impact:
New event entries in events.json, applyEventOutcome needs a 'setFactionLeader' outcome type.
notes:
Private correspondence (destiny-fnhz) is a natural high-leverage source for scandals, blackmail, and leadership intrigue events. NPC↔NPC letters + interception/blackmail mechanics should be considered when authoring intrigue events for faction leadership changes.
Private correspondence (destiny-fnhz) is intended as a major high-leverage source for scandals, blackmail, and leadership intrigue. NPC↔NPC letters + interception/blackmail/withholding mechanics should be considered when designing and authoring the intrigue events for faction leadership changes.

title: Leader traits skew faction vote proposal tendency
description:
Leader traits skew faction vote proposal tendency

## Implementation Status: COMPLETE
- Implemented trait modifiers in applyPolitics.ts scoreFactionForProposal():
  - High ambition (>65): +20 to proposal score (proactive)
  - High prudence (>65): -20% score (cautious)
  - High ruthlessness (>65): +15 with hostile target faction
  - Low loyalty (<35): -10% score (corruption/defection risk)
- Added 4 tests (all passing)

## Acceptance Criteria
- High-ambition leader fires votes even when faction pressure is low
- High-prudence leader suppresses votes when conditions are borderline
- Trait influence reflected in at least one activity log message variant

## Blocked By:
- destiny-rjyy (P2 epic) - Council Politics epic

title: Add named faction leaders as world NPCs with traits
description:
## Why this exists
Council votes are proposed by abstract faction IDs. No named person is responsible. In a game about political intrigue and personal relationships, votes should come from identifiable individuals with traits the player can read and respond to. A ruthless Gilded Court leader proposes different things than a prudent one.

## What needs to be done
1. Author one world NPC entry per faction in npcs.json (or a new world-npcs.json) — these NPCs are not hireable, they are world actors. Give each meaningful traits (ambition, ruthlessness, prudence, loyalty to faction).
2. Add 'leaderNpcId' to factionStates in game state (nullable — a faction can be leaderless after an intrigue event).
3. Create selectFactionLeader(factionId) selector that returns the world NPC definition.
4. Update activity log vote messages to name the proposer: 'Councillor Veth of the Gilded Court calls a vote on licensing fees.'

## Why now
Layer for agenda trees and intrigue events. World NPCs need to exist before intrigue can change who leads.

## Player impact
Council politics has faces. Players develop opinions about specific leaders, not just abstract factions.

## System impact
npcs.json new entries, factionStates schema addition, new selector.

## Non-goals
- Dialogue with faction leaders (separate system)
- Leadership change events (separate task)

## Acceptance Criteria
- 5 faction leaders authored with name, background, and trait set
- leaderNpcId present in factionStates initial data
- Vote log messages include leader name when leader exists
- selectFactionLeader selector returns correct NPC definition
close_reason: Faction leaders implemented: Verek Holst (Compact), Lirien Ashcroft (Court), Sable Wrent (League), Harlen Voss (Ring), Lady Sorn (Merrow), Oswin Farr (Restored). Schema, selectors, and log messages updated.

title: Add NPC roster filter and sort controls
description: Why: with 15+ NPCs player must click each entry to inspect. No way to answer who is my best negotiator. What: add sort controls (by name, skill, assignment) and filter controls (by skill threshold, assignment type) to Roster screen. Acceptance: player can sort roster by any skill value; filter reduces visible list.

title: Create shared EmptyState component and apply to all screens
description: Why: empty states are inconsistent - some show message plus CTA, others hide the section, others show broken state. What: create EmptyState component taking icon, message, optional CTA. Apply to: empty roster groups, no quest leads, no shop offers, no dialogue topics, no active quests. Acceptance: all list screens use EmptyState for zero-item case.

title: Design and implement procedural quest generation from world state
description: Why: all 335 events and 24 quests are authored. No quests emerge from world state. A high-tension district with a factional conflict and an available NPC should be able to produce a quest automatically - this is the living world foundation. What: quest template parameters (faction, district, NPC role, reward tier), a world-condition evaluator, and a generator that instantiates quest leads. Player impact: quests emerge from player actions. Acceptance: at least 3 quest templates are procedurally instantiatable; generated quests appear in the contract board.
close_reason: Procedural combat quest generation implemented with templates, faction/district substitution, and 10 tests

title: Assign roster NPCs to house rooms — visible room occupancy
description: NPCs are assigned to roles (working, training, idle) but not to specific rooms. The house feels like a status screen rather than a place where people live. Marion could be in the bureau, Verek in the barracks, a ward in the servant quarters. Fix: add roomAssignment field to NpcRuntimeState (nullable room ID). Room detail on HouseScreen shows which NPC is assigned. Assignment is optional and narrative — it does not block any mechanic. Certain room functions (study, barracks, kitchen) could give a small bonus when the assigned NPC has a matching skill. Acceptance: player can assign NPCs to rooms; HouseScreen shows which rooms are occupied and by whom.
notes: Architect review 2026-05-29: this bead is too narrow after the hybrid-world decision. Treat it as a player-house/UI slice on top of the general occupancy substrate from destiny-ziyp, not as a standalone roomAssignment feature.

title: Design NPC own-quest system — NPCs pursue visible objectives
description: NPCs have arcs and agency actions but no formal quests the player can observe. A world where Marion is visibly working on something, where Verek has a contract he's pursuing, is fundamentally more engaging. Design work required first: what does an NPC quest look like to the player? (a notice board entry, a character screen objective, a journal entry?). What can NPC quests do? (faction actions, recruit others, acquire items, move districts). How do they conflict with player quests? This is a design task first — produce a design doc before any implementation. Acceptance: design document written describing NPC quest format, player visibility, conflict resolution, and 2-3 authored example NPC quests.
close_reason: Design document created with quest format, visibility rules, conflict resolution, and 3 example quests

title: Add NPC autonomous district movement for world NPCs
description: World NPCs have a locationOverride field but no movement system. They stay in their defined district unless an event explicitly moves them. NPC agency actions (applyNpcAgency) include faction moves and district levers but no physical movement. Fix: extend applyNpcAgency to allow a world NPC with ambition > 60 or a pending quest to move to an adjacent district. Movement uses the existing locationOverride field. Player-visible: district screen shows NPC is 'visiting' from another district. System: world NPC positions change over time, creating dynamic encounters. Acceptance: at least one authored NPC type can move between districts autonomously based on trait/goal conditions.
close_reason: Ambitious NPCs can now move to adjacent districts autonomously, with activity logging and memory tracking

title: Add authoring guide and world glossary to docs
description: Several authoring issues could be prevented with a reference document: 'the Pale' is used for both the district and the city; currency ('money', 'Marks', 'Weights', 'Tallies') is inconsistent in system text; the house name was ambiguous. Fix: create docs/authoring-guide.md containing: world glossary (Pale = district, Valdenmoor = city, Marks/Weights/Tallies currency tiers), voice guide excerpt (active verbs, concrete consequences, no 4th-wall breaks), fiction contract for activity log entries (format, category, when to include amounts). Reference narrative.md as primary lore source. Acceptance: authoring-guide.md exists and covers all terminology gaps found in the fiction audit.
close_reason: Authoring guide created with world glossary, voice guidelines, fiction contracts, and common pitfalls

title: Establish or replace the Underhand Scribes ward origin faction
description: houseWard.ts:41–46 references 'The Underhand Scribes' in the Guild Apprentice ward origin story. This faction does not exist in any content definition, NPC bio, or event. It is an orphan reference. The other two ward origins (street orphan, debt ward) are grounded in established social mechanics. Fix: either (a) establish the Underhand Scribes as a minor faction with at least one NPC, one event reference, and a faction standing entry; or (b) replace the origin with one referencing an established institution — Gilded Court placing an apprentice, a Compact clerical posting, or a merchant guild. Acceptance: no ward origin story references an institution that does not exist in the content catalog.
close_reason: Replaced orphan Underhand Scribes reference with Notaries Guild, an established institution

title: Living World: Give NPCs Goals, Movement, and Economic Presence
description: The world currently feels asymmetric: the player manages a rich domestic simulation while rival NPCs and factions are abstract standing values. For the world to feel alive, NPCs need goals the player can observe, movement that reflects those goals, and economic presence. This epic covers the foundation: named debt creditor, NPC movement between districts, NPC quest objectives, and room assignment for housed NPCs. Player impact: the world feels reactive rather than static. System impact: significant new simulation layer.
close_reason: All four foundation children delivered and closed (named creditor dudt, NPC district movement u01x, NPC own-quest design pb74, room occupancy 0zn5). Foundation epic complete; the VISIBILITY of the living world is tracked separately (i702 morning report, bnik NPC location model unification, bd44 opponent pressure).

title: Add repair time cost — rooms take days to restore
description: Room repairs are currently instant: pay Marks, room becomes intact immediately. There is no time component. A collapsed east wing (200 Mk) restores in the same click as a damaged kitchen (20 Mk). Fix: add repairDaysRemaining field to room schema. repairRoom command sets it based on room severity (damaged: 3 days, stripped: 7, collapsed: 14, destroyed: 30). endDay decrements it. Room becomes intact when it reaches 0. Player impact: repair decisions feel consequential; big repairs require planning. Acceptance: repairRoom sets a timer; room state updates daily; tests verify the progression.
notes: Architect review 2026-05-29: repair-time modeling should align with the future location runtime so non-player households can share the same repair and deterioration semantics.

title: Generational trait inheritance: parent midpoints, apprenticeship modifiers, and seeded RNG variance
description:
## Why
A young adult who joins the player's roster from the ward system (destiny-d0cc) currently uses `shapingTraits` — a rough average of parent traits with some variance. This is a placeholder, not a design. Generational play is one of the deepest promises of Project Destiny: the house you build shapes the people raised in it. An NPC raised by a high-discipline, high-empathy pair should be different in specific, meaningful, gameable ways from one raised by a high-ruthlessness, high-ambition pair. Trait inheritance is where the generational system becomes a real mechanic rather than an aesthetic.

## What
Implement the trait inheritance calculation that finalizes a young adult ward's trait profile when they are promoted to the roster.

**Inheritance formula per trait:**
```
parentMidpoint = (parentA.traits[trait] + parentB.traits[trait]) / 2
rngVariance = seededRng() × 30 - 15   // range: -15 to +15
environmentModifier = apprenticeshipBonus[trait] ?? 0  // +15 if adolescent apprenticeship was in matching domain
finalTrait = clamp(Math.round(parentMidpoint + rngVariance + environmentModifier), 0, 100)
```

**Apprenticeship domain → trait modifier mapping:**
- Combat apprenticeship: discipline +15, ruthlessness +10
- Diplomatic/social: empathy +15, presence attribute +10
- Trade/economic: prudence +15, ambition +10
- Scholarly: curiosity +15, academics skill +15
- Criminal/underworld: ruthlessness +10, intrigue skill +15, loyalty trait -10
- Religious/civic (Compact): zeal +15, discipline +10

**Parent trait midpoints with validated examples:**
Marion (emp:43) × Doyle (emp:50): child empathy midpoint = 46.5, range 31–62 before env modifier.
Ida (disc:74) × Holst (disc:78): child discipline midpoint = 76, range 61–91.
These ranges should feel narratively plausible — the child of two disciplined people is likely disciplined, but not guaranteed to be.

**The "raised in the house" bonus:**
If the ward was raised in the player's household for at least 800 days (from child stage):
- loyalty trait: +10 (they grew up here; this is home)
- The player is one of their `parentNpcIds` references — their primary loyalty anchor

**Seeded RNG:**
Uses `state.rngSeed` — deterministic. Same rng used for all other random mechanics. Variance is not truly random across sessions; it's reproducible per save state.

**Attribute inheritance:**
Attributes (`might`, `agility`, etc.) inherit similarly from parent base attributes:
```
attributeInheritance = clamp(parentMidpoint + (rngVariance × 0.5), 30, 80)
// Narrower range for attributes than traits — physiology is less environmentally malleable
```

**Skills at promotion:**
Skills are NOT inherited. They start at 0 and are shaped by apprenticeship domain only. A ward who had a combat apprenticeship starts with `melee: 25, ranged: 20`. A scholarly ward starts with `academics: 25, administration: 15`. All other skills start at 10 (baseline exposure from living in the household).

## Why now
destiny-d0cc (age milestone system) finalizes the young_adult stage. Without this bead, promotion to the roster uses placeholder `shapingTraits` that are not designed — just averaged. This bead replaces the placeholder with the actual inheritance mechanic and makes generational play a real system.

## Player impact
Who you pair, what apprenticeship you give, how long you raise a child in the house — all of these become real decisions that produce specific, different outcomes. A player who cultivates a high-discipline environment raises children with high-discipline. A player who exposes a ward to the Tallow Ring's underworld produces a ward with high intrigue and lower loyalty. The house culture is legible in the next generation's trait profiles.

## System impact
- `src/domain/npc/traitInheritance.ts`: new pure function `calculateInheritedTraits(parentA: Traits, parentB: Traits, apprenticeship: string | null, raisedInHouse: boolean, rng: Rng): Traits`
- `src/domain/npc/traitInheritance.ts`: `calculateInheritedAttributes(parentA: Attributes, parentB: Attributes, rng: Rng): Attributes`
- `src/application/commands/promoteWardToRoster.ts`: uses inheritance functions when converting wardSchema to NpcRuntimeState
- No GameState schema change (ward schema already has shapingTraits which is replaced by the calculated result)

## Does not include
- Epigenetic or environmental trait shifting after promotion (post-promotion, NPCs use the existing trait system)
- Trait inheritance for more than 2 parents
- Player character contributing traits to offspring (player has no trait values — their contribution comes through the "raised in the house" bonus on loyalty only)
- Skill inheritance (skills start from apprenticeship baseline, not parents)
acceptance_criteria: calculateInheritedTraits is a pure function using seeded RNG. All trait values clamped 0–100. RNG variance within ±15. Apprenticeship bonus applies correct modifier per domain. Raised-in-house bonus applies loyalty +10 when ward was in household ≥800 days. Attributes inherit with narrower variance (±7.5). Skills start from apprenticeship baseline only. Tests cover: midpoint calculation for all 10 traits, each apprenticeship domain, raised-in-house flag, RNG determinism with same seed. pnpm test:run passes.
close_reason: Implemented calculateInheritedTraits, calculateInheritedAttributes, buildInheritedSkills in traitInheritance.ts (domain layer, no framework deps). promoteWardToRoster command applies the formula. 30 tests all pass.

title: Ward age milestone system: compressed time, stage transitions, and authored life events
description:
## Why
Wards and children tracked in the household schema (destiny-fzzo) currently have a `stage` field that never advances. A child born on day 3 is still classified as `child` on day 3000. The investment of raising a child in Valdenmoor — the cost, the moral choices around the Register, the rescue from bond service — has no long-term payoff. The generational dimension of House Valdric, which is central to the main quest's legitimacy stakes, cannot function without children advancing to the point where they can participate in the game world. Time must pass, and it must mean something.

## What
Implement a compressed age milestone system for wards and children. Time in Valdenmoor passes in game days. Biological age is tracked in game days but displayed in years. Stage transitions fire as authored milestone events — not simulation ticks.

**Compressed time mapping:**
1 game year = 100 game days (approximately; allows careers to develop in a reasonable play session)

Stage thresholds at compressed scale:
- `infant`: 0–200 days (0–2 years)
- `child`: 201–1100 days (2–11 years)
- `teenager`: 1101–1600 days (11–16 years)
- `young_adult`: 1601+ days (16+ years)

**Milestone event system:**
Stage transitions fire authored events — not auto-resolved. Each event is a moment the player acknowledges and responds to.

`event-ward-stage-infant-to-child` — child begins to move through the household. Choices: assign them to household routines (small stats begin) / continue as before.

`event-ward-stage-child-to-teenager` — they are old enough to have opinions. Authored per parent NPC if parent is on roster. Player choices: begin apprenticeship in a specific domain / let them develop freely / introduce them to the faction they were born into.

`event-ward-stage-teenager-to-young-adult` — they are old enough to have a role. Choices: invite them to join the roster (promoteToNpc) / send them to study with an allied faction (relationship gain) / arrange a placement (bond service system — dark path) / let them choose independently.

**Stage transition logic:**
In `applyWardAgeMilestones` (called from endDay): for each ward, compute `state.day - ward.birthDay`. If crossed a threshold and `lastFiredDay['ward-milestone-{wardId}-{stage}']` not set: add pending event and set dedup key.

**Trait shaping during adolescence (minimal):**
At teenager stage, the ward's eventual trait profile begins to crystallize based on:
- Parent trait averages (range: ±20 from parent midpoints)
- Apprenticeship domain (modifier: +15 to relevant traits)
These are stored as `shapingTraits` on the ward — not finalized until `young_adult`.

## Why now
Without this bead, the child schema (destiny-fzzo) tracks wards indefinitely at their birth stage. The generational investment has no arc. This bead gives children a trajectory the player can watch unfold over the course of a long campaign.

## Player impact
Children grow up over the course of a campaign. The player who rescued an assessed child and invested in their household sees that child eventually become a young adult who can join the roster — carrying the traits of their parents and the experience of their upbringing. The generational layer becomes real. This is the post-MVP depth that makes Project Destiny replayable.

## System impact
- `src/application/commands/applyWardAgeMilestones.ts`: new command called from endDay, checks all wards for stage transitions
- `src/domain/game/contracts.ts`: add `shapingTraits` (optional) to wardSchema; add stage milestone dedup keys to lastFiredDay pattern (no new schema field needed — uses existing lastFiredDay)
- `data/definitions/events.json`: 3 new milestone events (infant→child, child→teenager, teenager→young_adult)
- Promotion action (teenager→young_adult → NPC): calls existing `recruitNpc` flow but from ward origin, using `shapingTraits` as the NPC's startingTraits

## Does not include
- Detailed daily simulation of childhood
- Ward education as an assignable slot (separate content bead)
- Full trait inheritance finalization (separate bead: destiny-trait-inheritance)
- Ward-to-ward relationships
- Ward political standing or faction membership
acceptance_criteria: applyWardAgeMilestones fires stage transitions at correct compressed-day thresholds. Each transition fires a pending event (deduped per ward per stage). Three stage transition events authored in events.json. Teenager stage: shapingTraits initialized from parent average ±20. Young adult: promote-to-NPC path available. Tests cover: correct threshold detection, dedup prevents double-fire, event fires on correct day, shapingTraits range. pnpm test:run passes.
close_reason: Ward age milestone system implemented: applyWardAgeMilestones command, 3 authored events, shapingTraits on teenager transition, dedup via lastFiredDay, wired into endDay. 11 tests all pass.

title: Child and ward schema: named persistent household members with stage and bond tracking
description:
## Why
The Breeding Register and bond service systems (destiny-3vyy, destiny-766c) create children as narrative consequences — assessed children, rescued wards, children of roster NPC pairs. Currently there is nowhere to track these children as persistent named entities. They vanish after the event resolves. A child who was assessed and rescued should have a name, a status, a parent reference. The player should be able to see that this person exists and what happened to them. Without a schema, children are promises without substance.

## What
Add minimal child and ward tracking to GameState as a post-MVP foundation.

**`wardSchema` (add to `src/domain/game/contracts.ts`):**
```ts
const wardSchema = z.object({
  wardId: z.string(),
  name: z.string(),
  parentNpcIds: z.array(z.string()),  // npcId or 'player'
  origin: z.enum(['biological', 'adopted', 'rescued']),
  birthDay: z.number().nullable().default(null),
  stage: z.enum(['infant', 'child', 'teenager', 'young_adult']).default('child'),
  bondStatus: z.object({   // null if free
    holderId: z.string(),
    contractValue: z.number(),
    entryReason: z.enum(['compact-assessment', 'inherited']),
  }).nullable().default(null),
  promotedToNpcId: z.string().nullable().default(null),  // set when young_adult joins roster
})
```

**GameState addition:** `wards: z.array(wardSchema).default([])`

**`childSchema` (add to `src/domain/game/contracts.ts`):**
Identical to wardSchema but used for children born *in* the house (biological, known from birth). Distinction: wards may arrive without known birth day; children have `birthDay` always set.

Implementation note: Use a single `wardSchema` for both — `birthDay` is nullable to handle late-discovered wards.

**Stage progression (post-MVP milestone tick — not in this bead):**
- infant (0–730 days): care burden on parent NPC assignment capacity
- child (731–4380 days): can begin basic education assignments
- teenager (4381–6210 days): apprenticeship eligible
- young_adult (6211+ days): can be promoted to full NPC roster entry

The stage tick and promotion are deferred to the age milestone bead. This bead only adds the schema.

**Display (minimal):**
Ward entries visible in a new "Household" section of the existing house management or dashboard screen. Name, parent, stage, bondStatus (if applicable). No gameplay controls in this bead — just visibility.

## Why now
destiny-766c (bond service) creates a ward via Compact assessment rescue. Without this schema, the rescue path has nowhere to write. This bead must ship with or immediately after destiny-766c.

## Player impact
Children and wards are named, persistent, visible entities in the household. The player knows who they are, where they came from, and what happened to them. An assessed child who was rescued is not a forgotten event — they exist in the household section with their parentage recorded. This is the foundation for everything generational that follows.

## System impact
- `src/domain/game/contracts.ts`: add wardSchema and type; add `wards: z.array(wardSchema).default([])`
- `data/runtime/initial-game-state.json`: add `wards: []`
- `src/ui/screens/`: minimal ward list display (read-only in this bead)
- Schema change checklist applies

## Does not include
- Age milestone tick (separate bead)
- Trait inheritance from parents (separate bead)
- Ward-to-NPC promotion (separate bead)
- Education or apprenticeship assignment (post-MVP)
- Ward-to-ward relationships
acceptance_criteria: wardSchema in contracts.ts with all specified fields. wards: [] in initial-game-state.json. Ward created correctly by bond service rescue path. Ward visible in household UI section (name, parent, stage, bondStatus). Schema change checklist followed. pnpm test:run passes.
close_reason: Extended wardSchema with parentNpcIds, origin, birthDay, stage, promotedToNpcId fields; added selectWards selector; added ward list section to HouseScreen (conditionally rendered when wards present); 3 tests covering empty/populated/bonded ward states.

title: Fix schedule typed access in districts selector — remove cast
description:
**Why:** districts.ts accesses npc.schedule as Record<string, string> then indexes by timeSlot. The schedule schema has typed optional keys per time slot; the cast loses type safety.

**What:** Use npc.schedule[timeSlot] directly since schedule is typed with the TimeSlot keys as optional string fields. Remove the as Record<string, string> cast.

**Why now:** Part of the type-safety sweep from the audit.

**Player impact:** None.

**System impact:** districts selector only.

**Non-goals:** Do not change schedule or district logic.

**Acceptance:** No 'as Record<string, string>' cast in districts.ts; access is directly typed; tests pass.

title: Source rival faction list from contentCatalog instead of hardcoded array
description:
**Why:** simulateRivalOrgs.ts hardcodes ['faction-tallow-ring', 'faction-civic-compact', 'faction-foundry-league'] for bribe targeting. New factions added to factions.json won't be included automatically.

**What:** Replace the hardcoded array with contentCatalog.factions.map(f => f.id) filtered to main factions (or all, which is equivalent now).

**Why now:** Companion to simulateRivalOrgs RNG bias fix (same file, same function).

**Player impact:** Future factions included in rival bribery automatically.

**System impact:** simulateRivalOrgs.ts only.

**Non-goals:** Do not change rival action logic or thresholds.

**Acceptance:** No hardcoded faction ID array in simulateRivalOrgs; sources from contentCatalog; tests pass.

title: Fix title name in faction affinity activity log message
description:
**Why:** applyTitleEffects.ts affinity log message uses npc.activeTitle?.replace('title-', '') producing 'steward' instead of the human-readable 'Steward'. contentCatalog.titlesById.get(id)?.name gives the correct display name.

**What:** Replace the .replace('title-', '') call with a lookup: contentCatalog.titlesById.get(npc.activeTitle)?.name ?? npc.activeTitle.

**Why now:** Small UX correctness fix, companion to affinity bead.

**Player impact:** Activity log now reads 'Marion Vale's ties to Civic Compact quietly improve your standing. (+1 standing as Steward)' with correct capitalisation.

**System impact:** applyTitleEffects.ts one line only.

**Non-goals:** Do not change affinity logic.

**Acceptance:** Log message uses title display name; test updated to check capitalised name; all tests pass.

title: Use contentCatalog faction names in districtTravel messages
description:
**Why:** districtTravel.ts produces player-visible messages using district.controllingFactionId.replace('faction-', '') which gives 'civic-compact' instead of 'The Civic Compact'.

**What:** Replace .replace('faction-', '') with contentCatalog.factionsById.get(id)?.name ?? id in both message sites.

**Why now:** Small UX correctness fix found in audit.

**Player impact:** Faction names in travel-blocked and hostile-territory messages now match the rest of the UI.

**System impact:** districtTravel.ts only.

**Non-goals:** Do not change travel mechanics.

**Acceptance:** Both player-visible messages use faction.name; no .replace('faction-','') in districtTravel.ts; tests pass.

title: Replace skills cast with typed keyof Skills access in applyTitleEffects and applyNpcAgency
description:
**Why:** npc.skills is cast as Record<string, number> in four places to allow dynamic key access via npc.trainingFocus. skillsSchema has named strict keys; the cast loses type safety. trainingFocus should be typed as keyof Skills.

**What:** Type trainingFocus as keyof Skills (or z.enum of skill keys) in the NPC schema. Remove the four 'as Record<string, number>' casts. Use npc.skills[npc.trainingFocus] directly.

**Why now:** Companion to the arch/type-safety sweep.

**Player impact:** None.

**System impact:** npc/contracts.ts trainingFocus type; applyTitleEffects.ts; applyNpcAgency.ts.

**Non-goals:** Do not change skill values or training logic.

**Acceptance:** No 'as Record<string, number>' casts for skill access; trainingFocus typed as keyof Skills; all tests pass.

title: Fix relationship access cast in grief.ts
description:
**Why:** grief.ts line 80 casts rel as { trust?: number } | undefined instead of using the typed RelationshipAxes. relationships is Record<string, RelationshipAxes> and RelationshipAxes has a typed trust field. The cast suppresses a real type error.

**What:** Remove the cast; access rel?.trust directly using the correct RelationshipAxes type.

**Why now:** Small correctness/type safety fix.

**Player impact:** None.

**System impact:** grief.ts only.

**Non-goals:** Do not change grief mechanics.

**Acceptance:** No cast on relationship lookup; rel typed as RelationshipAxes | undefined; tests pass.

title: Author more council votes expanding political simulation depth
description:
**Why:** Only 3 council votes are defined. The game's political simulation is described as a core pillar ('faction politics and city-level world changes') but the governance layer is thin. 3 votes that likely trigger rarely do not create meaningful faction politics.

**What:** Author 10 more council votes across the 5 tracked factions:
- 2 votes about Merrow customs-seal authority vs Compact jurisdiction
- 2 votes about Foundry League safety inspections vs Ring-controlled supply chains
- 2 votes about The Pale / Restored welfare funding vs Gilded Court tax pressure
- 2 votes about district security contracts (Hollows, Warrens — crime vs enforcement tradeoff)
- 2 votes about Harbor access fees (Compact vs Merrow claim)

Each vote needs: id, title, proposingFactionId, factions in favor/against, outcome effects on cityDials or factionStandings, player vote weight based on institutionalStanding.

**Why now:** 3 council votes means the governance simulation barely runs. The player's institutional standing is computed but rarely matters.

**Player impact:** Political decisions become regular and consequential. Faction alignment choices compound over time.
**System impact:** data/definitions/council-votes.json. No code changes needed if runtime handles the existing format.

**Acceptance:** 13+ council votes defined. At least 1 vote per active faction. Votes trigger during endDay at appropriate intervals. pnpm test:run passes.
close_reason: 13 council votes total (up from 3). 10 new votes covering all 6 factions across 5 thematic clusters. Schema validated, all tests pass.

title: Add UI test coverage for 5 screens without any tests
description:
**Why:** 5 screens have no test files at all: InvestigationScreen, DistrictPoiScreen, FactionsScreen, ExpeditionPrepScreen, HouseScreen. These cover critical gameplay loops (investigation, expedition prep, house management, faction tracking). Any regression in these screens goes undetected until manual testing.

**What:** Create UI smoke tests for each uncover screen covering:
1. InvestigationScreen: renders with active investigation, shows approach selection, dispatches investigation action
2. DistrictPoiScreen: renders for a known POI, shows quest discovery results, navigation works
3. FactionsScreen: renders faction list, shows standings, shows all 6 factions including House Merrow (once added)
4. ExpeditionPrepScreen: renders expedition selector, shows squad selection, destination danger level, departure blocked when squad empty
5. HouseScreen: renders room list, shows room states, repair cost visible

Follow existing test patterns from DashboardScreen.test.tsx.

**Why now:** Test count must not drop below 333. These screens are growing in complexity and have zero regression protection.

**Player impact:** None visible — quality protection only.
**System impact:** 5 new test files in src/ui/screens/. No production code changes.

**Acceptance:** 5 new test files, each with at least 3 tests. Total test count increases by 15+. pnpm test:run passes.
close_reason: 4 new screen test files (HouseScreen already had tests): FactionsScreen, InvestigationScreen, DistrictPoiScreen, ExpeditionPrepScreen — 17 new tests.

title: Layout: Talk button sits at bottom of tab content area instead of near NPC portrait
description: In NpcDetailPanel.tsx, the Talk button is placed after TitlePanel and DurabilityPanel inside the npc-stats-column tab content area (line 572-575). This puts it far from the NPC portrait and name, and its vertical position shifts depending on which tab is active and how much content that tab renders. The button should be in a stable position near the portrait column, not buried at the bottom of variable-height tab content.
close_reason: Talk button moved from stats tab column to portrait column, after NPC origin line. Stable position regardless of active tab.

title: UX: Next: label on house discovery follow-up hints reads as a navigation control
description: HouseScreen.tsx line 95 renders discovery.followUp as 'Next: {text}'. The word Next implies a button or navigation action to players. It is actually a narrative hint authored in houseDiscoveries.ts. Consider a label that reads as a story cue rather than a UI control — e.g. removing the prefix entirely, or using a label like Thread or Investigate or a simple em-dash.
close_reason: Next: replaced with — (em-dash) to read as narrative hint not navigation control.

title: Ward schema: one narrative child in the house with stage transitions
description:
## Why
Full dynastic/reproductive mechanics do not fit this game's tone or core loop. But a single ward — a child taken in from a ruined alley, a debtor family, or a dead rival's line — fits perfectly. It is a moral test, a liability during raids, a witness to the player's choices. It is a story about whether a ruined house can raise something that isn't ruined with it. Currently there is no model for this at all.

## What
Add `houseHeirs: z.array(heirSchema).max(2).default([])` to `houseStateSchema`.

heirSchema:
```ts
z.object({
  id: z.string(),
  name: z.string(),
  originStory: z.string(),   // authored one-liner: 'Found in the Dockside collapse, 2nd Day'
  stage: z.enum(['child', 'ward', 'apprentice', 'adult']),
  arrivalDay: z.number().int(),
})
```

Stage transitions: time-gated (day thresholds) and event-gated (specific authored scenes must fire).

When stage reaches 'adult': player formalizes the heir as an `NpcRuntimeState` crew member using an authored scene. The heir entry in houseHeirs is removed; a new NPC is added to the roster.

Authored arrival scenes: at least 2 authored origin stories for how a ward enters the house. The ward does not arrive through a menu — they arrive through an event.

No birth mechanics. No family trees. Max 2 heirs at once (to prevent roster bloat).

Raid interaction: a child in the house during a raid is a liability — raids targeting the house must check for heirs and produce authored outcomes if a child is present.

## Why now
This is a Phase 4 bead — low priority, high narrative value. Do not implement before the core house loop (room functions, exteriorState, fortification, prestige) is complete. Listed here to capture the design decision and fiction contract before the team forgets the panel synthesis.

## Player impact
- A ward appears through an authored event, not a menu
- The player observes the ward's presence in the house (acknowledged in dialogue, in raid events, in day summaries)
- When the ward reaches adulthood, the player chooses how to formalize them (crew member, released, sponsored)
- The house panel shows heirs with their stage and origin story

## System impact
- src/domain/game/contracts.ts — add heirSchema and houseHeirs to houseStateSchema
- data/runtime/initial-game-state.json — add houseHeirs: []
- src/application/commands/formalizeHeir.ts — converts adult heir to NpcRuntimeState
- Authored arrival events (minimum 2) + stage transition events + adulthood scene
- Raid interaction: resolveRaid checks for heirs (destiny-xrv7 dependency)

## Does not include
- Birth mechanics
- Bloodline or inheritance trees
- More than 2 heirs at once
- Heir-specific skill trees or stat management
- Marriage or partnership mechanics

## Evidence
ND panel: 'A child in House Valdris is not cute legacy content. They are evidence that someone still thinks tomorrow exists. A moral test for the crew. A liability during raids. A witness to what the player chooses to become.' GD panel: full dynasty mechanics cut. ND: ward/foundling model fits; birth mechanics do not.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** Ward visible in house panel with stage and origin story; at least 1 authored arrival scene; stage transitions acknowledged in UI and dialogue; adulthood scene formalizes ward as crew
2. **System / technical:** heirSchema and houseHeirs on houseStateSchema (max 2, default []); initial-game-state.json updated; formalizeHeir command produces NpcRuntimeState and removes from houseHeirs; resolveRaid acknowledges heir presence
3. **Tests / verification:** houseHeirs rejects more than 2 entries; formalizeHeir produces correct NPC and clears heir; test count does not drop below 403

## Dependencies
destiny-xrv7 (raid system — heirs are raid liabilities)
destiny-4wlm (room functions — quarters/barracks capacity includes ward beds)

## Finding coverage
House progression panel — ward/heir model, dynasty verdict

## Fiction contract
The ward is not a mechanic. They are a story about protection, inheritance, contamination, and whether a ruined house can raise something that isn't ruined with it. If the game cannot give that child consequence, do not include children at all. They arrive through an event, not a form.

title: Refactor combat orchestration into smaller application/domain responsibilities
description: Why: The current combat command file owns encounter setup, action resolution, AI, durability loss, relationship effects, quest and mission aftermath, faction/world-state changes, recruitment offers, and conclusion logic in one place. That shape works for prototyping but is already large enough to hide bugs and blur architecture boundaries. What: split the combat orchestration into smaller modules or services by responsibility after the correctness and integration fixes land, while preserving the same externally visible behavior.
acceptance_criteria: 1. combat setup, resolution math, AI turn choice, aftermath application, and reward/settlement orchestration are split into smaller focused modules. 2. the public entry points remain stable or are intentionally migrated with tests. 3. behavior stays covered by existing and updated tests. 4. architecture docs or module-level comments reflect the new boundaries.
close_reason: Split combat.ts (1273 lines) into combatants.ts (189), combatResolution.ts (336), combatAI.ts (72), combatAftermath.ts (50), combatConsts.ts (4). Main combat.ts now 736 lines with only the 3 public commands. All 1062 tests pass, typecheck clean, public API unchanged.

title: Player Renown system — organisational progression
description: Player character uses Renown (not XP/levels). Renown earned from quest completions, key decisions, milestones. Renown levels 1-10 unlock organisational capacity: roster slots, district influence slots, council seats. Add renown field to playerCharacter in contracts.ts and initial-game-state.json. No NPC level field. Renown shown on main HUD. Level thresholds and unlock table to be designed in implementation.

title: NPC Progression: rarity caps + focused training + skill milestones
description: Keep 0-100 skill range. Add rarity-based caps: common=70, uncommon=80, rare=90, elite=95, legendary=100. Add trainingFocus field on RosterNpc (one active skill). Diminishing returns: 50% gain speed above 60, 25% above 80. Skill milestones at 25/45/55/70/85/100 trigger unlocks (titles, passive bonuses, ability slots). Mission skill gain: skills used in successful missions get +1-3 points. Remove or ignore npcLevel field if it exists.

title: NPC-NPC relationships — Known Associates UI
description: Add NPC-NPC relationship data to npcs.json and relationship state. Show in NpcDetailPanel > Relations tab as collapsible 'Known Associates' section. Each entry: other NPC name, trust/affinity axes, disposition note. Seed: Marion-Orren (trust+55, affinity+40, she knows he's alive but hasn't told player). Marion-Dalen Morke (trust:-80, she suspects betrayal). Relationship data drives dialogue options and quest unlocks.

title: Distrikt-Identität: Watermarks und Farb-Akzente zu schwach
description: Art-Critic: Watermarks waren 7% (jetzt 16%), aber Distrikt-Karten wirken noch wie generische Karten. Distriktkarten brauchen stärkere Charakterzüge: Typografie-Stil, stärkere Farbakzente, Texturelement. Betrifft: App.css.

title: Lineage and household hooks: first pairing, pregnancy state, and inheritance scaffold
description: Lineage and inheritance are MVP must-haves per the GDD — explicitly listed in the MVP must-have systems and Milestone 1 deliverable. This does not mean a full dynasty simulation in MVP; it means the scaffold is in place and the first loop is playable: a player can form a meaningful pairing between two NPCs, that pairing can result in a pregnancy state, and a child NPC can eventually join the roster with inherited potential from both parents. This is the long-term emotional hook of the game — the thing that makes players care about their NPCs across generations. Defer complexity; prove the concept in Milestone 1.
design: Add to NPC data: pairBondId (nullable npcId), pregnancyState (none/expecting/rearing), householdId (nullable). New command: formPairing(npcId1, npcId2) — requires mutual affinity > 60 and trust > 50, sets pairBondId on both. New command: initiatePregnancy(npcId) — sets pregnancyState, starts a countdown (30 days). On countdown complete: generate a child NPC with attributes interpolated from both parents + random variance, add to roster as status=family. New UI surface: Household panel on NPC detail showing pair bond, pregnancy state, children. Attraction/compatibility indicators visible on NPC detail before pairing.
acceptance_criteria: Two NPCs can be paired if relationship thresholds are met. Pairing is visible on both NPC panels. Pregnancy state can be initiated and progresses across days. A child NPC appears in the roster after the countdown. Child has interpolated attributes from parents.
close_reason: householdLore state, OpeningScreen backstory, antagonist-notice event, selector.

title: World beyond the walls: surrounding regions, refugees, traveling specialists, and outside pressure
description: The city exists in a world. What presses in from outside shapes the city's labor pool, faction tensions, item availability, and event types. Currently the game has no outside world at all. The surrounding regions should be defined at lore level first, then connected to mechanics: (1) REFUGEE FLOWS — conflict or disaster outside the city drives desperate people into specific districts. They appear as recruitable NPCs with unusual skill combinations, near-zero starting loyalty, and trauma states. (2) TRAVELING SPECIALISTS — rare experts who pass through the city on a schedule: a foreign weapon-smith available for 3 days, a mercenary surgeon who charges triple but heals critical injuries, a cartographer who sells district intelligence. (3) OUTSIDE FACTIONS — foreign trading companies, distant noble houses, mercenary charters from other cities. They create recruitment competition and can be antagonists or patrons depending on the player's standing. (4) SUPPLY DISRUPTIONS — war or blockade outside affects item availability in specific shops. Creates scarcity events the player must navigate. (5) POLITICAL PRESSURE — a distant power threatens the city, which forces the internal factions to either unify or exploit the crisis. Changes the city's political dial balance.
design: Define 3-5 surrounding regions in docs/narrative.md: a name, their current condition (stable/in conflict/collapsed/foreign-controlled), what they send to the city (refugees/goods/soldiers/agents), and what they want from the city. Each region connects to at least one recurring event type. Traveling specialists use a time-gated availability system: they appear in a district for N days then disappear. Player is alerted by a contact or district visit.
acceptance_criteria: docs/narrative.md contains a 'World Beyond the Walls' section with 3+ surrounding regions. At least 2 event types are triggered by outside-world conditions. Traveling specialist NPCs have time-gated availabilityWindow fields in their data. Refugee NPCs have an origin region and a trauma state flag.
close_reason: 3 beyond-walls missions, district narrativeHook, shown in DistrictMapScreen.

title: Tone pass: replace prototype language with dark medieval fantasy voice
description: The current UI still reads as a systems prototype: 'operatives', 'credits', 'application state', 'seeded encounter', generic button labels. The GDD and art direction specify dark medieval fantasy tone: urban decay, political strain, material scarcity, status struggle. The visual layer currently undermines this. Every screen's copy, labels, status text, and log entries need to speak the world's language. 'Credits' might become 'coin' or 'marks'. 'Operatives' might become 'retainers' or 'crew'. 'Assign title' needs a world-appropriate label. Combat log entries should read like dispatches, not system events. This is not a cosmetic change — tone is what makes the mechanics feel like a world rather than a spreadsheet.
design: Audit every user-facing string: screen headings, button labels, badge text, activity log entry formats, stat labels (are 'might/agility/endurance' appropriate terms for this world?). Produce a voice guide (10-15 example strings showing the before/after). Apply throughout. Keep currency name consistent — pick one term and use it everywhere. NPC status terms (Citizen, Mercenary, Servant) are already good — make the rest match that register.
acceptance_criteria: No screen uses 'operative', 'credits' (if replaced), or other prototype-era language. Activity log entries read as in-world dispatches. A new player reading the UI would believe they are in a dark medieval city, not a management software demo.
close_reason: UI tone pass complete: nav labels, activity log messages, opening screen, dashboard text all use Valdenmoor voice.

title: Relationships affect squad performance: trust, loyalty, fear create synergy or tension
description: The GDD specifies that relationship values influence squad cohesion in combat. Currently NPCs fight as independent stat blocks with no awareness of each other. Relationship axes (affinity, respect, fear, loyalty, trust) are stored but never consulted during combat or assignment. Making relationships matter in combat is the fastest path to NPCs feeling like individuals rather than units. Suggested effects: high trust between two NPCs → +5 accuracy when they are both in the squad (they cover each other). high fear of player → -5 morale but +10 obedience (follows orders more reliably). low affinity between two NPCs → -5 morale for both when deployed together. high loyalty to player → resist fear threshold effects longer.
design: Add a relationship modifier step to combat resolution: before each action, check paired relationship values for all squad members. Implement as a squadCohesionModifier(squad, relationships) function that returns a flat morale/accuracy offset. Keep it simple: check 2-3 relationship pairs, return additive modifiers. Surface the result in the pre-combat squad summary ('Marion and Ida work well together: +5 accuracy').
acceptance_criteria: Two NPCs with high trust show a synergy indicator in mission prep. That synergy produces a visible accuracy bonus in combat log. Two NPCs with low affinity show a tension warning. Combat outcome differs based on relationship composition.
close_reason: Relationship modifiers in combat, post-combat boosts, cohesion selector and UI.

title: Improve combat screen range state visibility
description: Why: The MoR analysis identified that without spatial representation, range transitions feel arbitrary. Players struggle to understand why a weapon is penalized this turn but not last turn. The current CombatScreen shows range as plain text inside a stats grid, which is insufficient visual weight for a mechanic that determines damage on every action. What: make the Close/Distant range state a large, persistent zone indicator that dominates the encounter state panel — not a small text field. Include a short explanation of what the current range means for the active combatant's effective range. Add turn separator markers in the combat log feed so round boundaries are visually distinct. Owner: UI role. Files: src/ui/screens/CombatScreen.tsx, App.css.
close_reason: Range indicator panel, ally range match badges (✓/✗), and round-separator dividers in combat log. All 41 tests pass.
